philosophy: Golgari Midrange seeks to control the early game with efficient disruption and removal while out-grinding opponents through resilient threats and card advantage. It wins by dictating the pace of the match, leveraging graveyard synergy and targeted removal to outvalue slower, combo, or aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocks for fixing) and at least one early interaction (Thoughtseize, removal, or Badgermole Cub). Keep any hand containing Sentinel of the Nameless City if you have 3+ mana sources. Mulligan flood, screw, or hands with only high-cost threats and no early game plan or disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on a key combo piece, early threat, or removal spell. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Badgermole Cub or a 2-drop removal spell. If opening with Sentinel, ensure you have 3 lands and a removal spell or another threat to support it. Sequence lands to ensure color fixing, prioritizing duals/shocks if available.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in Graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) and early removal. vs Control/Combo: Bring in more removal (Dismember, Go for the Throat), card draw (Nighthawk Scavenger, Liliana), and additional discard. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, graveyard hate, and additional hand disruption.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Thoughtseize for high-impact targets (combo pieces, key removal, or win conditions). Use Badgermole Cub's card advantage efficiently by letting it trade with removal or block early threats. Manage graveyard resources carefully; avoid overcommitting if facing graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for their response. Hold back Sentinel if you lack an answer to their removal or if they have sweepers. Prioritize trading efficiently rather than overextending into sweepers or counters.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Toxic Deluge), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents hold up mana for removal vs. counterspells. Track graveyard content if facing graveyard-dependent decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sentinel of the Nameless City clearing the board, resilient mid-range threats, and graveyard-fueled card advantage. Win by out-resourcing through removal and threat cycles.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage secured, removal for their potential answers, and they are at low life or have no sweepers. Commit when you can trade multiple threats for their removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked combo or infinite mana with no interaction, if your hand is completely dead against their strategy, or if you're down to one card against a board with sweepers and no recovery.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to discard counterspells or key pieces. Cast threats in response to their removal or when they have no mana. Utilize flash or instant-speed interaction if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize and Badgermole Cub. Don't let them stabilize; remove their mana rocks or key ramp spells. Use Sentinel to exile their biggest threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Thoughtseize to disrupt early plays, use removal efficiently, and deploy Sentinel to clear the board. Transition to card advantage once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with mid-range threats. Use Thoughtseize to disrupt their hand. Out-grind them with card advantage and removal. Force them to use resources on your threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Thoughtseize for their hand disruption and removal. Use Sentinel to exile their key threats. Control the board with efficient trades and graveyard advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction suite to the metagame. Against control/combo, increase hand disruption and graveyard hate. Against aggro, add early removal and life gain if needed. In the mirror, prioritize targeted removal and card advantage. Always keep a balance between disruption and threats.
notes: Badgermole Cub's card advantage is conditional; don't cast it into a sweeper. Sentinel of the Nameless City is a board wipe in a single card slot but requires three colors; sequence mana carefully. Thoughtseize is your most important spell; save it for game-ending threats or combo pieces. Avoid overcommitting to the graveyard if facing hate.

philosophy: Selesnya Company is a value-driven midrange strategy that overwhelms opponents through relentless token generation, +1/+1 counter synergy, and recursive value engines. It wins by out-grinding opponents in the mid-to-late game, leveraging board presence and card advantage to close out games against both aggressive and control-oriented archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing untapped or fetchable sources), a turn-1 or turn-2 creature/token generator, and at least one +1/+1 counter enabler or protection spell. Mulligan to 5 if you have 0-1 lands, multiple unplayable high-cost spells, or no early threats/answers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap creature or enabler if mana allows. Turn 2: Develop board with a token generator or counter enabler, ensuring mana flexibility for interaction. Prioritize playing lands that enter untapped or can fetch basics to smooth mana development.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: In 2-3 removal spells, 1-2 board wipes, 1-2 protection spells. Out 2-3 high-cost value engines. vs Control/Combo: In 2-3 card draw engines, 1-2 direct damage or artifact destruction, 1-2 protection spells. Out 2-3 early creatures. vs Mirror: In 1-2 targeted removal, 1-2 board wipes, 1-2 counter enablers. Out 2-3 redundant token generators.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until you establish a board. Use the graveyard strategically if recursion is present, but avoid overcommitting if graveyard hate is likely. Keep 2-3 cards in hand for flexibility and avoid emptying your hand prematurely.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or protection to answer opponent interaction. Hold back if you lack answers and the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Prioritize trading tokens for removal to exhaust opponent resources.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal on key engines, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents hold up mana for counterspells or spot removal, as this dictates your commitment level and timing for casting finishers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large counter-distributed creatures, recursive value engines, or enchantment-based board states. Win by overwhelming the board with +1/+1 counters and closing with a decisive combat step or enchantment finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board presence, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can safely cast a finisher without fear of interaction. Also commit when you can trade up on value or force unfavorable blocks.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a locked combo, have no interaction against a control deck's sweepers, or are consistently losing trades due to dead draws. Concede if you have no way to answer their win condition or stabilize the board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats, cast multiple spells in response to counterspells, or rely on uncounterable effects if available. Bait counters with cheap enablers before committing finishers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt mana development. Use removal on key ramp creatures and prioritize board wipes to reset their advantage before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and board wipes. Transition to value engines once stabilized, using +1/+1 counters to outscale their early aggression and close out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats to force interaction, then capitalize on card advantage. Use protection spells to keep key creatures alive against targeted removal and out-value their late-game answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-valuing in the midgame, using targeted removal to eliminate key engines, and leveraging board wipes to reset while retaining card advantage. Prioritize counter distribution and protection to outlast the grind.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift toward matchup-specific adjustments: increase removal and sweepers against aggro, add card draw and protection against control, and tune counter distribution and targeted removal in the mirror. Always keep 1-2 versatile interaction spells and ensure your mana base can handle the new card distribution without flooding.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to token generation without answers, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. Always track opponent mana and hand size. Leverage +1/+1 counters to make creatures resilient to spot removal. Avoid playing all lands at once if you need to hold mana for interaction. Protect your key engines with counters or protection spells to maximize value recursion.

philosophy: Orzhov Greasefang is a control-midrange hybrid that stabilizes early with disruption and efficient removal, then transitions into a growing threat strategy centered on Greasefang, Okiba Boss. It wins by out-scaling opponents through sacrifice synergies, card advantage, and out-resourcing them in the late game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a turn-1 Thoughtseize or Parhelion II, and early game pieces like Greasefang or Monument to Endurance. Mulligan on 2 lands with no plays, or 3+ lands with no early interaction/growth. Prioritize hands that can stabilize turn 1-2 and set up the sacrifice engine by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on a key threat or card draw spell. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Parhelion II to filter/draw, or play a sacrifice outlet/early threat. Turn 3: Drop Greasefang or Monument to Endurance, begin sacrificing creatures (including tokens or sacrificed creatures from previous turns) to grow Greasefang. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit 4 mana by turn 3 or 4 for your win conditions.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Niv to Light): Bring in more removal, counterspells, and early interaction. Against control/midrange (Golgari, Selesnya): Bring in more threats, card advantage, and hate bears. Against mirror: Bring in more Thoughtseize, counters, and sideboard cards that disrupt the opponent's sacrifice outlets or growth.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting growth spells, maintaining hand size via Parhelion II/Thoughtseize, and preserving creatures for sacrifice. Don't overcommit to sacrificing your entire board early; keep a threat on the board to pressure. Manage graveyard carefully if playing against graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Greasefang when you have a clear path to growth and can protect it with removal or counters. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal; use Thoughtseize to dig for answers. Prioritize stabilizing against aggro before going wide with sacrifices.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents play discard or hand disruption, as it can starve your Parhelion II engine. Track their mana development to predict when they can hit key spots.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Greasefang, Okiba Boss (often 5/5+ or massive) supported by Monument to Endurance. Close out games by sacrificing remaining creatures to Greasefang, then attacking with the grown threat. Use Parhelion II to dig for the final answer or threat if needed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can safely grow Greasefang without fear of immediate removal. Also commit when you're ahead on board and can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo or aggro deck with no stabilization tools, if you're completely locked out of mana/land, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board with sweepers and you lack answers. Also concede if you're down to dead hands with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Parhelion II to dig for multiple threats, cast Thoughtseize to remove counterspells, and sequence spells to hit non-protected targets. Consider casting growth spells in response to counterspells if possible, or use mana rocks/acceleration to outpace them.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize and Parhelion II to disrupt their mana development. Use removal to kill their ramp sources, and stabilize quickly to prevent them from out-scaling you.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (Thoughtseize, removal) to stall. Drop Greasefang or Monument to Endurance as soon as possible to turn the board. Use Parhelion II to dig for answers and stabilize. Don't overcommit to sacrifice until you have a clear path to growth.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and discard to disrupt their hand. Use Parhelion II to out-value them in card draw. Grow Greasefang steadily and force them to use removal on it, then follow up with additional threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Thoughtseize to disrupt their discard and draw engines. Protect your Greasefang with removal and counters. Out-value them with Parhelion II and sacrifice outlets. Sideboard for more disruption and hate against their sacrifice synergies.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, add more early interaction and less late-game card draw. Against slower decks, add more threats and card advantage. Always keep a balance between disruption, removal, and your core engine. Sideboard in hate cards against specific strategies (e.g., Rest in Peace vs. graveyard, Dispel vs. counters).
notes: Common mistake: Sacrificing too many creatures too early, leaving you with no board presence. Always keep a threat on the board. Another mistake: Over-relying on Parhelion II when the opponent has hand disruption; have backup plans. Greasefang's ability triggers on sacrifice, so plan your turns to maximize growth while maintaining board control. Monument to Endurance is a great early threat but can be sacrificed to Greasefang for a massive swing; time it carefully.

philosophy: A five-color control/value hybrid that leverages Ancient Cornucopia for mana fixing and spell fueling, Yorion for card advantage and board presence, and Niv-Mizzet Reborn as a late-game storm/counter engine. It wins by out-resourcing opponents with superior card quality and converting high spell density into massive value or direct wins.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetchable basics or Cornucopia), at least one early game piece (Yorion, Cornucopia, or a cheap cantrip/removal), and a win condition or setup tool. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only late-game threats and insufficient mana/spells to cast them. Prioritize hands with Cornucopia + cantrips for consistency.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cantrip or deploy Cornucopia. Turn 2: Develop board with Yorion or cheap creatures/spells, fix mana with Cornucopia fetches, and start casting cheap instants/sorceries to build Niv's storm count. If missing Yorion/Cornucopia, prioritize drawing into them via cantrips. Never overextend into known sweepers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +1 Dispel/Counterspell, +1 targeted removal (e.g., Fatal Push, Go for the Throat), -1 Cornucopia/Yorion if too slow. vs Control: +1 extra value/spell (e.g., Spell Pierce, Thoughtseize), -1 Cornucopia if redundant. vs Combo: +1 hate (e.g., Grafdigger's Cage, Rest in Peace, or specific discard), +1 early interaction. vs Mirror: +1 extra Niv/Yorion, +1 targeted hate, -1 generic cantrips.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard instants and sorceries for Niv-Mizzet Reborn. Use Cornucopia to fix mana and fuel Niv's ability, but avoid sacrificing it prematurely if you need the mana for stabilization. Don't overcommit Yorion if the opponent has sweepers in hand. Manage your graveyard carefully if facing hate cards, though Niv's primary engine is hand-based.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Yorion early to establish board presence and trigger card draw, but hold back Niv until you have a clear path to win or enough instant/sorcery density. Use removal sparingly; prioritize trading Yorion for threats and saving answers for sweepers or combo pieces. Protect Niv with counterspells or discard when possible.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (sweepers), targeted removal on Niv/Yorion, and combo win conditions. Note if the opponent is playing around Niv (counterspells, discard, or graveyard hate). Track their mana base and hand for sweepers or hard counters. Pay attention to when they tap out for value vs. when they hold up interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Niv-Mizzet Reborn copying key instants/sorceries for a storm win or massive value, or Yorion out-valuing through repeated triggers. Ancient Cornucopia can be sacrificed to cast a game-winning spell or fuel Niv's ability. Once Niv is on the board with 3+ instants/sorceries in hand/graveyard, you can often win in one turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ instants/sorceries ready, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can safely cast Niv. Also commit Yorion when you can draw into answers or finishers. If the opponent is tapped out or has no interaction, cast Niv immediately and chain spells to win.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has a hard counter to Niv (e.g., Force of Will, Counterspell) and you have no backup plan, or if you're facing a fast combo/aggro with no stabilization tools. Also concede if you're dead to a specific hate card (e.g., Rest in Peace if relying on graveyard synergy) and cannot pivot to Yorion value.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells to probe for counterspells, cast Niv/Yorion in response to counterspells if possible, or use Cornucopia to cast around them. Sideboard in more uncounterable threats or extra interaction. If heavily countered, pivot to Yorion value and out-grind the opponent.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Yorion, disrupt mana with discard/removal, and use Cornucopia to outpace their mana development. Don't let them stabilize; keep casting spells and trading efficiently. Sideboard in more early interaction if needed.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal/counters, use Yorion to draw into answers, and don't overextend into sweepers. Cornucopia helps fix into removal colors. Prioritize surviving turn 3-4, then close the game with Niv or Yorion value.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with Yorion, use Niv to copy key spells (e.g., draw spells, removal), and apply pressure with cheap threats. Don't let them stabilize; keep casting spells and forcing trades. Sideboard in more uncounterable threats if needed.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage (Yorion), protect Niv, and sideboard in targeted hate. Trade efficiently and out-value in the late game. Control the board with removal and use Cornucopia to fuel Niv faster than your opponent.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control-heavy metas, bring in more uncounterable threats and extra interaction. Against aggro, prioritize early removal and sweepers. In mirrors, bring in card advantage and targeted hate. Generally, reduce Cornucopia if the format is fast, increase it if slow. Always keep a backup win condition if Niv is heavily hated, and consider bringing in additional discard or counterspells based on opponent hand size.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting Yorion into sweepers, failing to hoard instants/sorceries for Niv, and mismanaging Cornucopia's tap ability versus its sacrifice ability. Unique interactions: Cornucopia can be sacrificed to cast Niv's ability or other spells, enabling storm combos. Niv's ability copies spells even if they target it, so protect it but don't fear targeting. Always track opponent's hand for sweepers/counters, and remember that Yorion's triggers can be chained with cheap creatures to draw into game-winning pieces.

philosophy: Izzet Prowess aims to cast cheap instants and sorceries to rapidly grow Prowess creatures into unblockable threats, leveraging tempo and card advantage to close games before slower decks can stabilize. It wins by outpacing midrange/control with early pressure and outvaluing aggro through efficient removal and combat scaling.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (at least one Island and one Mountain), at least one Prowess creature (Cori-Steel Cutter), and 2-3 cheap instants/sorceries (Opt, Shock, Lightning Strike, etc.). Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no spells, no Prowess creatures, or only expensive spells with no early game. Hands with 1 land and 3+ spells are playable if they contain a Prowess creature and a cantrip.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap instant if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Cori-Steel Cutter, then follow up with an instant/sorcery to trigger Prowess and attack for 3+ damage. If drawing lands, play them and hold up removal for potential early threats. Always cast instants/sorceries before creatures to maximize Prowess scaling, and prioritize keeping a creature on the board to maintain pressure.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in 2-4 targeted removal (Shock, Lightning Strike), 1-2 early interaction spells. vs Control/Combo: Bring in 2-3 counterspells or cheap removal, 1-2 additional cantrips, 1-2 finishers. vs Mirror: Bring in 2-3 targeted removal, 1-2 counterspells, 1-2 additional cheap instants. vs Midrange: Bring in 2-3 removal, 1-2 hate cards, 1-2 additional threats. Adjust based on specific meta threats, but always maintain a balance between early interaction and late-game finishers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for Prowess triggers versus holding up removal. Use cantrips to dig for answers or threats without overcommitting. Avoid casting multiple instants/sorceries in a row if you lack a creature to grow, as this wastes value. Manage your graveyard carefully if the format includes graveyard hate, but prioritize keeping spells in hand for instant-speed interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have sufficient instants/sorceries in hand or graveyard to keep growing them, and when the opponent has no sweepers. Hold back if they have potential board wipes or if you're low on resources. Use Prowess creatures to block and grow simultaneously, trading up when possible. Don't overextend into known sweepers; wait for them to be answered or played.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), targeted removal, and ramp/mana rocks. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up counterspells. Track their graveyard if they use discard or sacrifice outlets. Pay attention to when they start tapping for multiple colors, as this often signals a late-game combo or midrange plan.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emberheart Challenger and fully grown Cori-Steel Cutters. Win via combat damage or direct damage from Stormchaser's Talent. Use cantrips to dig for the right finisher and ensure you have enough instants/sorceries to grow them to lethal size.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ instants/sorceries in hand/graveyard, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can attack for lethal or force a trade that leaves you ahead. Commit when you've forced interaction and can safely grow a threat to 5+ toughness. Use Stormchaser's Talent to clear blockers or finish the game directly.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 lands with no threats, the opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're facing a locked-out control/midrange deck with no way to break through. Concede if you've exhausted your resources and the opponent has stabilized with a board of unblockable or highly resilient threats.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, redundant spells to force counterspells. Cast threats in response to their counterspells. Use cantrips to dig for answers. Don't overcommit to one big play. Value Prowess triggers over raw damage, and be willing to trade a creature if it forces a counter and leaves you ahead in cards.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to punish their mana development. Use removal on their mana dorks/rocks. Stabilize with cheap instants and close quickly. Don't let them reach their late-game threats; win before they can cast their big spells. Use Stormchaser's Talent to remove key ramp pieces.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use removal on key early threats. Cast Prowess creatures to block and grow. Don't overextend into combat tricks. Stabilize by turn 3-4. Use cantrips to dig for answers and keep pressure on. Win by outvaluing their early board with efficient removal and growing threats to block their entire lineup.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early to force interaction. Use cantrips to dig for answers. Win with unblockable Prowess threats or direct damage. Don't let them stabilize with sweepers. Force them to use resources on your early threats, then close with a fully grown Emberheart Challenger. Use Stormchaser's Talent to clear blockers or finish the game.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value Prowess triggers. Cast instants/sorceries in response to their creatures. Sideboard in more removal/counterspells. Win by outvaluing spell interactions and closing with a bigger threat. Track their graveyard and hand size, and be willing to trade creatures if it leaves you with more resources. Use Stormchaser's Talent to remove their key threats or finish the game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your spell count based on matchup. Against control/midrange, increase removal and counterspells. Against aggro, add early interaction and hate. In the mirror, prioritize targeted removal and counterspells. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and consider bringing in additional finishers if the game plan shifts to longer games. Maintain a balance between early pressure and late-game resilience.
notes: Common mistake: Overcasting instants/sorceries without threats to grow, or overcommitting to a single Prowess creature. Always keep a Prowess creature on the board to maximize trigger value. Remember that Prowess triggers when you cast the spell, not when it resolves, so timing matters. Use cantrips to dig for both threats and answers. In specific formats, check for format-specific hate cards like graveyard hate or specific removal. Practice counting your instants/sorceries in hand and graveyard to accurately predict Prowess scaling.

philosophy: Azorius Control aims to stabilize early with efficient interaction, then out-value opponents using card advantage engines like Consult the Star Charts and Teferi, Hero of Dominaria. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and combo decks while outmaneuvering aggressive strategies through precise answers and late-game finishers like Kaheera.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks), at least one early interaction (e.g., Dissolve, Path to Exile, or similar), and a Star Charts or Teferi if possible. Mulligan to 4-5 lands if no early plays, or 3+ lands with zero interaction against aggro. Keep 2 lands + 2+ spells if they synergize (e.g., Star Charts + removal).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, potentially cast a cantrip or hold up interaction. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast early removal or counterspell, or start setting up Star Charts. Prioritize untapped lands for shock/fetch sequencing. Against aggro, prioritize cheap interaction; against control/midrange, prioritize card draw/setup.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): +1 Dispel/Spell Pierce, +1 Path to Exile, -2 Star Charts, -1 Teferi. vs Midrange (Golgari/Selesnya/Orzhov): +1 Teferi, +1 Kaheera, +1 Anger of Gods/Supreme Verdict, -2 early cantrips. vs Control/Combo (Niv to Light): +1 Teferi, +1 Star Charts, +1 Negate/Counterspell, -2 removal. Mirror: +1 Teferi, +1 Kaheera, +1 countermagic, -2 draw engines.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until the late game. Use Star Charts to dig for answers or threats. Don't overcommit to the board; keep a removal spell for key threats. Manage graveyard if needed, though it's not a primary focus.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Kaheera or Teferi only when you have a clear path to victory or can protect them. Hold back on casting expensive spells if you lack protection or answers to their response. Use Teferi's -2 to draw into answers/threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), removal (Path, Murder), and combo pieces. Note if they're holding up mana for multiple turns (likely holding a sweeper or counter). Track graveyard hate if playing against Golgari.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Teferi, Hero of Dominaria (draws cards, protects board, wins via loyalty), Kaheera (flying threat that grows), and occasionally a late-game sweeper or direct damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage advantage, can protect your finisher, or when the opponent has no sweepers/removal left. Go all-in with Teferi + Kaheera or multiple threats if you can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1-2 cards with no interaction or finisher against a deck with a proven win condition (e.g., Greasefang, Prowess combo, or midrange with multiple threats). If you're locked out of lands or completely out of answers to their board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig past counters. Cast spells at end step or with flash. Use Teferi's -2 to draw into uncounterable threats or protection. Don't overcommit to one spell.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction is key. Use cheap removal/counters to slow them down. Teferi shuts down their mana development. Kaheera pressures before they can cast big threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap interaction (Dissolve, Path). Use Teferi's -2 to draw into more answers. Kaheera can trade efficiently. Don't overextend; stabilize first.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Star Charts and Teferi. Force them to use multiple spells on one threat. Pressure with Kaheera to make them use removal inefficiently.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Teferi is the best card. Use Kaheera to pressure while holding up interaction. Don't overcommit to the board; win via loyalty and card draw.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve. Against aggro, reduce draw engines and increase cheap interaction. Against control/midrange, increase late-game threats and card advantage. Always keep at least one Teferi and one Kaheera unless the matchup is extremely unfavorable. Adjust land counts if needed for specific mana bases.
notes: Teferi's -2 is your most important tool; use it to dig for answers or threats rather than just drawing cards. Kaheera's +1/+1 counter from combat is a great way to bypass removal. Consult the Star Charts is fragile; protect it or have a backup plan. Avoid casting multiple spells in a row without assessing their interaction. Watch for sweepers in control mirrors.

philosophy: Out-value opponents by leveraging creature deaths for card draw and applying steady pressure with lifelinked threats, while disrupting key pieces early to control the game's pace. Wins against control and combo by grinding them out through card advantage, and against aggro/midrange by stabilizing with removal and out-resourcing.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including a Parhelion II if possible), a Thoughtseize, and a turn-2 play (2-drop creature or mana acceleration). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, multiple Parhelion II with no other ramp, or only removal without threats. Prioritize hands that can play a land, cast Thoughtseize, and develop a board by turn 2-3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on the opponent's key early threat, combo piece, or answer to your plan. Turn 2: Ramp with Parhelion II if available, or play a 2-drop creature. If you have Greasefang and sufficient mana, cast it on turn 3-4 to stabilize. Sequence lands to ensure you hit your mana steps, prioritizing untapped sources when possible to maintain spell speed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Golgari Midrange): Bring in board wipes (Anger of the Gods, Wrath of God), additional removal (Path to Exile, Fatal Push), and lifegain. Against control/combo (Niv to Light): Bring in more hand disruption (Thoughtseize, Surgical Extraction), graveyard hate, and additional threats. Against mirror (Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in extra hand disruption, targeted removal, and graveyard hate to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Manage mana to keep options open for removal and threats. Don't overcommit to the board if you can draw into answers. Use Parhelion II early to accelerate into Greasefang or key midgame threats. Track graveyard resources to avoid falling behind on card advantage and recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or discard to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or counterspells. Greasefang is a primary win condition but vulnerable to board wipes; protect it or have a backup plan like planeswalkers or additional threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger), counterspells, graveyard recursion, and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or hold up mana for protection. Track their graveyard for recursion threats and plan discard/removal accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Greasefang with a board of lifelinked creatures, or a planeswalker (Teferi, Hero of Dominaria) that can lock down the board and draw cards. Win by trading up through combat and card draw, leveraging Greasefang's death triggers to out-resource the opponent.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and Greasefang on board. Also commit when they've used their sweepers or counters, or when you can safely trade creatures to trigger Greasefang's draw engine.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, they have a guaranteed win condition (like a combo or sweepers + big threat), or you're completely out of resources and they're stabilizing. Don't cling to a dead hand when the math is against you.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, discard their counters, or cast spells in response to their counterspells. Greasefang's value can outlast counters, and Thoughtseize can remove key counters before they're needed.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, disrupt their mana sources with Thoughtseize, and use removal to stop their big threats. Force them to answer your board while you stabilize into Greasefang.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal, lifegain, and sweepers. Greasefang can turn the tide by making your creatures unblockable/unkillable in combat and drawing you into answers through creature deaths.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats, use discard to disrupt their answers, and win through card advantage. Greasefang out-grinds control by turning every combat into a card draw engine.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the hand war with Thoughtseize, track graveyard, and be the first to cast Greasefang. Use targeted removal to break their board and force unfavorable trades. Prioritize graveyard hate to disrupt their recursion.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Against control/combo, increase disruption and graveyard hate. Against aggro/midrange, add sweepers and lifegain. In the mirror, prioritize hand disruption and targeted removal. Always maintain a balance between removal and threats, and consider bringing in additional planeswalkers if the format is heavy on sweepers.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Greasefang without backup, ignoring graveyard resources, and mulliganing too aggressively. Parhelion II can be sacrificed for mana if absolutely necessary, but it's usually kept for ramp. Greasefang's draw trigger synergizes with sacrifice outlets and natural combat deaths. Watch for opponent's recursion and plan discard/removal accordingly. Always track their graveyard to avoid falling behind on card advantage.

philosophy: Izzet Phoenix is a discard-and-draw combo deck that uses cantrips and discard spells to dig through its library, filling its graveyard to cast Arclight Phoenix for free. It aims to outpace midrange and control by generating explosive card advantage and deploying a game-winning threat that bypasses traditional board interaction.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), and a cantrip (Treasure Cruise/Opt). Keep hands with 2+ cantrips/discard and 2 lands. Mulligan pure land hands, single-land hands, or hands lacking early interaction/cantrips.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast discard or cantrip. Sequence discard first to see opponent's hand, then cantrip to dig. Turn 2: Cast second cantrip/discard, play second land. Aim for 3+ cards in graveyard by turn 3-4. Cast Treasure Cruise as early as possible (turn 2 or 3) to dig 4 cards and rapidly fill the graveyard.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Prowess, Company): Bring in Lightning Strike, Abrade, Red Elemental Blast, Surgical Extraction. Against control/midrange (Golgari, Orzhov, Niv to Light): Bring in Abrade, Lightning Strike, Surgical Extraction, Grafdigger's Cage or Rest in Peace. Against mirror: Bring in Surgical Extraction, Lightning Strike, Red Elemental Blast, and additional discard hate.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize graveyard filling over board presence. Manage mana to ensure you can cast Phoenix when the board is clear or protected. Use Treasure Cruise strategically when you need to dig deep, not just for card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing all resources until you have a clear path to cast Phoenix or protect it. Use discard to remove key threats early. If the opponent has a board, discard their removal, then cast Phoenix on the following turn.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for removal (Lightning Strike, Abrade, Fatal Push), board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, Grafdigger's Cage). Track opponent's card count and discard usage to gauge their game plan and graveyard vulnerability.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Arclight Phoenix is the primary finisher. Artist's Talent can tutor for Phoenix or other graveyard synergies. Once you have 7+ cards in graveyard, cast Phoenix for free. Protect it with discard, countermagic, or by casting it when the opponent has no removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 7+ cards in graveyard, the opponent has no removal or board wipes, or you have a way to protect Phoenix. Also commit when you cannot stabilize against aggro and need to race.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if facing a graveyard hate lock with no way to break it, if you are down to one card and the opponent has removal or a board wipe ready, or if you are consistently mulliganing into dead hands against a fast deck.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast Phoenix on the stack after they counter your cantrip, or use cheap spells to dig past it. If they have multiple counters, consider conceding if they have a hard lock.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and cantrips. Do not let them stabilize. Use Lightning Strike or Abrade to remove ramp creatures or planeswalkers. Cast Phoenix before they can deploy a game-ending threat.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use discard to remove key threats. Use cantrips to dig for answers. Cast Phoenix as soon as possible to race. Do not overcommit to board stalls; you are a combo deck.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with cantrips and discard. Use discard to remove their removal. Cast Phoenix when they have exhausted their interaction. If they have a board wipe, wait until they have used it or dig for a way to protect Phoenix.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It is a graveyard race. Prioritize graveyard filling. Use Surgical Extraction to remove their Phoenix or key cards. Use discard to remove their cantrips or discard. Track graveyard size and win by casting Phoenix first or using Artist's Talent to find it when they do not.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against control and midrange, increase graveyard hate and targeted removal. Against aggro, add cheap removal and discard. In the mirror, prioritize graveyard disruption and card advantage. Always keep at least one copy of your main deck discard and cantrip engine to maintain consistency.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board before casting Phoenix. Always prioritize graveyard count. Artist's Talent is flexible but can be dead against graveyard hate; have a backup plan. Treasure Cruise is a one-time draw engine; use it when you need to dig deep, not just for fun. Watch for opponent discard; they may be trying to empty your graveyard to prevent your win condition.

philosophy: Mono-Red Prowess wins by casting multiple spells per turn to trigger Prowess, rapidly converting cheap threats and burn into lethal damage while outpacing slower, resource-heavy strategies. It thrives against control, combo, and midrange decks that struggle to answer a flurry of damage efficiently.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands and at least two Prowess creatures or a creature plus a burn spell. Prioritize hands with 1-drops and early burn (e.g., Shock, Incinerate, Lightning Strike). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands with no early spells, or hands lacking a turn-1 Prowess threat.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-drop Prowess creature. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a burn spell to trigger Prowess on your creature, then cast a second creature or another burn. If opening with two lands, play both, then cast two spells (creature + burn or two burns). Always sequence burn to trigger Prowess on threats rather than dealing direct damage to the face.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control/combo (Niv to Light, Izzet Prowess): Bring in targeted removal (Lightning Strike, Abrade), hate (Red Elemental Blast), and card draw (Kuldotha Ringleader, Emberwilde Captain). Against midrange/aggro (Golgari Midrange, Selesnya Company, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in sweepers (Pyroclasm), more burn, and efficient spot removal to clear their board before they stabilize.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is for casting, not saving. Prioritize triggering Prowess on threats over dealing damage to the face. Keep 1-2 cards in hand to react to removal or close out games. Avoid overcommitting to the board without a burn spell ready to clear blockers or finish the game.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a burn spell ready to clear blockers or when the opponent has no interaction. Hold back if they have sweepers or spot removal; use burn to trade efficiently. Don't overextend into combat if you can't close the game next turn.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Pyroclasm), spot removal (Path to Exile, Shock), and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for answers. Track their life total and board state to calculate lethal. Watch for graveyard recursion or combo pieces, especially against Niv to Light and Greasefang.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emberheart Challenger combined with burn spells to trigger Prowess and deal 3-4 damage per turn. Monastery Swiftspear with Prowess triggers is the primary win condition. Use burn to clear blockers or close out games at 3-4 life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a lethal combination in hand/graveyard, or when the opponent has no removal/sweepers and you can outpace their life total. Commit when you can trigger Prowess on multiple threats in one turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you have no Prowess triggers, no burn, and the opponent has a board wipe or lock. Concede if you're down to 1 life with no threats and they have an answer to your only spell, or if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells (1-drops, burn) to trigger Prowess before they can counter. Use Red Elemental Blast or Lightning Strike to remove their counterspellers. Sequence spells to trigger Prowess on threats, not just burn.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum pressure early. Don't let them stabilize. Use burn to deal with their ramp creatures or finish them before they cast their big spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use burn to trade efficiently. Don't overextend into combat. Use removal to kill their key threats. Win by out-grinding or closing out before their board stabilizes.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Use Prowess to bypass their spot removal. Force them to use multiple spells to answer one threat. Win by out-casting them or closing out with burn.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by card advantage and better sequencing. Use draw engines to out-value them. Prioritize triggering Prowess on your threats while using burn to clear their blockers. Sideboard in more hate (Red Elemental Blast, Pyroclasm) and bring in more burn to stabilize.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw speed to resilience. Against control/midrange, prioritize targeted removal, hate cards, and card draw to out-value them. Against aggro/mirrors, bring in sweepers and more burn to stabilize and close out. Always keep at least one copy of your key Prowess creatures and maintain a balance between threats and interaction.
notes: Common mistake: casting burn spells for damage instead of triggering Prowess on threats. Always prioritize Prowess triggers over face damage unless you're at lethal. Soul-Scar Mage's triggered ability lets you destroy a creature when you deal damage to it, making it an excellent blocker-killer and finisher when paired with Prowess burn. Emberheart Challenger's token generation synergizes with Prowess, so cast it early to maximize triggers. In the mirror, card advantage wins; don't trade evenly. Against Selesnya Company and Greasefang, respect their board wipes and recursion; use burn to trade efficiently and close out before they stabilize.

philosophy: Rakdos Midrange seeks to disrupt the opponent's early game with targeted discard, establish a resilient board of value-generating threats, and close out games by out-grinding resources and leveraging game-ending finishers. It excels against combo and control decks by denying key pieces and wins midrange mirrors through superior card advantage and life total management.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and 1-2 early spells. Prioritize keeping Thoughtseize paired with a land, or a 1-drop/removal spell with a land. Keep Bloodtithe Harvester only if you have 2 lands in hand. Mulligan pure land wads, 3+ lands with no early spells, or hands lacking discard/removal when facing aggressive or combo strategies.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast Thoughtseize if possible, or drop a 1-drop threat/removal. Turn 2: Develop mana to 2, cast Bloodtithe Harvester if supported, or play a 2-drop threat. Sequence lands to guarantee 2-mana on turn 2 and 3-mana on turn 3. Avoid tapping out for expensive threats before establishing early disruption.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2 Inescapable Blaze, +1 Fatal Push, -1 Sheoldred, -1 Duress. vs Control/Combo: +2 Duress, +1 Sorin the Mirthless, -1 Bloodtithe Harvester, -1 Inescapable Blaze. vs Mirror/Golgari: +2 Bloodtithe Harvester, +1 Sheoldred, -1 Duress, -1 Sorin the Mirthless.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for interaction rather than overcommitting to the board. Use discard to protect your key threats or remove opponent's answers. Manage your graveyard by ensuring Harvester triggers for life draw and preserving Sheoldred's enter-the-battlefield ability for maximum impact. Avoid casting multiple spells in a row if you lack removal for their response.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or discard to clear blockers or answer targeted removal. Hold back if the opponent has potential sweepers or board wipes in hand or the graveyard. Use Bloodtithe Harvester to stabilize against aggressive boards by gaining life and drawing cards, but don't overextend into known sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), targeted removal, and combo win conditions. Note when the opponent discards versus when they hold up mana for counterspells or answers. Track graveyard hate if facing graveyard-dependent strategies, and monitor life total swings to anticipate when they will attempt to close out games.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sheoldred, the Apocalypse serves as the primary finisher, using its enter-the-battlefield sacrifice to destroy a key permanent and then applying massive late-game pressure. Bloodtithe Harvester provides a sustainable engine of life gain and card draw to outlast opponents. Close out games by leveraging card advantage, protecting Sheoldred, and using efficient removal to clear the path.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established card advantage, possess removal for their blockers, and have confirmed they lack sweepers in hand or graveyard. Commit fully when Sheoldred is on the battlefield and you can protect it with discard or removal. Push for the win when your life total is high and you can out-value their remaining resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you are down to a single card, facing a known hard counter like a ready board wipe, and have no way to break through or disrupt their strategy. Concede if you are losing to a combo deck that has already assembled its win condition, or if you are consistently out-grinded in a mirror without the necessary sideboard cards to turn it around.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells from their hand. Cast threats in response to their discard or when they are tapped out. Utilize cheap, efficient threats that are difficult to counter or cast multiple threats in a single turn to force multiple counter spells. Avoid overcommitting to expensive spells when they have untapped mana.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseize their mana dorks, ramp spells, or early threats. Use efficient removal on early threats to slow their acceleration. Bloodtithe Harvester's life gain is crucial to survive their early aggression. Do not overextend; stabilize with removal and grind out the game with superior card advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep Thoughtseize for their key early threats and discard their most dangerous one-drop or two-drop. Use removal efficiently to answer their board without overcommitting. Bloodtithe Harvester stabilizes by gaining life and drawing cards, turning their aggression into a resource deficit. Do not play too many threats at once; prioritize answering their board and surviving to turn 4.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with midrange threats to force interaction. Use discard to find answers or finishers, and deny them card draw or sweepers. Do not let them stabilize with card draw; keep applying pressure and force them to use resources inefficiently. Sheoldred is excellent against control's permanents and can swing the board state dramatically.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by prioritizing card advantage and value threats over raw power. Use Sheoldred to remove their key permanents and disrupt their game plan. Prioritize life total management and card draw to out-grind them. Sideboard in more value threats and discard effects to gain the upper hand in the matchup.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and strategy based on the specific matchup. Against slower decks, bring in more finishers and discard to apply pressure and deny resources. Against faster decks, bring in more removal and life gain to stabilize and survive the early game. Always keep at least one copy of Sheoldred unless facing a specific graveyard hate deck that completely shuts it down. Adjust your mulligan strategy to prioritize survival in aggro matchups and disruption in control matchups.
notes: Common mistake: Overextending into sweepers without checking for board wipes. Always count the opponent's mana and track their discard decisions to anticipate their next play. Bloodtithe Harvester's life gain is crucial against aggressive decks but can be a liability against sacrifice strategies, though it remains strong in this meta. Sheoldred's enter-the-battlefield ability acts as a pseudo-sweeper; use it wisely to remove the most threatening permanent. Don't forget to track graveyard hate if the meta shifts, and always prioritize protecting your key finishers with discard or removal.

philosophy: Hidden Strings is a five-color control-combo deck that uses cheap hand disruption and removal to stabilize the early game, then assembles Lotus Field and Vibrance to generate infinite mana for a decisive spell or combo finish. It excels against midrange and control archetypes by out-grinding them with interaction and combo potential, while relying on efficient early threats and disruption to survive aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably shock/fetch capable), at least one early interaction (Fatal Push, Thoughtseize, or Duress), and a Lotus Field or Vibrance if paired with a land. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early interaction, or 4+ lands with no lands. Keep hands with 1-2 lands if they contain multiple early interaction spells or a clear combo piece with a land.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Duress to disrupt opponent's key pieces (target combo pieces, fast mana, or early threats). If you have Fatal Push and a target, hold it for removal or discard. Turn 2: Develop mana with shock lands/fetches, cast early interaction or a low-cost threat. If you have Lotus Field + a land, play Field, then Vibrance (or vice versa) to untap it, setting up infinite mana. Prioritize untapped lands for shock costs if needed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, fast decks): Bring in additional removal (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike), artifact hate, and discard. Against control/midrange (Golgari, Orzhov, Niv to Light): Bring in more combo pieces, additional interaction (Thoughtseize, Duress), and hate cards for graveyard (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void). Against mirror: Bring in additional disruption, artifact hate, and sideboard combo pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve hand disruption for key turns; don't overcommit to early trades. Manage mana carefully to ensure you can untap Lotus Field with Vibrance. Use fetch lands to dig for combo pieces or interaction. Graveyard is a resource for Fatal Push and discard; protect it from hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction to handle removal or when you're close to comboing off. Hold back if the board is unstable or if you're missing key pieces. Prioritize protecting Lotus Field and Vibrance with counterspells, bounce, or discard.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's discard targets (keep your combo pieces hidden). Track their removal count (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike, etc.). Note if they're playing graveyard hate or artifact hate. Watch for combo indicators (fast mana, specific win cons).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Infinite mana from Lotus Field + Vibrance allows you to cast a high-impact spell (like a massive creature, storm combo, or direct damage) or chain multiple removal spells. Sometimes you can combo off with other cards in the deck.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Lotus Field + Vibrance on the board, enough mana to cast your win con, and no immediate threat to your combo pieces. Also commit if you're ahead on board and can close the game with a single high-impact spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're missing both Lotus Field and Vibrance against a fast deck with no interaction, if your combo pieces are permanently removed (Rest in Peace + artifact hate), or if you're down to one life with no path to combo off and the opponent has a clear win con.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells before they're cast. Chain interaction (Thoughtseize into Fatal Push) to break through. Use mana generation to cast multiple spells or use instant-speed combo pieces.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and removal. Don't let them stabilize; use Fatal Push on key ramp creatures. Sideboard in additional disruption and hate.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early interaction (Fatal Push, discard). Use shock lands to survive early damage. Once stabilized, pivot to combo or grind with interaction. Don't overextend into combat.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with hand disruption and cheap removal. Force them to use resources on your interaction, then combo off when they're tapped out. Use fetch lands to dig for answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war. Prioritize Thoughtseize/Duress to remove their combo pieces. Protect your Lotus Field/Vibrance with counterspells or discard. Sideboard in additional disruption and artifact hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction count based on the metagame. Against control/midrange, increase discard and combo consistency. Against aggro, add more removal and artifact hate. Always keep at least one copy of Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void against graveyard strategies. Adjust your land count if you're consistently flooding or screwing.
notes: Lotus Field enters tapped, so you need a land in play to untap it with Vibrance. Don't play Vibrance without a target land. Fatal Push is versatile but can be a dead draw against non-black creatures; consider holding it for discard if needed. Avoid overcommitting to the board without interaction. Watch for opponent's sideboard hate like Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, or artifact hate. Practice your mana sequencing to ensure you can untap Lotus Field reliably.

philosophy: Golgari Food is a sacrifice-synergy midrange deck that converts creature deaths into card advantage, mana acceleration, and spell replication. It aims to out-value opponents through efficient resource generation while applying consistent pressure, winning against control by out-grinding them, against aggro by stabilizing with discard and efficient trades, and in mirrors by leveraging superior late-game value and hate cards.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals/shocklands or basic swamps/forests), at least one core engine piece (Cauldron Familiar, Badgermole Cub, or Witch's Oven), and early interaction (discard or cheap removal). Keep any hand with 3+ lands and a turn-2 play. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only expensive spells, or hands lacking a sacrifice outlet if you have no early interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a discard or cheap creature (e.g., Graveyard Trespasser, Bloodstoke Mawhorn, or a 1-drop). Turn 2: Cast Cauldron Familiar or Badgermole Cub, sacrifice it immediately to draw a card and generate food. If you have Oven, set it up on turn 2 or 3 with mana available. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast key pieces on curve while maintaining mana for food activation.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): Bring in discard (Thoughtseize, Duress), efficient removal (Fatal Push, Murderous Rider), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Grafdigger's Cage). vs Control (Niv to Light, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in more threats (Tarmogoyf, Bloodstoke Mawhorn), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and card draw (Liliana, Dreadhorde General). vs Company/Midrange: Bring in creature hate (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), additional removal, and late-game finishers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Cauldron Familiar and Badgermole Cub alive until you can sacrifice them for maximum value. Use food tokens for mana acceleration to cast larger threats or Oven copies. Avoid over-sacrificing early; preserve your engine for late-game value. Manage graveyard resources carefully if facing graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction or a board state advantage. Hold back key pieces if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use discard to disrupt their key plays. Trade efficiently with sacrifice outlets to maintain board presence without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Fatal Push), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or planeswalkers. Track their life total and card advantage to know when to pressure versus grind.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Witch's Oven copying a high-impact spell (removal, threat, or draw), Badgermole Cub drawing lands for mana, Cauldron Familiar drawing cards to refill hand, and efficient midrange threats (Tarmogoyf, Bloodstoke Mawhorn, Liliana). Win by out-resourcing or comboing off Oven with cheap spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Oven set up, enough mana for a copy, and the opponent is low on interaction. Commit when you can stabilize the board and have card advantage. Push for the win when you can close the game in 1-2 turns without leaving yourself vulnerable to a sweep.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no way to break through, if you're down to 1-2 cards with no interaction or threats, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., multiple sweepers, life gain lock, or combo finisher). Also concede if you're in a dead matchup post-sideboard with no answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter. Set up Witch's Oven to copy spells after counters are used. Use discard to remove counterspells. Play around counters by having multiple threats or using instant-speed interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use discard to hit their ramp spells. Bring in removal and hate cards. Stabilize with efficient trades and then out-value them midgame.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Mulligan for early interaction and discard. Use sacrifice outlets to trade efficiently. Stabilize with removal and graveyard hate. Transition to midrange once the aggro deck runs out of steam.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats and discard to remove key answers. Use food mana to cast multiple threats or Oven copies. Out-grind them with card advantage and late-game value. Don't overcommit to one threat; apply consistent pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by leveraging better sideboard choices, superior graveyard hate, and more efficient resource management. Track their key pieces and discard them. Use Witch's Oven to copy their threats or removal. Win the attrition war by having more answers and better late-game threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against control, prioritize graveyard hate and card advantage. Against aggro, bring in early interaction and discard. In mirrors, focus on hate cards and efficient removal. Always keep your core engine (Familiar, Cub, Oven) but swap out low-impact cards for matchup-specific answers. Maintain a balance between threat density and interaction.
notes: Common mistake: Over-sacrificing early and running out of fuel. Always keep at least one engine piece alive until you can safely sacrifice it. Another mistake: Setting up Oven without enough mana or interaction to protect it. Remember that food tokens can be sacrificed for mana, but don't forget they can also be used for other effects if your deck includes them. Watch for graveyard hate and have a plan to protect or bypass it. In mirrors, card advantage and hate cards win games, not just threats.

philosophy: Jund Sacrifice aims to generate incremental value by sacrificing creatures for mana and card advantage, then deploying efficient, hard-to-answer threats like Mayhem Devil and Witch's Oven to close out games. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and control decks while stabilizing against aggro through early pressure and discard.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Cauldron Familiar, Witch's Oven, or early threats like Bloodtithe Harvester. Keep discard (Thoughtseize/Liliana) if paired with a land. Mulligan on 1-land hands, triple-colored mana without lands, or hands lacking early plays and no way to generate mana.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Cauldron Familiar or Bloodtithe Harvester if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, sacrifice Familiar for {R}{G}, cast Witch's Oven or another threat. Prioritize playing lands to ensure sacrifice outlets can be activated on curve. Sequence mana carefully to avoid dead turns.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Prowess, Company): +1 Anger of the Gods, +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Liliana, Veilborn, +1 Grafdigger's Cage. Against control/ramp (Niv to Light, Greasefang): +2 Leyline of Punishment, +2 Rest in Peace, +1 Anger of the Gods, +2 Liliana. Against mirror: +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Liliana, +1 Anger of the Gods, -2 basic lands.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat creatures as resources, not just threats. Sacrifice only when it generates immediate value (mana for threats, card draw, or damage). Preserve graveyard resources if possible, but be ready to play around Rest in Peace. Maintain hand size with discard and card draw; never let your hand empty without a clear win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection (discard, removal) or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if they have sweepers or heavy removal. Witch's Oven is a commitment point—only activate it when you can protect it or have a clear path to damage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment), and counters. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or leave mana open for protection. Track graveyard contents to anticipate Rest in Peace or graveyard recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Mayhem Devil (haste, unblockable), Witch's Oven + sacrifice outlet for infinite damage/mana, Liliana's ultimate, or unblockable threats. Secure the win by attacking with haste creatures or using Oven to swing for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to damage, the opponent is tapped out, or you can protect your win condition with discard/removal. Commit when you've established board control and have the resources to follow up.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has multiple sweepers, you're out of resources, or you're facing a locked-in control/combo with no interaction. Also concede if your key win conditions (Oven, Mayhem Devil) are permanently removed and you have no backup plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple threats to force counter trade-offs. Sacrifice outlets for mana to cast around counters. Use discard to remove counterspells or protect your threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to punish their slow start. Use discard to remove mana dorks. Bring in hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment) and sweepers to clear their board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early discard and removal. Use Witch's Oven to generate value and stabilize the board. Once stabilized, close out with threats and sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early to force interaction. Use discard to disrupt their game plan. Sacrifice for mana to cast around counters. Win with unblockable threats or Oven.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Protect your Witch's Oven. Use Rest in Peace to shut down their graveyard. Out-value with better sequencing and card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize hate cards against control/ramp (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment) and sweepers against aggro (Anger of the Gods). Adjust land counts if needed, but generally keep the mana base stable. Focus on maintaining card advantage and protecting your win conditions.
notes: Common mistakes include over-sacrificing early, forgetting to protect Witch's Oven, and mismanaging mana for sacrifice outlets. Unique interactions: Oven + Cauldron Familiar/Bloodtithe Harvester creates infinite sacrifice for mana or damage. Mayhem Devil's haste makes it a surprise attacker. Always track opponent's hate cards and adjust your game plan accordingly.

philosophy: Sultai Scapeshift is a fast combo deck that sacrifices its lands with Scapeshift to generate massive land tokens, untaps a Forest, and casts Bellow of the Woods for lethal damage, supported by Yorion, Sky Nomad for tutoring, protection, and late-game board presence. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and control decks or comboing off before they stabilize, while using early threats and disruption to survive against aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one Forest), Scapeshift, and a land fetcher (Farseek, Kodama's Reach, or a fetch land). Keep Yorion if paired with a land or fetch. Mulligan hands with 4+ lands, no Scapeshift/Yorion, or only one land. Prioritize hands that can play a turn-1 Lumra or turn-2 Scapeshift/Bellow sequence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Lumra if possible, or play a fetch land/shock land. Turn 2: If you have Scapeshift + 2 lands, fetch a Forest, untap it, and cast Bellow for 2 damage. If you have Yorion + land, cast Yorion. If you have Scapeshift + 3 lands, you can combo off for 3+ damage. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can untap a Forest after Scapeshift.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Prowess, Greasefang): Bring in +1/+1 counters, removal (Fatal Push, Drown in Sorrow), and early threats (Thoughtseize, Inquisition). vs Control/Midrange (Golgari, Selesnya, Niv to Light): Bring in more disruption (Thoughtseize, Surgical Extraction), additional finishers (more Yorion, additional Bellow), and hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void if playing it). vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counterspells if available, or additional disruption.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Scapeshift for when you have enough lands to combo off for lethal or near-lethal. Use fetch lands to dig for Forests. Don't overcommit to Yorion if you can combo off next turn. Manage graveyard carefully if facing Rest in Peace or Surgical Extraction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Yorion or Lumra to stabilize against aggro or to pressure control. Hold back Scapeshift until you have a clear path to lethal or to remove a key threat. Use Yorion's ability to tutor for Scapeshift/Bellow when you're close to winning.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for removal targeting Yorion or Lumra. Track opponent's mana to know if they can counter Bellow or kill Yorion. Note if they're playing graveyard hate or discard. Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, etc.) that can reset your board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Scapeshift into Bellow of the Woods for lethal. Yorion, Sky Nomad as a massive attacker or to tutor for the combo. Lumra as a late-game finisher if you have enough mana.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Scapeshift + enough lands to combo for lethal, or when Yorion can attack for lethal or tutor for the win. Commit to Yorion if it can stabilize or pressure to death.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card and the opponent has a board wipe or removal ready. Concede if you're facing a hard counter to your combo (like Rest in Peace + discard) with no way to interact. Fold if you're down to a dead hand against a stabilized opponent.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Yorion as a threat that doesn't need to be countered to win. Split your combo (cast Bellow, then Scapeshift, or vice versa if possible, though Scapeshift is instant). Use fetch lands to dig for answers. Attack with Yorion/Lumra to force a response.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them with Yorion and early threats. Use discard to remove their ramp pieces. Combo off before they can stabilize or cast their win condition.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Lumra and Yorion. Use removal to kill key threats. Don't combo off too early if you can stabilize and win next turn. Use discard to disrupt their early game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Yorion and Lumra. Use discard to remove their counterspells or key spells. Combo off when they have no mana up. Use Yorion to tutor for Scapeshift/Bellow when they're tapped out.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Race to combo off first. Use discard to remove their Scapeshift or Bellow. Prioritize keeping lands and fetchers. Use Yorion to tutor for the combo when they're close.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against control/midrange, bring in more disruption and graveyard hate. Against aggro, bring in removal and early threats. Always keep Scapeshift and at least one Bellow/Yorion. Adjust land count if needed. Use sideboard cards to disrupt opponent's game plan while advancing your own.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Yorion when you can combo off, forgetting to untap a Forest after Scapeshift, and not tracking opponent's removal/counters. Scapeshift is instant, so you can respond to things, but Bellow is a sorcery and can be countered. Yorion's tutor is a sorcery. Plan around graveyard hate and always count your lands before casting Scapeshift to ensure lethal damage.

philosophy: Selesnya Angels is a midrange-control hybrid that leverages lifegain, flying threats, and token generation to stabilize early and out-value opponents in the late game. It wins by trading efficiently, controlling the aerial board, and closing out games with high-impact Angels that pressure through removal and drain opponents through combat.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (ensuring at least one W source) and at least one early game piece: Bishop of Wings (1W), a 2-drop Angel, or a mana dork/land fetch. Keep 3+ lands if you have a 1-drop or 2-drop. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only 3+ mana costs, or hands lacking a way to stabilize (no early flyer, removal, or lifegain).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, pass. Turn 2: If you have 1W, cast Bishop of Wings to start generating 1/1 Spirit tokens and lifegain. If you have 2W or 1GG, play a 2-drop Angel or token enabler. Sequence lands to guarantee W on turn 1, then W/W or W/G on turn 2. Prioritize basic lands or fetch/shock lands that can produce W early to cast Bishop of Wings on curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Niv to Light): Bring in board wipes (Day of Judgment, Wrath of God), spot removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and lifegain/defense (Ajani's Pridemaster, Healing Salve). Against midrange/control (Golgari Midrange, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in additional threats (Resplendent Angel, Righteous Valkyrie), card advantage (Elvish Mystic + Nature's Lore, or format-specific draw), and hate bears (Rest in Peace, if applicable). Against mirror (Selesnya Company): Bring in targeted removal, additional Angels, and cards that punish token strategies.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Reserve mana for answering key threats or casting your own Angels; avoid overextending into sweepers. Use tokens as chump blockers to preserve your Angels. Grind value by trading tokens for opponent's removal or blockers, and keep card advantage in hand to out-resource midrange decks.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Angels when you have board presence, removal to protect them, or when you can safely trade through their answers. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or spot removal ready. Use lifelink to stabilize against aggressive decks, but don't overcommit to trading if you can stabilize with blockers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (wraths), spot removal (Path, Go for the Throat), and finishers (Greasefang, Niv-Mizzet, etc.). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or board wipes. Track graveyard hate if they play it, and monitor when they run out of removal to pressure with Angels.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Righteous Valkyrie and Resplendent Angel are your primary win conditions. Use them to close out games when you have board control or can trade through their removal. Combine with token generation to overwhelm blockers and pressure the opponent's life total through flying and lifelink.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent is low on removal, or you can safely attack with Angels and still have blockers for their remaining threats. Commit when you can trade your tokens for their key pieces or when you can force a favorable combat math scenario.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a combo deck that can win in 1-2 turns, if you're completely locked out of the game (no lands, no answers, opponent has a board wipe + finisher), or if you're down to 1-2 cards against a midrange deck with a clear board advantage and no comeback potential.
