threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats like Bishop of Wings to pressure before they can counter. Cast Angels in response to their spells if possible. Use mana dorks or ramp to cast threats at instant speed. Don't overcommit to expensive spells without backup or protection.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with tokens and lifelink to slow down their ramp. Use removal to kill their mana sources or key threats. Stabilize with board wipes and angelic blockers, then close out games before they can cast their finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize lifegain and early flyers to stabilize. Use removal on their biggest threats. Trade tokens to absorb damage. Don't overextend into combat; play defensively until you can drop a Valkyrie or Resplendent Angel to turn the tide.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with tokens and Angels. Force them to use removal on your threats. Use card advantage to out-grind them. Attack with Angels when they have no answers, and be ready to trade through their sweepers or use lifelink to stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage and efficient removal. Use your Angels to pressure while trading tokens. Sideboard in targeted removal and additional Angels. Win by out-valuing them in the late game and forcing unfavorable trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the meta: prioritize early interaction and lifegain against faster decks, and bring in more threats and card draw against slower decks. Always keep your core Angel package but swap out generic removal for format-specific hate. Maintain a balance between offense and defense, and ensure your sideboard contains answers to the top 3-4 archetypes in your local meta.
notes: Common mistake: Overextending into sweepers without backup. Always keep a land drop and a removal spell in hand. Another: Forgetting to use tokens as chump blockers. Lifelink is your best friend; use it to stabilize and drain opponents. Watch out for graveyard recursion if the format supports it. Ensure your mana base can handle W/G demands; basic lands or fetch/shock combos are key. Practice combat math with flying and lifelink to maximize value in close games.

philosophy: This deck is a land-sacrifice combo that converts mana acceleration into a sudden 7-point burst of damage, then untaps those lands to deploy a massive, game-ending threat. It wins by out-accelerating midrange, out-resourcing control, and racing or out-valuing aggressive decks through sudden board resets and finishers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetchable sources or basics), Scapeshift, and at least one land-fetching spell. Keep hands with 3+ lands if they include Scapeshift and a fetcher. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple non-fetching lands, or hands lacking Scapeshift and fetchers unless they feature a strong early Formidable Speaker play. Prioritize hands that can reliably hit 7 untapped lands by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast Formidable Speaker if paired with a land to stabilize or draw. Turn 2: Ramp with green spells, fetch untapped basics, or play Formidable Speaker to apply pressure. Turn 3: Continue ramping, fetch more basics, and set up for Scapeshift. Land sequencing is critical: prioritize fetching untapped Plains, Forest, Swamp, and Island to ensure you can cast Scapeshift and the 7/7 on the same turn. Avoid playing non-fetching lands that don't tap for the needed colors.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Izzet Prowess, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in targeted removal (Go for the Throat, Path to Exile), counterspells, and card advantage. Against Control (Niv to Light): Bring in additional threats, discard spells, and protection. Against Midrange (Golgari Midrange, Selesnya Company): Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and sideboard Scapeshift variants. Against Mirror: Bring in counterspells, discard, and extra land acceleration.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana until you have 7 untapped lands. Use fetch lands to fix mana and search for basics. Keep Scapeshift in hand until you have the full 7 lands and a target. Manage your graveyard carefully against hate, but don't over-worry unless facing specific graveyard strategies. Formidable Speaker should be played early to stabilize or as a fallback threat if Scapeshift is disrupted.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can protect them or when you're ahead in the game state. Hold back Scapeshift until you have a clear path to 7 lands and a safe turn to cast it. If the board is crowded, use removal or Formidable Speaker to clear space before going for the combo.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard spells, and board wipes. Note when opponents play sweepers or targeted removal aimed at your lands or Scapeshift. Track their graveyard if they play graveyard strategies. Pay attention to mana development; if they're ahead on mana, they may have a win condition ready.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Scapeshift into 7 lands, sacrifice them for 7 damage, then untap and cast Lumra, Bellow of the Woods (or another 7+ power threat). If Scapeshift is countered, use Formidable Speaker + land drops to build a massive board or go wide.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 7 untapped lands, a clear path to cast Scapeshift, and no immediate threats that can stop you. Also commit if you're behind and need the 7 damage to close the game or stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 lands with no fetchers, Scapeshift is in the graveyard or countered, and you have no way to rebuild. Concede against hate decks if you have no answers and your win conditions are completely shut down.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple casting steps (fetch lands, instant-speed ramp), discard counterspells, or use Formidable Speaker to pressure while they hold up mana. Cast Scapeshift in response to their counterspells or use protection spells if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with your own acceleration. Use Formidable Speaker to stabilize and out-value them. If they're ahead, use removal to clear their threats and go for the combo.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play Formidable Speaker early to stabilize and draw cards. Use removal to clear key threats. Hold lands for Scapeshift to potentially win the game or clear the board with the 7 damage.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Formidable Speaker and land drops. Force them to use resources on your threats while you set up Scapeshift. If they try to stall, go for the combo when they have no answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use discard to remove their Scapeshift. Use counterspells to protect yours. Prioritize land acceleration and Formidable Speaker to out-value them. The player who resolves Scapeshift first usually wins.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land count if needed (usually keep 26-27 lands). Bring in hate cards against specific archetypes (e.g., Rest in Peace vs. graveyard, Dispel vs. counters). Adjust your threat curve: more removal against aggro, more card draw against control. Always keep Scapeshift and fetchers in the maindeck or sideboard depending on the matchup. Prioritize keeping your mana base functional by sideboarding in fetchable basics or shock lands if your maindeck is too clunky.
notes: Common mistake: Playing too many non-fetching lands, making it hard to hit 7 basics. Always prioritize fetching untapped basics. Another mistake: Casting Scapeshift too early without a target or protection. Remember that Formidable Speaker can be a great fallback but doesn't replace the combo. Watch out for Niv to Light sweepers and discard. Practice land sequencing to ensure you can cast Scapeshift and the 7/7 on the same turn. In 4c, be mindful of mana screw; consider including additional ramp or land-fetching spells to smooth out your draws.

philosophy: Boros Prowess wins by casting a high volume of cheap, efficient noncreature spells to trigger Prowess, rapidly inflating your attackers to outpace midrange and control strategies. It leverages spell density and tempo to race aggro, out-resolve slower decks, and close games before opponents can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (duals/shocklands preferred, basics acceptable) and at least one Prowess creature (Screaming Nemesis, Emberheart Challenger, or Soul-Scar Mage). Also keep hands with 2+ cheap spells (Shock, Lightning Strike, Path to Exile, Abrade, Lightning Axe) that can pump a creature on turn 1 or 2. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no creatures/spells, 4+ lands, or only creatures with zero spells to pump them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap spell (Shock/Lightning Strike) to trigger Prowess, or cast a creature if you already have a spell in hand. Turn 2: Cast a Prowess creature, then immediately pump it with a second spell to make it a lethal threat. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast two spells by turn 2. If drawing lands, play a land, then a creature, and pump it next turn if you have spells. Never attack with a creature unless you have a spell in hand to pump it further or remove a blocker.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Selesnya Company, Golgari Midrange): Bring in Lightning Axe, Abrade, Lightning Strike, and targeted removal. Take out extra lands or redundant creatures. vs Control (Niv to Light, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in more counterspells/removal, additional cantrips, and Lightning Axe to clear blockers. Take out aggressive creatures if you're running too many. vs Mirror (Izzet Prowess): Bring in targeted removal (Path, Shock), counters if running any, and extra pump spells. Take out redundant creatures and focus on spell outpacing.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is for casting spells, not just attacking. Always prioritize casting noncreature spells to maintain Prowess momentum. Don't overcommit to the board if you're out of spells. Manage hand size by using cantrips efficiently; Prowess decks run thin, so dig early. Graveyard is generally irrelevant, so don't waste turns worrying about it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have at least one spell in hand to pump them, or when you can trade removal efficiently. Hold back if you're out of spells and facing removal-heavy decks. Use removal to clear key blockers or stabilize against aggro. Never overextend into known sweepers or targeted removal you can't answer.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), targeted removal (Path, Shock), and counterspells. Note when opponents save removal for your creatures versus their threats. Track how many noncreature spells they've cast to gauge their Prowess potential in mirror matches. Pay attention to when they tap out for a big threat, as that's your window to attack with a pumped creature.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Pumped creatures (Emberheart Challenger with multiple permanent counters, Screaming Nemesis with 3+ spells cast, Soul-Scar Mage with pump + trample/first strike if applicable) attacking with spells in hand to block or pump further. Use Lightning Axe to finish games or pump a creature for lethal. Soul-Scar Mage's triggered ability should be resolved after pumping to maximize damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a creature with 3+ Prowess counters, at least one spell in hand to pump it further or remove a blocker, and the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal in hand. Also commit when you're ahead in life and can race, or when the opponent is tapped out for a non-interactive play.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, facing a known sweeper or targeted removal you can't answer, and have no creatures or spells to threaten. Also concede in slow matchups if you're out of resources and the opponent is stabilizing with life gain or board wipes.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter (Shock, Lightning Strike). Cast creatures first, then pump with uncounterable spells if needed. Use removal to clear their counterspell casters. Don't overextend into known counterspells; wait for them to use it on a non-critical spell or save your pump spells for combat.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their mana acceleration. Use removal on their ramp sources (manlands, signets, etc.) if possible. Win quickly before they cast their big threats. Don't trade inefficiently; use your spells to clear their board while you build your own.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use cheap removal (Shock, Lightning Strike, Path) to trade efficiently. Don't overcommit creatures early; stabilize with removal, then win with your own Prowess threats. Use Lightning Axe to clear multiple small blockers. Track their life total and adjust your aggression accordingly.
threat_responses.against_control: Outspeed with spell density. Use removal to clear key blockers, then attack with pumped creatures. Bait out removal with cheap spells, then commit your main threat. Use counterspells if running any to protect your pump spells. Don't overcommit to the board; Prowess wins by out-casting, not out-blocking.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by spell outpacing and removal efficiency. Track how many noncreature spells each player has cast. Use targeted removal to kill their pumped creatures before they can pump them further. Save your best pump spells for when you can go wide or finish the game. In prolonged mirrors, prioritize cantrips and Lightning Axe to maintain spell advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control/midrange, increase removal and counterspells, reduce aggressive creatures. Against aggro, add more efficient removal and maybe some life gain or early blockers. In mirrors, prioritize targeted removal and spell outpacing tools. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't break your mana base or spell density, and keep your core Prowess engine intact.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting creatures without spells to pump them, leaving you vulnerable to removal. Another: Forgetting that Prowess triggers on any noncreature spell, including your own removal or pump spells. Always sequence spells to maximize pump before combat. Emberheart Challenger enters with counters, so cast your pump spells before it enters. Soul-Scar Mage's ability triggers on attack, so pump it before declaring attackers. Watch out for 'pump tax' in mirrors where both players are casting spells to grow creatures. Lightning Axe can be used as a pump spell or finisher, but don't waste it on a creature that's already at lethal.

philosophy: Gruul Prowess aims to overwhelm opponents by casting a high volume of cheap, efficient noncreature spells to repeatedly pump its creatures via the Prowess mechanic. It excels at out-valuing control and midrange decks through spell density while racing aggressive strategies with early, escalating threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Prowess creature (Screaming Nemesis, Emberheart Challenger, or Soul-Scar Mage), and 2-3 cheap noncreature spells (Lightning Strike, Shock, Charm, etc.). Keep if you can cast a turn-1 spell or play a turn-1 creature. Mulligan if 0-1 lands, all creatures with no spells, or all spells with no creatures/lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast a cantrip or removal spell to trigger Prowess on a potential turn-2 threat, or play a 1-drop creature if available. Turn 2: Play land, cast another noncreature spell to pump your creature, then attack. Prioritize sequencing spells to maximize Prowess triggers before combat. If opening with a creature, hold a spell for flash or to pump before attacking.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro, bring in Lightning Axe and Kari Zev to race and stabilize; against control/midrange, add Kari Zev, Lightning Axe, and Abzan Charm for value and interaction; against mirrors, prioritize Kari Zev and Lightning Axe to win the spell war. Generally reduce land count by 2-3 to maximize spell density.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana should be kept flexible for flash threats and instant-speed spells. Prioritize keeping cards in hand to fuel Prowess; don't overcommit to board state if you can cast a spell to pump next turn. Graveyard is generally a liability (avoid self-mill), but be aware of opponent's graveyard recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough Prowess triggers to make trades favorable or when you can close the game. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or if you're low on resources. Use Soul-Scar Mage to trade into larger creatures, but don't overextend if you can't follow up.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation, etc.), targeted removal (Path, Go for the Throat), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for counterspells or sweepers. Track if they're playing around flash threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emberheart Challenger and pumped Screaming Nemesis are primary finishers. Kari Zev can close games by drawing cards and pumping. Use flash threats to surprise block or attack through removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ Prowess triggers ready, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can close the game in 1-2 turns. Also commit when you can trade up favorably with Soul-Scar Mage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (Stax, heavy control with no interaction), have no spells to trigger Prowess, or are down to 1 life with no threats and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash threats to attack after they counter your spell. Cast multiple cheap spells to force multiple counters or let Prowess triggers resolve. Use spells with flash or instant speed to pump before combat.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their mana development. Use removal to kill their ramp creatures. Sideboard in hate bears or early threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use efficient removal to kill early threats. Pump your creatures to trade up. Don't overextend; stabilize with removal and then win with Prowess threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with spell density. Use flash threats to bypass removal. Pressure early to force them to use interaction on non-critical spells. Use cantrips to dig for answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the spell war. Prioritize card advantage (Kari Zev, cantrips). Sideboard in more removal and hate bears. Play around their flash threats.

sideboard_advice: After sideboarding, shift from a pure race to a more interactive game plan. Bring in targeted removal and hate bears against midrange/control, and more aggressive threats against slower decks. Adjust land counts to ensure consistent mana for your new spell mix. Prioritize keeping hands with interaction and Prowess creatures in the post-sideboard.
notes: Avoid casting spells unnecessarily just to trigger Prowess if it leaves you vulnerable; timing is key. Remember that Soul-Scar Mage's ability triggers on combat damage, so you can attack with it to remove a large creature's +1/+1 counters or toughness. Don't overcommit to a single threat if you can pump multiple creatures. Watch out for opponent's trick removal like Lightning Axe or Shock in response to your Prowess triggers.

philosophy: A flexible combo/ramp deck that wins by assembling seven lands via Scapeshift to trigger massive landfall effects, primarily using Tron for stack-independent wins, Storm for speed, or hybrid finishes to adapt to the board state.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with Scapeshift plus one to two lands, or two+ lands paired with cantrips/searchers (e.g., Life from the Loam, Farseek, Kodama's Reach, or fetches). Against aggro, keep early interaction or discard. Mulligan hands with only one land, no searchers, or no Scapeshift unless heavily supported by cantrips.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast a cantrip or early threat if available. Turn 2: Search for Scapeshift or additional lands using fetches, ramp spells, or search creatures. Sequence lands to ensure you can reach seven by turn 3-4, prioritizing color fixers and mana sources that support your chosen finisher.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Golgari Midrange/Selesnya Company: Bring in counterspells, discard spells, and graveyard hate. Vs Orzhov Greasefang/Niv to Light: Add counters, discard, and additional Scapeshifts. Vs Izzet Prowess: Prioritize counterspells and discard. Vs mirror: Bring in graveyard hate, counters, and sideboard Scapeshifts.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana to assemble seven lands without overcommitting. Use cantrips to dig for Scapeshift or the final land. Protect your graveyard if running Life from the Loam, and avoid unnecessary land drops that delay your win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to seven lands or can protect your combo. Hold back if the opponent has hard interaction; use cantrips to dig around it rather than overextending with vulnerable permanents.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard spells, and land destruction. Note if the opponent is playing sweepers or targeted removal aimed at your lands or Scapeshift. Track their graveyard if they run Life from the Loam or similar hate.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Scapeshift into seven Forests for Tron (Lumra/Nissa), seven Islands for Storm (Formidable Speaker/Emrakul), or seven Mountains for burn. Tron wins via stack-independent triggers, while Storm wins quickly on the stack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Scapeshift in hand, six lands on the battlefield, and cantrips to find the seventh. Commit when the opponent has no interaction left or when you can safely resolve your finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has multiple hard counters to all your finishers, you have no backup plan, and your hand is dead. Also concede if you're facing a fast aggro deck and cannot stabilize or race in time.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Tron to bypass the stack, or split your combo across multiple targets. Use discard to remove counterspells, or win via instant-speed cantrips and landfall triggers that don't rely on Scapeshift resolving.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with faster finishes (Storm or Tron), use counterspells/discard to disrupt their mana, or win via burn if they overextend. Prioritize speed and interaction.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early interaction, discard, or counterspells to stabilize. If running Mountains, Scapeshift into seven for burn to race. Otherwise, dig for Scapeshift quickly and win via Tron/Storm before they close out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with cantrips and card advantage. Protect Scapeshift with counterspells or discard. Use Tron to win on the ground, bypassing their counterspells entirely.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Grind for Scapeshift and early lands. Use graveyard hate to disrupt their Life from the Loam or landfall triggers. Sideboard in counterspells and discard to disrupt their combo, and prioritize protecting your own finisher.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize interaction (counterspells, discard) against midrange and control archetypes, and add graveyard hate to disrupt recursive strategies. Against aggro, bring in faster interaction or burn finishes. In the mirror, increase counterspells and discard while adding additional Scapeshifts to out-grind. Adjust your land base if needed to support your chosen finisher.
notes: Scapeshift is a sorcery, so it's vulnerable to instant-speed interaction; always have a backup plan or protection. The five-color mana base is complex, so practice land sequencing to avoid mana screw. Tron is your most reliable finisher against control, while Storm offers speed but requires stack protection. Avoid overcommitting to one finisher type; keep your options open based on the board state.

philosophy: Accelerate mana production using land-based synergies to cast game-ending threats or generate overwhelming card advantage before opponents can stabilize. Excels against slower midrange and control decks but struggles against fast aggro and hard-countered strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a playable land plus at least one ramp spell (Farseek/Kodama's Reach) or a turn-2 threat. Keep Leyline if you can play it turn 1 or 2. Mulligan hands with only non-lands, lands without ramp/threats, or redundant lands lacking diversity.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Farseek/Kodama's Reach on it if possible, or play a basic. Turn 2: Ramp into a second land, prioritizing Nykthos or Vibrance. Play Leyline turn 1 or 2 if drawn. Sequence lands to maximize Nykthos triggers by playing multiple lands per turn when possible.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in board wipes, removal, and life gain. vs Control: Bring in uncounterable threats, card draw, and counter hate. vs Combo: Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, additional removal, and graveyard hate. vs Mirror: Bring in extra removal, card advantage, and Leyline hate.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize playing lands over holding mana; Nykthos scales with land count, so every land played generates additional mana. Avoid overcommitting to hand if a land can be played. Manage graveyard if opponent runs strategies that exploit it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have sufficient mana to answer responses or when the board is clear. Hold back if opponent has sweepers or hard removal. Use Ugin or Eldrazi to clear the board when you have excess mana.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), counterspells (Counterspell, Negate), and targeted removal (Path, Swords). Note if opponent is playing discard or graveyard hate. Track whether they are racing or trying to stabilize.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ugin, the Spirit Dragon (board wipe + card draw), Kozilek/Ulamog/Emrakul (game-ending threats), or massive creatures like Gideon. Maximize Nykthos triggers and play all lands to reach 10+ mana.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 10+ mana, opponent has no sweepers, or you can resolve a threat through their removal. Commit if you can win before their next turn or if you have a clear path to game over.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against fast aggro if you haven't found early ramp, if opponent has multiple sweepers and you're out of threats, or if you're dead to their hate (e.g., Leyline of Punishment, Rest in Peace) with no comeback plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Split your ramp and threats into multiple steps, use uncounterable threats (Eldrazi, Ugin), or clear the board with Ugin to draw into game-winners. Avoid casting single massive spells if heavily countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race if possible, but prioritize board wipes and removal. Use Ugin to clear their board and draw cards. Don't overextend into their finishers; stabilize first.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal, board wipes, and life gain. Prioritize survival over ramp. Use Nykthos to find answers quickly and stabilize the board before going off.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with threats and use card advantage to out-value them. Ugin is key for clearing their board and drawing into game-winners. Don't let them stabilize with sweepers; commit when they lack answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through card advantage and better sequencing. Use removal to clear their Leyline or threats. Prioritize resolving your own finishers and track their Leyline and hate cards.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure ramp to a balanced control/ramp hybrid. Bring in targeted hate for specific metagame threats (discard, graveyard, counters), adjust threat density based on matchup speed, and ensure you have enough answers to stabilize against aggro while maintaining sufficient ramp to close out control and midrange games.
notes: Leyline of the Guildpact enables your ramp but makes you vulnerable to Leyline of Punishment or artifact/enchantment hate. Don't overcommit to Nykthos if the opponent has sweepers. Ugin, the Spirit Dragon is often the optimal finisher due to its board wipe and card draw. Watch for opponent discard; keep hands with multiple threats or digging tools. Avoid overpaying mana when Nykthos triggers can cover costs.

philosophy: Mono-Red Aggro aims to close out games in 3–5 turns by deploying cheap, high-impact threats and direct damage while using discard to disrupt the opponent’s hand. It excels against slower, midrange, and control decks by applying relentless pressure before they can stabilize or execute their game plan.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1–2 lands and 2–3 one-drops (Screaming Nemesis, Eidolon of the Great Revel, Lightning Strike, Firebrand, etc.). Prioritize hands that can play a turn-1 one-drop or turn-2 Monastery Swiftspear. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no early threats, or only high-cost spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a one-drop if possible. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a second one-drop or Monastery Swiftspear. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast early threats and have mana available for pump spells or burn. Avoid overcommitting to the board if you suspect a sweeper.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Golgari Midrange/Selesnya Company: +3 discard, +2 direct damage, +1 extra threat. Against Orzhov Greasefang/Niv to Light: +4 Lightning Strike/Flame Slash, +3 discard, +1 Red Elemental Blast. Against Izzet Prowess: +3 discard, +2 direct damage, +1 extra one-drop. Against mirror: +3 discard, +2 direct damage, +1 extra threat.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource for threats and burn, not for storing. Use discard to disrupt opponent’s hand on their turn, preferably targeting key spells or combo pieces. The graveyard is generally irrelevant for this deck, but monitor opponent’s graveyard for recursion or mill strategies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no sweepers or cannot afford to block. Hold back if they have potential board wipes or heavy removal. Use direct damage to clear blockers, finish players, or punish draw-go strategies.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), counters, and discard outlets. Key tells include holding up mana on turn 2–3, drawing extra cards, or playing unusual lands. Track opponent’s life total and hand size to optimize discard and burn timing.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Monastery Swiftspear (pumped via combat tricks or spells), direct damage spells, and coordinated combat with multiple threats. Win by applying early pressure, using discard to clear the path, and burning the opponent down to lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no sweepers, has low life total, or you have enough threats to trade favorably. Commit when you can force unfavorable blocks or when you can finish the game before they can respond.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has stabilized with a sweeper, you have no threats or draw spells, and they have a clear win condition. Concede if you’re at 1 life with no board presence and they have a finisher, or if you’re consistently losing the discard war with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells before they can be cast. Cast threats in response to their mana sources or use multiple threats to force counter-decisions. If possible, play around counters by holding up mana for pump spells or burn.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum pressure early to prevent them from reaching their ramp spells. Use discard to hit their ramp or mana rocks. Don’t let them stabilize; race them or disrupt their early game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: You’re usually ahead in the mirror or against other aggro decks. Use discard to disrupt their early game, burn their threats, and keep applying pressure. Don’t overcommit to blocking if you can win the race.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Use discard to remove key control pieces (sweepers, counters, finishers). Burn through their blockers and don’t let them stabilize. Play around sweepers by holding back a threat or using discard to remove them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Use more discard and direct damage to disrupt their hand. Track opponent’s hand and play accordingly. Prioritize threats that are hard to remove and use burn to finish the game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to the metagame. Against control/combo-heavy fields, increase direct damage and discard to disrupt their game plan. Against aggro/midrange, add more early threats and interaction to stabilize. Always maintain a balance of pressure and disruption, and avoid over-indexing on one strategy. Keep your core game plan intact while adjusting for specific matchups.
notes: Common mistakes include overextending into sweepers, misusing discard on irrelevant cards, and failing to track opponent’s mana for counterspells or sweepers. Unique interactions: Monastery Swiftspear’s double strike makes it a premier finisher; Screaming Nemesis punishes draw-go and control strategies; Eidolon of the Great Revel pressures spell-heavy decks. Always prioritize discarding key spells over threats, and use burn to clear blockers or finish players efficiently.

philosophy: Mono-Black Midrange controls the early game through precise hand disruption and efficient removal, then transitions into a resilient value engine that outlasts opponents by leveraging the graveyard and high-impact threats like Sheoldred. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on specific pieces, while grinding out attrition matches against midrange and aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a Thoughtseize, and a turn-2 threat or removal. Prioritize hands that can play a land, Thoughtseize, and a 2-drop by turn 2. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, multiple Thoughtseizes with no threats, or only high-cost spells without answers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, Thoughtseize if possible (target opponent's key combo piece, early threat, or removal). Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-drop threat or removal. If opponent plays a creature, answer it; if they play a land, hold Thoughtseize for later or use it on a key spell. Land sequencing: Prioritize basic swamps or duals that enter untapped if possible to ensure mana consistency for Thoughtseize and early plays.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): +2 Inquisition of Kozilek, +2 Fatal Push, +2 Duress, -2 Sheoldred, -1 Graveyard Trespasser. vs Control (Niv to Light): +2 Duress, +2 Thoughtseize (if main is light), +2 Graveyard Trespasser, -2 early threats. vs Midrange (Golgari/Orzhov): +2 Graveyard Trespasser, +2 Duress, +1 Inquisition, -2 early removal. vs Combo: +2 Duress, +2 Inquisition, +2 Graveyard Trespasser, -2 Sheoldred, -2 late-game threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Thoughtseize for critical turns (opponent's draw step or when they have a key combo piece). Don't overcommit mana to threats if you lack answers. Use the graveyard strategically: feed it to Trespasser for value, but avoid giving Sheoldred or other recursion targets to opponent graveyard hate unless necessary. Manage hand size: keep 3-4 cards minimum to weather disruption.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for their answers. Hold back if you suspect a sweep or if you need to preserve mana for disruption. Prioritize answering their most dangerous permanent (e.g., a fast combo piece or a key midrange threat) over developing your own board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's discard spells (they'll try to empty your hand). Track their graveyard (Trespasser needs to know what's there). Note if they're playing sweepers (you need to have threats out or answers ready). Watch for mana development (if they're ramping, you need to disrupt early).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sheoldred, the Apocalypse is the primary win condition. Get it on board and protected with removal or discard. Graveyard Trespasser can close games by cycling through their graveyard and dealing damage or drawing cards.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Sheoldred on board, opponent has no sweepers, and you have hand disruption to protect your threats. Also commit when you can lock them out of their combo or control game plan.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can go off before turn 4, if you're out-graveyard-hated with no Trespasser, or if you're in a dead hand against control with no way to break their board. Also concede if you're down to 1 life with no threats and they have a sweeper ready.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells. Cast threats in response to their removal or when they're low on mana. Rely on discard to win before they can cast, or use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early disruption is key. Thoughtseize their mana rocks or ramp spells. Answer their early threats efficiently. Don't let them stabilize; pressure them with threats and discard.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal and discard over late-game threats. Use Thoughtseize to remove their early threats or combo pieces. Hold up removal for their key turns. Graveyard Trespasser can help if they rely on graveyard recursion.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with resilient threats. Use Thoughtseize to remove their counters or sweepers. Don't overextend; play one threat at a time and answer their removal. Sheoldred wins long games.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a game of hand efficiency and graveyard advantage. Prioritize Thoughtseize on their key threats or discard. Use Graveyard Trespasser to disrupt their recursion. Win by having better card selection and managing the graveyard better.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against faster decks, prioritize early disruption and removal. Against slower decks, bring in more value engines and graveyard hate. Always consider the opponent's primary win condition and tailor your disruption accordingly. Keep an eye on their graveyard and hand size, and adjust your threat commitment based on their sweepers and answers.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Thoughtseize on irrelevant cards. Always target their most impactful spell or permanent. Another mistake: Committing to a board without answers, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. Unique interaction: Graveyard Trespasser can be used to cycle your own graveyard to find value, but be careful not to feed Sheoldred or other key threats to opponent graveyard hate. Also, track opponent's discard spells to know when to hold up hand.

philosophy: Grixis Midrange is a disruption-driven value deck that uses Thoughtseize to control the early game, Fatal Push to efficiently clear threats, and Flow State to generate tempo swings and card advantage. It aims to out-resource opponents by trading efficiently, establishing resilient board presence, and closing out games before control or combo strategies can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and Thoughtseize or Fatal Push. Prioritize hands with 2-3 lands, a discard spell, and a one-mana spell. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early interaction, or hands with multiple one-drops and no land. Keep hands that can play a land, Thoughtseize, and a one-drop on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, Thoughtseize opponent (target graveyard, hand disruption, or key early threat). Turn 2: Play second land, cast Fatal Push if opponent has a 1-2 mana threat, or cast Flow State if you need card advantage or tempo. If opponent is slow, hold interaction for turn 3-4. Sequence lands carefully to ensure blue/red mana availability for Flow State and discard.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Prowess/Company): Bring in Lightning Strike, Abrade, or additional discard. Vs Control (Niv to Light): Bring in additional removal, threat cards, or card draw. Vs Greasefang/Golgari: Bring in hate cards against life gain/trample or additional removal. Vs Mirror: Bring in additional Thoughtseize, discard, or sideboard cards that disrupt their game plan.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana: Always maintain flexibility for blue/red spells; don't overcommit to black mana early. Hand: Keep Thoughtseize for critical turns; don't waste it on irrelevant cards. Graveyard: Be mindful of opponent's recursion; Fatal Push and discard are your primary graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for their response. Hold back if you suspect a sweep or if you need to preserve interaction for their combo/finisher. Use Flow State to swing tempo when you're behind or to dig for answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and late-game finishers. Note when they tap out for big spells or hold up blue mana. Track graveyard contents for recursion threats and adjust removal priorities accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Resilient mid-range threats combined with removal to clear the path. Flow State can also close games by generating multiple value engines or finding a killer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, opponent has no sweepers, and you can trade efficiently. Commit when you've established a clear advantage in card advantage or life total.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a combo deck that has assembled its win condition and you have no interaction left. Concede if you're down to a dead hand with no lands or removal, or if the matchup is mathematically lost.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Flow State's multiple targets to bypass counters. Cast threats in response to their counterspells. Use discard to remove their counterspells from hand.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseize to remove ramp spells or key threats. Fatal Push to clear early ramp threats. Hold removal for their finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize Fatal Push and discard to slow them down. Hold removal for their biggest threats. Use Flow State to stabilize and find answers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with mid-range threats. Use Thoughtseize to find their answers or disrupt their game plan. Use Flow State to out-value their card draw.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize Thoughtseize on their hand. Use Fatal Push to clear their early threats. Track their sideboard and adjust accordingly.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure disruption to matchup-specific hate. Against control, bring in more threats and card advantage. Against aggro, bring in more removal and life total preservation. In mirrors, prioritize additional discard and hand disruption. Always keep at least one copy of Thoughtseize and Fatal Push unless the matchup is completely dead.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Thoughtseize on irrelevant cards early. Always ask what they will do next turn before discarding. Flow State is a tempo engine, not just a draw spell; use it to swing the board or dig for specific answers. Be mindful of mana screw; consider mulliganing aggressively if you lack blue/red sources.

philosophy: Esper Midrange wins by disrupting early game plans, efficiently answering threats, and out-valuing opponents through superior card quality and late-game threats. It excels against combo and aggressive decks by stalling and punishing mistakes, while grinding down control and midrange mirrors through card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one early interaction (Thoughtseize or Fatal Push). Prioritize hands that can play Thoughtseize on turn 1 or 2. Mulligan to lands if you have fewer than two, or if your hand consists entirely of 3+ mana spells with no land/draw support.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible (target discard, removal, or early threat). Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Fatal Push on a 2-drop threat, or develop board with a midrange creature. Land sequencing: Prioritize basics that support WUB; if playing fetch/shock lands, fetch for the right color and shock for tempo when needed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2 Fatal Push, +2 discard (Inquisition/Duress), -2 lands, -1 midrange threat. vs Control: +2 card draw, +1 threat, -2 removal. vs Combo: +2 discard, +1 board wipe, -2 removal. vs Mirror: +2 targeted removal, +1 card advantage, -2 lands.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard removal for key threats and use Thoughtseize to disrupt opponent's hand before committing threats. Manage mana to maintain flexibility between spells and threats. Avoid overcommitting to the board without card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no removal or sweepers. Hold back if they have counterspells or sweepers in play or hand. Use removal efficiently on tempo threats that disrupt your game plan.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Doomskar), counterspells, and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out for threats versus holding up removal. Track graveyard usage and discard patterns to predict their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Quantum Riddler combined with other midrange threats and card advantage. Use discard and removal to clear the path, then close the game with resilient threats and card draw.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal or sweepers, or when you have a clear card advantage advantage. Commit when you can safely develop your board without fear of being wiped.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has a hard lock (e.g., mass counterspells, infinite combo, or unbreakable board control) and you have no interaction. Concede if you are down to one card and they control the board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split effects, cast threats at end of turn, and use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells. Overcommit with value threats that can't be countered or that provide immediate impact.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early disruption with Thoughtseize and Fatal Push. Don't let them cast big spells; answer threats efficiently and pressure before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Fatal Push on 2-drops and Thoughtseize on early threats. Stabilize with removal, then close the game with midrange threats. Don't overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with midrange threats and use discard to disrupt their answers. Out-value them with card draw and resilient threats. Force them to use removal on multiple targets.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by tracking their hand and removing key threats. Hold up removal for their threats and sweepers. Win with superior late-game threats and card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust land count based on matchup (more basics vs combo, fewer vs control). Prioritize hate cards against top meta: +2 graveyard hate vs Golgari, +2 enchantment hate vs Selesnya, +2 lifegain/planeswalker hate vs Orzhov, +2 artifact/enchantment removal vs Niv to Light, and +2 cheap removal/discard vs Izzet Prowess. Always track opponent's sideboarded cards and adjust accordingly.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without card advantage or tracking opponent's removal. Quantum Riddler's value scales with discard and removal synergy, so prioritize keeping it in hand. Always track opponent's mana and hand size to predict their next move. Avoid playing around cards that don't exist; focus on the current board state and known information.

philosophy: Mardu Greasefang is a sacrifice-driven midrange engine that generates a swarm of flying Spirit tokens to pressure opponents while using those tokens as fuel for card advantage and value generation. It aims to out-value opponents through efficient disruption, resilient board presence, and a Greasefang finisher, excelling against control and midrange decks that lack immediate removal or sweepers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early game plan (Thoughtseize, Parhelion II, or a sacrifice outlet), and a clear path to developing Greasefang. Keep hands with Thoughtseize + land, or Parhelion II + land. Mulligan to 4+ lands, or hands with only threats and no lands/speed. Prioritize hands that can disrupt the opponent's early game or establish a board presence by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible to remove key answers (targeted removal, sweepers, or combo pieces) or disrupt their game plan. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Parhelion II or a sacrifice outlet if mana allows, or develop a 2-drop threat. If opening with Greasefang, play land, land, Greasefang on turn 3, using turns 1-2 for disruption or setup. Sequence sacrifices carefully to maximize value without overcommitting.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in board wipes, efficient removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Helix), and life gain. vs Control: Bring in additional threats (Greasefang, Inverter of Truth), discard (Thoughtseize, Duress), and hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void if applicable). vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, discard, and fast removal. vs Mirror: Bring in extra value engines, discard, and targeted removal to out-value in the late game.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between developing your board, casting disruption, and fueling sacrifice outlets. Avoid over-sacrificing when you need creatures for blocking or attacking. Use Parhelion II's sacrifice ability for card draw only when you have a clear advantage or need to dig for answers. Track your graveyard and opponent's potential sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Greasefang when you have protection or can trade effectively. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use flying tokens to trade up against larger creatures or to apply pressure while your opponent is stuck on lands. Prioritize trading into blockers rather than attacking into removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (Path, Lightning), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for counterspells or answers. Track graveyard contents if playing against graveyard decks. Pay attention to when they save removal for Greasefang versus your tokens.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Greasefang (often buffed via sacrifice outlets or equipment/aura if in format), or a massive swarm of flying Spirits. Secure the win by trading into blockers, using discard to remove answers, and leveraging card draw to outlast the opponent. Close the game when you have established board control and the opponent has no sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can trade your tokens for their key threats. Commit when you have enough resources to survive their removal and close the game. Push damage with flying tokens while using sacrifice outlets to draw into answers or finishers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be disrupted, if you're swept and have no recovery tools, or if you're dead to the board (e.g., opponent has multiple sweepers and you have no answers). Concede when your hand is empty, your board is gone, and you have no win conditions left.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells or key pieces. Cast threats in response to counterspells if possible, or use cheap/sacrifice outlets to value out counters. Consider sideboard hate like Dispel or Negate if needed. Force them to use multiple spells to deal with your engine.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to beat them out. Use discard to remove ramp spells. Once they stabilize, use sweepers or targeted removal to clear their board and close the game with Greasefang. Don't let them get too far ahead in mana before committing to a board wipe.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and early disruption. Use flying tokens to trade efficiently. Gain life if possible. Don't overextend into combat traps or sweepers. Prioritize surviving the early turns and transition to a value game once the aggro deck runs out of steam.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and discard to remove answers. Use sacrifice outlets to value out their control spells. Close the game quickly before they can stabilize with card draw or sweepers. Force them to use resources on your engine rather than their win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through card advantage and better sequencing. Use Thoughtseize to remove their key pieces. Trade efficiently, prioritize Greasefang protection, and out-value them in the late game. Control the board with flying tokens and force them to use removal on your less valuable threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your game plan to the specific matchup. Against control/combo, increase disruption and hate cards. Against aggro, add removal and sweepers. Maintain a balance between threats and answers. Adjust land counts if facing heavy mana bases. Prioritize keeping your core engine intact while adding matchup-specific tools. Always consider the opponent's likely sideboard cards and adjust your play accordingly.
notes: Common mistakes include over-sacrificing too early without a plan, ignoring the opponent's removal, and failing to track graveyard/sweeper counts. Greasefang is vulnerable to targeted removal, so protect it or have a backup plan. Parhelion II's card draw is powerful but can be a liability if you're low on life or facing sweepers. Always weigh the value of sacrificing tokens against board presence. Practice sequencing sacrifice outlets to maximize value without overcommitting.

philosophy: 4c Legends is a control-tempo strategy that leverages legendary synergy, flexible threat generation, and efficient interaction to outvalue midrange and aggressive decks. It aims to stabilize early, answer key threats, and close games with resilient legendary finishers or copied value engines.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fixing) and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (e.g., a cheap legend, Glasspool Mimic, or spot removal). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands consisting solely of high-cost legends and no answers. Prioritize fixing over raw land count.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-drop legend or cantrip if available. Turn 2: Develop board with a 2-drop legend or Glasspool Mimic, using it to copy a threat or block. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetch/shock lands that can enter untapped or pair with basics for fixing. Play around spot removal until you establish board presence.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2/+1 [Aggro hate/removal], -2 [high-cost finishers]. vs Control: +2/+1 [card draw/finishers], -2 [early threats]. vs Combo: +2/+1 [artifact/enchantment/graveyard hate], -2 [non-essential threats]. vs Mirror: +2/+1 [more removal/legality tricks], -2 [redundant finishers].

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 3. Use cantrips to dig for answers and maintain hand size. Treat the graveyard as a secondary resource for recursion or value, avoiding unnecessary milling. Balance removal, threats, and answers in hand; avoid flooding the board with unprotectable legends.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal in hand or a board advantage. Hold back against sweepers or targeted removal. Use Glasspool Mimic defensively to trade up or offensively to pressure. Prioritize board stability over aggressive plays until you have card advantage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and combo pieces. Note if the opponent is playing around specific legends. Track graveyard activity for recursion threats. Pay attention to mana development to predict their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Tinybones Joins Up as a legendary commander/finisher, or a copied high-impact threat via Glasspool Mimic. Carnage and Crimson Chaos serve as scalable finishers if they can resolve safely.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their answers, and they have no sweepers. Commit when you can protect your finisher or have multiple threats that force unfavorable trades.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one card against a combo deck, if you have no answers to a locked board, or if you are facing a dead hand with no comeback potential. Concede when the opponent has established an insurmountable board state and you lack interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split casting costs, or force counters with cantrips. Protect key spells with counters or protection effects. Prioritize spells that are difficult to counter or have multiple targets.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early interaction, discard, or artifact destruction. Do not let them stabilize; pressure early and disrupt their mana development. Use efficient removal to trade up against their large threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Deploy early removal, board wipes, or efficient blockers. Use Glasspool Mimic to trade up against multiple small threats. Stabilize by turn 3-4 and transition to value generation.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw, resilient threats, and steady pressure. Do not overcommit; wait for them to run out of answers. Use Glasspool Mimic to copy their key threats or protect your own.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing removal and card draw. Use Glasspool Mimic to copy their key threats or protect your own. Win the sideboard game by bringing in more interaction and legality tricks.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift your game plan based on the metagame. Bring in more interaction and early threats against combo and aggro, while increasing card draw and resilient finishers against control. Adjust your land base if you are facing heavy mana disruption or need more fixing. Prioritize sideboard cards that address the top archetypes while maintaining your core strategy.
notes: Watch for legend rule interactions when copying or entering the battlefield. Glasspool Mimic's tap ability is a key resource for value and defense; do not overextend with it. Tinybones Joins Up likely functions as a commander or legend enabler; use its ability to generate value or protect your board. Common mistake: playing too many legends without protection or answers. Always keep a hand of answers to survive early aggression and control lock.

philosophy: Esper Control neutralizes early threats and disrupts opponent game plans to out-value them into a late-game scenario where Kaheera, the Orphanguard becomes a self-sustaining, unblockable win condition. It excels against midrange and combo decks that rely on key pieces or board presence, but requires careful resource management to survive fast aggro and spell-slinging strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocklands or basic WUB), a turn-1 Thoughtseize, and a clear turn-2 play (land + spell, or two lands + spell). Keep Consult the Star Charts only if paired with a land drop and you have a plan to mitigate the discard. Mulligan on 1-land hands, 3+ land hands, or hands with only removal/draw without disruption or a viable early sequence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Thoughtseize on a key non-land card (discard a threat, ramp piece, or combo piece). Turn 2: Play a land, then cast a counterspell, removal, or another Thoughtseize/Consult if possible. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit WUB on turn 3 for Kaheera or key answers. Prioritize maintaining hand size above 3 cards when possible.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo (Izzet Prowess, Niv to Light): +2-3 Dismiss/End, +1-2 Negate/Force, -2 Consult the Star Charts, -1 Thoughtseize. vs Midrange/Control (Golgari, Orzhov, Selesnya): +2-3 Path to Exile, +1-2 Dismiss/End, -2 Consult the Star Charts. vs Mirror: +1-2 Dismiss/End, -1 Thoughtseize, -1 Consult the Star Charts.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until you stabilize. Use Consult the Star Charts when you have excess cards or need to dig for a specific answer/wincon, but avoid it when you're low on life or need to hold up mana for counters. Manage your graveyard by Thoughtseizing key pieces from Golgari or Greasefang, and avoid unnecessary scry/draw if the meta features graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing Kaheera until you have at least one removal spell or counter in hand, or until the board is clear of sweepers. Use counterspells to protect Kaheera on the stack or to stop opponent key spells. Trade efficiently: do not use removal on 1/1s if you can counter a 3-drop, and avoid overcommitting to board stalls that leave you vulnerable to a single sweep.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for discard outlets (Golgari), token generators (Company, Greasefang), and spell chains (Prowess, Niv). Note when opponents play lands out of order, which often signals a specific turn-3/4 play. Track graveyard size for graveyard-based wincons and monitor hand size to anticipate when they are out of answers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kaheera, the Orphanguard is the primary win condition. Once it hits the battlefield, it draws you a card each combat, effectively replacing itself. Combine with lifelink and vigilance to outlast opponents. Consult the Star Charts can be used to dig for Kaheera or additional answers if you are behind.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Kaheera on board, a clear path to combat, and sufficient interaction to handle one or two removal spells. Also commit when you are ahead on life and the opponent is out of answers, or when you can force them to use multiple counterspells on Kaheera's entry.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a fast combo that cannot be answered by your current hand, if you are at 1-2 life with no Kaheera and no way to stabilize, or if the opponent has a board wipe + a wincon you cannot answer. Also concede if you are dead to the matchup post-sideboard (e.g., no counters vs storm, no removal vs tokens).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats if available, or cast spells in response to their counterspells (e.g., Thoughtseize on their counter, or use Consult to dig for a way around). Cast multiple spells in a chain to force multiple counters, and prioritize combat damage and triggered abilities that do not go on the stack.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseize their ramp pieces early. Use counterspells to stop key spells. If they get ahead, use removal on their mana sources or threats. Consult can help dig for answers faster than they can cast, but hold up interaction to stop their finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap counterspells and removal. Thoughtseize their early threats. Use Consult sparingly; hold up mana for interaction. Stabilize with lifelink/vigilance creatures or planeswalkers if available, then transition to Kaheera. Do not overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw. Use Thoughtseize to disrupt their game plan. Cast Kaheera when they run out of counterspells. Use Consult to dig for answers or wincons. Pressure with threats to force them to use resources, and track their hand size to know when they are vulnerable.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the hand war. Thoughtseize their counterspells and draw spells. Use Consult to dig for Kaheera or additional answers. Play around their counterspells by casting spells in response to their interaction. Prioritize keeping hand size and mana open for counters, and be willing to trade resources to gain a slight advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a balanced control shell to a specialized interaction package. Against fast decks, prioritize counterspells and cheap removal, reducing draw/discard engines. Against control/midrange, keep disruption and add targeted removal for key permanents. Always ensure your mana base can support the new card distribution, and adjust your mulligan strategy to account for the new card ratios.
notes: Consult the Star Charts is powerful but can backfire if you discard your own Kaheera or key answers; use it when you have a full hand or can mitigate the discard. Kaheera's lifelink and vigilance make it excellent for trading life, but be wary of board wipes and targeted removal. Thoughtseize is your best early-game tool; do not be afraid to use it on lands if the opponent is mana-screwed, but prioritize non-lands. In the current meta, Golgari and Company are prevalent; ensure you have enough removal for multiple threats and consider graveyard hate if needed. Practice sequencing: Thoughtseize -> land -> spell/land -> counterspell/removal -> Kaheera.

philosophy: Orzhov Midrange controls the early game through targeted disruption and efficient removal, then out-values opponents by leveraging graveyard recursion and incremental board advantage. It wins against combo and control by stabilizing and outlasting them, and against aggro/midrange by trading efficiently and closing out with resilient threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for W/B), a Thoughtseize, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (e.g., Gifted Aetherborn, efficient creature, or Fatal Push). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early interaction, or hands with only removal and no threats/lands. Keep if you can play a land and Thoughtseize on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, Thoughtseize opponent's most disruptive card (prioritize removal, early threats, or combo pieces). Turn 2: Play a second land, then either cast a cheap creature (like Gifted Aetherborn) or use Fatal Push on a problematic early threat. Sequence lands to ensure W/B mana availability; fetch/enter-the-battlefield lands if applicable.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Selesnya Company): Bring in more cheap removal, card advantage, and hate cards. Against control (Niv to Light): Bring in more threats, graveyard hate if needed, and ways to protect threats. Against midrange (Golgari, Orzhov Greasefang): Keep disruption, bring in graveyard recursion enablers, and targeted removal for key permanents. Against mirror: Bring in additional discard, graveyard hate, and sideboard cards that disrupt recursion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping a full hand of interaction until you establish board control. Use Fatal Push efficiently (save for uncounterable or high-value threats). Manage graveyard by balancing recursion with the risk of giving opponents targets; use discard to keep your own graveyard clean if needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for countersweeps or removal. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered interaction or a clear win condition. Trade efficiently: use removal on threats that can't be blocked or that enable combos, and use creatures to trade up.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and graveyard recursion engines. Note when opponents tap out for key spells. Track graveyard contents if they have recursion. Pay attention to mana development—midrange decks often commit to a specific mana base, so timing your attacks around their land drops is crucial.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Efficient midrange creatures, graveyard recursion loops, and incremental value from discard/removal. Close out games by applying pressure with unblockable or resilient threats while holding removal for last-ditch answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers, or you've established a board that forces them to overcommit. Also commit when you can safely cast a finisher without fear of being countered or removed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life with no threats, no interaction, and the opponent has a clear path to victory (e.g., a locked board, infinite combo, or overwhelming board state). Also concede if your hand is completely dead against their specific strategy post-sideboard.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells early. Cast threats at end step or when they have no mana. Use discard to force them to reveal interaction. If possible, use spells that can't be countered or cast multiple threats to force counter-sweeps.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early disruption (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push) to slow their mana development. Use removal on key ramp spells or mana dorks. Apply pressure to force them to interact instead of ramping.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and discard. Don't overcommit to threats early; focus on surviving turns 1-3. Use Gifted Aetherborn to recur threats or gain life if applicable. Transition to midrange threats once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with efficient threats. Use Thoughtseize to remove key answers or win conditions. Leverage graveyard value to out-value their card draw. Force them to use multiple spells to deal with your threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize Thoughtseize on their key threats or recursion enablers. Use Fatal Push on their early threats. Track graveyard contents to disrupt recursion. Win by out-resourcing and making better trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize cheap removal and discard. Against slower decks, bring in more threats and card advantage. Always keep a balance of interaction and threats; avoid over-indexing on one category. Track opponent tendencies and adjust your sideboard strategy accordingly.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting to the board without removal, misusing Fatal Push on low-value targets, neglecting to track the opponent's graveyard, and failing to sideboard effectively. Unique interactions: Gifted Aetherborn can be a powerful finisher if you have a way to sacrifice it or draw cards, but be cautious of graveyard hate. Thoughtseize is your most important card; use it to disrupt the opponent's game plan rather than just grabbing the most powerful card in hand.

philosophy: Azorius Flash is a tempo-control archetype that leverages instant-speed value engines and graveyard recursion to out-resource opponents while maintaining board presence. It wins by trading efficiently, answering threats on the opponent's turn, and overwhelming them with sudden bursts of card advantage and flying threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetch/shock sources for W/U), at least one flash enabler (Consult the Star Charts or Aven Interrupter), and a Drannith Magistrate or early removal. Mulligan pure curve-outs, hands with only flash spells and no lands, or hands with multiple Dranniths but no early interaction or card draw.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, flash in a cheap threat or removal if possible, or hold up mana for interaction. Turn 2: Flash in a second enabler or threat, deploy Drannith Magistrate to enable graveyard recursion. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast flash spells on the opponent's turn. Prioritize keeping mana open for flash triggers rather than committing to a single turn's plan.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Izzet Prowess): +2 Dispel, +1 Negate, +1 Swords to Plowshares, -1 Consult the Star Charts, -1 Aven Interrupter. Against Midrange/Control (Golgari, Orzhov, Niv to Light): +2 Rest in Peace, +1 Engineered Plague or +2 Dispel, +1 Swords to Plowshares, -1 Aven Interrupter, -1 Consult the Star Charts. Against Mirror: +2 Dispel, +1 Negate, +1 Swords to Plowshares, -1 Drannith Magistrate (if redundant), -1 Enablers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is for flash triggers and interaction, not just casting. Keep 1-2 cards in hand to react. Use the graveyard strategically; Drannith enables recursion, but don't overcommit to the graveyard if hate is present. Prioritize card advantage engines when the board is stable.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or flash counterspells to protect them. Hold back enablers if the board is empty and you need to stabilize. Flash in value on the opponent's upkeep or end step to maximize tempo and bypass sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze), board wipes, and counter suites. Note if opponents are holding up mana for flash responses. Track if they're playing around Drannith by keeping creatures untapped or using exile-based removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A board full of flying threats enabled by Drannith, backed by card advantage from Consult the Star Charts and Aven Interrupter. Win by trading efficiently and out-grinding with flash value, often closing out in combat after flashing in blockers or removal on the opponent's turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and they have no graveyard hate or board wipes. Go all-in when you can flash in multiple threats on their turn to bypass sweepers or combat damage, forcing them to answer multiple lines simultaneously.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If they have multiple Rest in Peace/Scavenging Ooze and you're out of card draw, or if you're facing a combo that can win before you stabilize. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no flash enablers and they have a locked board or unstoppable win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash to cast spells on the opponent's turn when they've already spent mana. Use Drannith to cast from the graveyard, bypassing stack-based disruption. Use cheap threats and value engines that don't rely on a single spell, and trade removal efficiently to clear the path for your finishers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with flash threats. Use removal on their mana dorks/accelerants. Don't let them stabilize; flash in value to disrupt their plans and force them to answer multiple threats before they can cast their big spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal and counterspells. Flash in blockers or removal on their combat step. Use Drannith to recur removal. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then close out with flying threats and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage. Flash in threats when they're tapped out. Use Drannith to recur value. Don't overcommit to the stack; trade efficiently and force them to use multiple answers for a single threat.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Prioritize keeping interaction. Use Drannith to recur your enablers. Trade removal efficiently. Flash in threats on their turn to bypass sweepers and force them to answer multiple lines simultaneously.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure card draw to interaction and hate cards. Against midrange/control, prioritize graveyard hate and board wipes. Against aggro, prioritize early removal and counters. In the mirror, prioritize interaction and card advantage. Always track the opponent's graveyard and adjust Drannith usage accordingly, as it becomes a liability if they have consistent exile hate.
notes: Common mistake: Flashing in enablers on your turn when you could save mana for the opponent's turn. Always keep mana open for flash triggers. Drannith Magistrate is your engine; protect it or have a backup. Consult the Star Charts and Aven Interrupter are your card advantage; use them to out-value, not just to cast spells. Watch for exile-based graveyard hate and adjust your recursion strategy accordingly.

philosophy: Mardu Transmogrify is a disruptive midrange deck that uses discard to control the early game, establishes board presence with efficient creatures, and closes out matches by using Transmogrify to transform a modest threat into an insurmountable finisher or combo piece. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on specific game-winning pieces, while struggling against fast aggro and highly resilient midrange strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), and a turn-2 play (land + creature or discard into creature). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple discard spells with no threats, or only high-cost spells. Prioritize hands that can execute a turn-1 discard into a turn-2 creature or land drop.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on opponent's key piece (remove removal, combo pieces, or early threats). Turn 2: Play a land, cast a cheap creature (e.g., Aethergeode Miner, Aetherborn Marauder, or Kari Zev) or another discard if needed. If opponent plays a creature, consider using a spot removal or holding Transmogrify for a better target. Mana base: Ensure early access to W/B/R; fetch lands and shock lands are crucial for consistency.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): +2 Lightning Strike, +2 Fatal Push, +1 Dismember/Go Blank; -2 Transmogrify, -1 Case of the Stashed Skeleton. vs Control/Midrange (Golgari, Orzhov, Niv to Light): +2 Transmogrify, +2 Go Blank/Dismember, +1 Case of the Stashed Skeleton; -2 Lightning Strike, -2 Fatal Push. vs Selesnya Company: +2 Go Blank, +2 Transmogrify, +1 Dismember; -2 Lightning Strike, -1 Fatal Push.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Transmogrify as your primary win condition and versatile answer; only use it on targets that guarantee game wins or remove unblockable threats. Manage discard carefully—don't overcommit early if you have no follow-up. Use graveyard recursion/searchers (like Case of the Stashed Skeleton) to dig for Transmogrify or key threats when hand is empty.
