midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit creatures when you have removal or discard to handle their response. Hold back if opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Prioritize trading efficiently; don't overextend into a potential board wipe or counter magic.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God, etc.), counterspells, and combo pieces. Note when opponent saves removal for your key creatures. Track graveyard contents for recursion threats. Pay attention to mana development—if they're ramping, prioritize early disruption.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Transmogrify into a massive creature (e.g., Aethergeode Miner, Aetherborn Marauder, or a combo piece like Arcbound Ravager if available) or use it to copy a key threat. Alternative finishers include going wide with multiple creatures and using Transmogrify to remove a blocker or protect a swing.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board advantage, opponent has no sweepers, and you can safely cast Transmogrify into a lethal threat. Also commit when you've established discard dominance and opponent is out of answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be disrupted, control that can out-value you with sweepers and counters, or if you're consistently mulliganing to dead hands with no early interaction. Also concede if opponent has established an unblockable win condition you can't answer.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells early. Cast Transmogrify on turn 3 or later when they've likely used counters. Use cheap threats that can trade or force multiple blocks. Consider casting spells at end step or with mana open for protection if needed.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early disruption (Thoughtseize, Inquisition) to remove mana dorks or acceleration. Use spot removal to clear ramp threats. Transmogrify into a fast finisher to race their late-game plan.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike) and discard to remove early threats. Play efficient blockers and use Transmogrify defensively if necessary. Don't overcommit to Transmogrify early; focus on surviving to turn 4-5.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient creatures and discard to remove key answers. Use Transmogrify to bypass sweepers by turning a small creature into a massive threat that can't be blocked or removed efficiently. Dig for value with searchers to out-resource their card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by keeping more interactive hands. Prioritize removing opponent's Transmogrify or key threats. Use Case of the Stashed Skeleton to dig for Transmogrify or value creatures. Trade efficiently and avoid overextending; the player who manages resources and lands the first lethal Transmogrify wins.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against slower decks, increase your Transmogrify count and value creatures. Against faster decks, cut Transmogrify for more spot removal and discard. Always ensure your mana base can support the sideboarded cards, and consider bringing in hate cards against specific combo or ramp strategies.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Transmogrify on non-essential targets. Always ask 'Does this target win the game or remove a critical threat?' Another mistake: Hoarding discard spells too long; use them to disrupt opponent's game plan early. Note that Case of the Stashed Skeleton's search ability can be used to find Transmogrify from library or graveyard, so plan your graveyard strategy accordingly. Transmogrify can target your own creatures, so use it to create value or remove blockers.

philosophy: A fast, mana-accelerated strategy that leverages cheap elementals and Collected Company to find game-winning threats or value engines, aiming to overwhelm midrange and control decks while racing aggressive matchups.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one mana dork (Llanowar Elves/Elvish Mystic), and 2-3 cheap elementals or Collected Company. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, zero acceleration, or only expensive spells. Prioritize hands that can cast Collected Company by Turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play Llanowar Elves or Elvish Mystic. Turn 2: Cast another cheap spell (e.g., Kirtar's Wrath, Risen Reef, or another dork), then play a land. If holding Farseek, use it on Turn 2 to ensure double-forest for Turn 3. Turn 3: Cast Collected Company targeting high-impact elementals or value planeswalkers. If you have a strong early board, consider holding Company for a better window or to stabilize.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): +2 Rancor/+2 Giant Growth, +1 Dispel, -1 Collected Company, -1 Risen Reef. vs Midrange (Golgari/Orzhov): +2 Rancor, +2 Path to Exile, -1 Farseek, -1 Llanowar Elves. vs Control (Niv to Light): +2 Dispel, +2 Risen Reef, -1 Llanowar Elves, -1 Elvish Mystic. vs Mirror/Greasefang: +2 Path to Exile, +1 Dispel, -1 Farseek, -1 Risen Reef.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Collected Company for the best possible target; avoid casting it on Turn 3 if you can wait for a more favorable window. Track graveyard resources if facing recursion decks. Keep 1-2 mana open for instant-speed interaction when possible, but don't let it slow your curve.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit elementals aggressively early to pressure, but hold back if you suspect a board wipe or targeted removal. Use Collected Company to stabilize or close out games rather than overextending into sweepers. Don't cast Company into a known Supremacy Verdict or Anger of the Gods.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal (Path, Dispel), and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for counters or removal to time your Collected Company cast. Track their life total to know when to race versus when to stabilize.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Collected Company into Risen Reef, Karn, Gideon, or multiple elementals. Alternatively, cast a string of cheap elementals to win combat. Planeswalkers like Karn or Gideon can also close out games by generating value or removing key threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Collected Company in hand, sufficient mana, and the opponent has no clear answer to your targets. Also commit when you're ahead on board and can close the game in 1-2 turns, or when you're forced to race against aggro.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-down control deck with no way to break through, or if you're down to one life with no threats, no acceleration, and no Collected Company. Also concede against aggressive decks if you've stabilized but have no clock and they're at 3+ life with no way to race.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast cheap spells first to force counters, then follow up. Use Collected Company to find threats that are hard to counter or have evasion. Split your casts across turns if needed, and be ready to pivot to a planeswalker or finisher if your main targets are countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: They will outpace you. Prioritize racing with cheap elementals and use Collected Company to find a game-ending threat or planeswalker. Don't get bogged down in long games; force the pace and look for early wins.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and cheap elementals. Use Collected Company to find a sweeper or a clock. Sideboard in more removal and less acceleration. Play conservatively early, then close the game quickly once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early with cheap threats. Use Collected Company to find value that outclasses their answers. If they lock down the board, use Company to find a planeswalker or finisher they can't answer. Don't overcommit into sweepers; wait for them to spend mana on counters or removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is key. Track their Collected Company targets and use spot removal efficiently. Prioritize keeping Collected Company for a better target than theirs. Don't overcommit elementals if they have removal ready, and look for opportunities to trade up on planeswalkers or finishers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against control, reduce acceleration and increase spot removal/counters. Against aggro, increase early interaction and clock. Always keep Collected Company as your primary win condition, but be willing to mulligan for hands that can stabilize or close games quickly. Prioritize hands with 2-3 lands and interaction over pure acceleration in sideboard games.
notes: Collected Company is the deck's engine; never cast it on an empty board unless you're desperate. Risen Reef is a finisher, not a turn-2 play. Watch for graveyard hate if facing Niv to Light. The deck is fragile to board wipes, so timing your Collected Company is critical. Avoid overcommitting to the board if you have no way to recover from a sweeper.

philosophy: 4c Midrange leverages the best interaction and versatile threats across all colors to out-value opponents through efficient resource exchange and card advantage. It aims to win against control and combo by stabilizing early and outlasting them, while trading efficiently against midrange and aggro archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including duals/fixing), a Thoughtseize or Fatal Push, and a turn-2/3 threat. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, multiple unplayable removal without threats, or a hand that can't interact with the top meta (e.g., no discard against control/combo, no early removal against Izzet Prowess or Selesnya Company).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if you can identify a key threat or combo piece. Turn 2: Play a fixing land or dual, cast Fatal Push on early aggro threats, or develop a midrange threat like Quantum Riddler. Prioritize fixing early to enable 4c spells without stalling.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Selesnya Company): Bring in cheap removal, artifact/enchantment hate, and life gain. Against control (Niv to Light, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in more threats, card draw, and discard. Against midrange (Golgari): Bring in additional removal, graveyard hate, and sideboard cards that disrupt recursion. Against mirror: Bring in additional discard, counters, and efficient removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for interaction vs. development. Don't overcommit to one color early. Use discard to disrupt opponent's hand while preserving your own threats. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary self-discard unless it fuels a specific win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for their response. Hold back if you suspect a sweep or if you need to preserve interaction for a specific threat. Trade efficiently: use cheap removal on early threats, save versatile removal for key targets.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), combo pieces, and recursion engines. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or hold up mana for counterspells. Track graveyard usage for midrange/recursion decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Quantum Riddler and other midrange threats combined with card advantage. Win by out-resourcing them through discard, removal, and threat generation.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, sufficient card advantage, and the opponent has no sweepers or interaction left. Also commit when you can close the game before they can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no interaction, and the opponent has a sweeper or combo in hand. Also concede against unfavorable matchups where your deck has no win conditions (e.g., no threats, no removal, dead hand).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast threats in response to their counterspells if possible. Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (e.g., instant-speed interaction, or threats that don't rely on a single spell).
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction is key. Use Thoughtseize to remove ramp spells. Use Fatal Push to kill early threats. Don't let them get ahead in mana; pressure them with threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and discard. Don't overextend. Use life gain if available. Transition to midrange threats once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with threats and discard to remove counterspells/sweepers. Use card advantage to out-value them. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through better card advantage and efficient trades. Use discard to disrupt their hand. Prioritize keeping threats on board. Use removal efficiently.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction curve based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, bring in more threats and card draw. Against aggro, bring in more removal and life gain. Always keep a balance of interaction and threats.
notes: Quantum Riddler's draw/discard ability synergizes with discard outlets and card advantage engines. Avoid over-drawing if you have no way to discard. Be mindful of mana base requirements; ensure you have enough fixing to cast 4c spells consistently. Common mistake: overcommitting to interaction and running out of threats.

philosophy: A graveyard-centric combo-control deck that bypasses traditional hand restrictions by casting spells from the graveyard via Indomitable Creativity. It wins by out-valuing opponents through relentless cantrips, discard, and flexible spell casting, excelling against control and midrange decks that expect to interact with your hand.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks), at least one cantrip or discard, and early interaction (Spell Pierce, Thoughtseize, or cheap removal). Prioritize hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple high-cost spells, or Creativity with no support. If you have Creativity, you need at least two cheap spells or cantrips to enable it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip (Opt, Ponder, Brainstorm) or discard to dig for Creativity and interaction. Turn 2: Cast another cantrip, play a land, or cast a cheap spell (Lightning Bolt, Go Blank, Prismari Command) to fill the graveyard. If you draw Creativity early, protect it immediately with counterspells or discard. Sequence mana to ensure you can cast multiple spells per turn once Creativity is active.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Golgari Midrange/Selesnya Company: Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Damnation), early interaction (Lightning Bolt, Go Blank), and additional discard. vs Orzhov Greasefang/Niv to Light/Izzet Prowess: Bring in more counterspells (Spell Pierce, Counterspell), card advantage (Thoughtseize, Duress), and finishers. vs Mirror: Bring in additional discard, counters, and graveyard protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is flexible; always keep enough for multiple spells per turn once Creativity is active. Hand is secondary to graveyard; prioritize filling the graveyard with cheap spells and cantrips. Use discard to disrupt opponent's hand while building your own graveyard. Never overextend mana if you lack Creativity or interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or counterspells to protect them, or when you can close the game. Creativity itself is a board presence; protect it at all costs. Hold back on overcommitting if the opponent has graveyard hate or sweepers in play or in hand.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction), sweepers, and counterspells. Note when opponents start holding up interaction for Creativity. Track their graveyard if they play graveyard-based strategies. Pay attention to mana development to predict when they can cast their key spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A combination of a large creature (e.g., Kethis, the Hidden Hand, or a big spell like Storm the Festival) cast from the graveyard, or a direct damage/combo finisher. The goal is to cast multiple spells in a single turn to overwhelm the board and close the game before the opponent can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Creativity active, a full graveyard, and enough interaction to protect your board or answer their response. If you can cast 3+ spells this turn and close the game, commit. Do not hold back if you have the resources to win this turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard graveyard hate lock with no removal, if you're down to one card and the opponent has a sweeper, or if you're completely mana-flooded with no Creativity or interaction. Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has stabilized with a board presence you cannot answer.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Creativity bypasses hand restrictions, but counters still work. Use cheap spells to trade, cast Creativity in response to their counters, or use discard to remove their counterspells. Prioritize casting spells when they have no mana up. Use Prismari Command to counter or draw into answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them with card draw and discard. Use early interaction to slow their mana development. Once Creativity is active, cast multiple spells to race their big threats. Use discard to remove their ramp spells and counterspells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early discard and cheap removal to stabilize. Use cantrips to dig for Creativity and interaction. If you can't stabilize, use sweepers or go aggressive with cheap threats. Do not overcommit to the graveyard if you're being pressured.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with graveyard advantage. Use discard to remove their key answers. Cast Creativity and overwhelm them with card advantage and flexible spells. Force them to use multiple resources to answer your graveyard threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the graveyard war. Prioritize discard to empty their graveyard, protect your Creativity, and out-value them with better sequencing. Use counterspells to protect your key spells. Track their graveyard to know when to strike.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against graveyard hate, bring in more interaction and alternative win conditions. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and sweepers. Against control, bring in more card advantage and counters. Always keep at least one copy of Creativity and key cantrips. Adjust your sideboard based on the specific meta share of the listed archetypes.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the graveyard without protecting Creativity. Unique interaction: Creativity lets you cast spells from the graveyard, but you still can't cast spells from your hand. Use this to your advantage by keeping your hand empty of spells to avoid accidentally casting them. Also, note that Creativity doesn't bypass counterspells or removal, so sequencing is critical. Prismari Command is a versatile tool for drawing cards, countering, or dealing damage, so use it flexibly based on the board state.

philosophy: Dimir Midrange controls the early game through targeted disruption and card advantage, then transitions into a late-game aerial assault centered on Yorion, Sky Nomad. It wins by out-resourcing opponents with efficient threats, superior hand management, and flying bodies that are difficult to block or remove.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or shocklands, otherwise basics that tap for U/B), plus at least one of Thoughtseize or Stormchaser's Talent. Keep any hand with Yorion if paired with a land and a removal spell or card draw. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only high-cost spells, or hands with multiple Thoughtseizes but no early interaction or land.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible to strip key early threats or combo pieces. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Stormchaser's Talent to draw into a threat or Yorion, or deploy a cheap removal spell. If opening with Yorion, ensure you have two lands and a way to protect it or follow up. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your spells on curve without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Selesnya Company, Izzet Prowess): Bring in +1/+1 counters, board wipes, or targeted removal. Against Control (Niv to Light, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in more card advantage, finishers, or graveyard hate. Against Midrange (Golgari): Keep disruption, bring in additional removal and threat protection. Against Mirror: Bring in hand disruption and targeted removal to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping your hand size above 3 cards; use Stormchaser's Talent and other draw spells to maintain tempo. Don't overextend into known removal; save interaction for key threats. Manage your graveyard carefully if the format has graveyard hate, but generally, Dimir wants to keep its own graveyard clean while disrupting the opponent's.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Yorion when you have sufficient interaction to protect it or when the board is clear enough to establish aerial dominance. Hold back on overcommitting if the opponent has sweepers or hard removal. Use Thoughtseize to strip removal or win conditions before deploying your own threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, hard removal, and combo finishers. Pay attention to when the opponent holds up mana; if they have open mana on your turn, assume they have interaction. Track their graveyard if they play graveyard-centric decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Yorion, Sky Nomad is the primary finisher, often supported by Stormchaser's Talent to draw into additional Yorions or flying threats. Secondary finishers include efficient flying creatures or planeswalkers that can stabilize and close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers in hand, and you can safely deploy Yorion or a board of flying threats. Commit when you can protect your key pieces with interaction or when the opponent is at low enough life that flying damage is lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a combo deck that has assembled its win condition and you have no interaction left, or if you're in a hard matchup with a dead hand and no way to disrupt the opponent's plan. Also concede if you're down to one life and the opponent has a guaranteed sweep or finisher in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter, or cast your threats in response to their counterspells. Use Thoughtseize to strip counterspells early. If they have multiple counters, commit multiple threats or use uncounterable finishers if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize to disrupt their mana development. Use efficient removal to deal with their ramp targets. If they get ahead, stabilize with Yorion and card advantage to out-value their acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and Thoughtseize to disrupt their early game. Use removal to kill key threats. Deploy Yorion to establish flying presence that they struggle to block. Don't overcommit to card advantage if you're losing the board; stabilize first.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their hand and graveyard. Use Thoughtseize to strip their answers. Deploy Yorion and flying threats to force them to use multiple spells to deal with one threat. Out-value them with card advantage and efficient removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by winning Thoughtseize exchanges. Prioritize keeping your hand size and card advantage. Use targeted removal to kill their Yorion or key threats. Play around their sweepers and hold interaction for their finishers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize early interaction and removal. Against slower decks, bring in more card advantage and finishers. Always keep at least one copy of Thoughtseize and Yorion unless facing a specific hate deck. Adjust your land base if needed, and consider bringing in specific hate cards based on the top archetypes.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Stormchaser's Talent without a follow-up, or holding too much interaction and missing early disruption windows. Always track the opponent's mana and hand size. Yorion is vulnerable to sweepers, so don't cast it into known board wipes. In the mirror, winning the Thoughtseize war is paramount. Be mindful of format-specific hate cards and adjust your sideboard accordingly.

philosophy: Sultai Midrange aims to out-value opponents through efficient interaction and card advantage engines, stabilizing the early game before closing out matches with resilient threats or late-game finishers. It excels against combo and control decks by out-resourcing them, while using cheap removal and hand disruption to neutralize aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks), at least one turn-1 interaction (Thoughtseize) or turn-2 removal (Fatal Push), and a threat or engine. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ non-basic lands without fetches, or hands lacking early interaction/threats. Prioritize keeping lands that can untap for casting Fatal Push or Thoughtseize on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if available, or hold for interaction. Turn 2: Deploy Fatal Push against aggro, play a threat (Tarmogoyf/Bloodtithe Harvester), or begin casting Consult the Star Charts. Sequence lands by playing non-basics first, then fetch/shock as needed to maintain hand size above 3. Aim to have a creature with power 4+ by turn 3 to enable Shark Typhoon and discounted Star Charts.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Prowess, Company): +4 Fatal Push/Dismember, +2 artifact hate, +2 pressure threats. Against control (Niv to Light, Greasefang): +3 card advantage (Star Charts/Thoughtseize), +2 threat protection, +2 graveyard hate. Against mirror: +3 Thoughtseize/Liliana, +2 graveyard hate, +2 targeted removal. Always ensure your post-sideboard mana base can support the new card distribution.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize hand size >3; use Consult the Star Charts to dig for answers when resources are low. Manage your graveyard carefully—exploit it for value when safe, but respect graveyard hate. Avoid overcommitting mana to a single threat without protection. Cast Star Charts when you control a 4+ power creature to maximize card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to clear blockers or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if you suspect a sweeper or heavy removal. Trade favorably using cheap removal on key threats rather than overpaying. Use Shark Typhoon to pressure when the board is clear or when you can trade it for a sweeper.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger), counterspells, and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Grafdigger's Cage). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or discard their hand dry. Track when they run out of interaction to apply pressure, and monitor their graveyard for recursion or combo pieces.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Shark Typhoon (attacks for 4+ and draws cards), Consult the Star Charts (digs for win conditions or answers), and resilient threats like Tarmogoyf. Secure the game by transitioning to card advantage when threats are blocked or by attacking with Typhoon when the opponent has no sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you hold card advantage, have interaction to clear blockers, or the opponent is tapped out/low on resources. Commit when you can trade favorably or close the game in 1-2 turns. Use Shark Typhoon to pressure when you know they lack board wipes.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if facing a hard lock (Rest in Peace + Cage), if you're at 1 life with no interaction or threats, or if the opponent has already won via combo. Don't bluff through established board states or overextend into known sweepers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats, discard counterspells with Thoughtseize, or go wide/small. Cast threats at end step or use instant-speed interaction to bypass counters. Force them to use multiple counters on different threats to exhaust their resources.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push) to disrupt mana dorks. Discard ramp spells, stabilize with removal, and avoid overextending into their late-game finishers. Win the early game and transition to value before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Deploy cheap removal and Thoughtseize to disrupt early. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to your midrange game plan. Don't overcommit to trading; focus on survival and value. Use Shark Typhoon to close games quickly once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and use card advantage to out-value. Go over the top with Shark Typhoon and Star Charts. Force them to use interaction on multiple threats and exploit their tapped-out turns to apply pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war by using Thoughtseize to disrupt their engines. Win the card advantage race and sideboard in disruption/graveyard hate. Trade favorably and avoid overextending into their removal. Control the board state and force them to make the first mistake.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction and threat density to the metagame. Against control-heavy fields, increase card advantage and finishers. Against aggro, prioritize cheap removal and hate. In mirrors, bring in disruption and graveyard hate. Always keep 1-2 copies of key interaction and ensure your mana base can support the new card distribution. Adjust your win condition based on what the opponent is trying to do.
notes: Consult the Star Charts is powerful but can strand you if you lack mana or need immediate answers; cast it when you have a clear plan and a 4+ power creature. Shark Typhoon is vulnerable to sweepers but excels at closing games when unanswered. Avoid overextending into sweepers, and always track graveyard hate. Common mistake: overcommitting to a single threat without protection or ignoring hand size maintenance. Practice recognizing when to pivot from midrange to control or aggression based on the opponent's resources.

philosophy: A ramp-combo archetype that accelerates into a massive land base, then casts Scapeshift to convert those lands into a board of basic lands for a decisive finisher. It aims to out-resource midrange and control decks by leveraging landfall synergies, card draw, and resilience, while racing faster aggressive strategies before they stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/mana sources), Spelunking, Lumra, or other ramp spells. Prioritize hands that can reliably hit 3, 4, and 5 mana on curve. Mulligan pure curve decks, hands with only 1 land, or hands featuring Scapeshift without sufficient ramp or card draw to support it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Spelunking or a ramp spell. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Lumra or another acceleration piece. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your spells on curve; use fetches to find duals or shock lands as needed. Turn 3: Play a land, cast a second ramp or draw spell. The goal is to assemble 5-6 mana by turn 3-4 to cast Lumra and dig for Scapeshift.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Golgari Midrange and Orzhov Greasefang: Bring in counterspells, spot removal, and graveyard hate. Against Selesnya Company and Izzet Prowess: Bring in counters, board wipes, and fast interaction. Against Niv to Light and other control/combo: Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, additional counters, and alternative win conditions. Against mirror: Bring in extra counters, discard, and additional Scapeshift targets.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping lands in play and in the graveyard for Scapeshift targets. Use Lumra’s card draw to dig for Scapeshift or finishers rather than overcommitting to creatures. Manage your graveyard carefully if hate is present, and avoid casting Scapeshift if you don’t have enough lands to sacrifice for a meaningful board state.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to going off or can stabilize against aggression. Hold back Scapeshift if the opponent has significant removal, counters, or hate in play. Use counterspells to protect key ramp pieces or the Scapeshift itself, and only attack with creatures when you can safely close the game or force a favorable trade.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment), counterspells, and board wipes. Note if the opponent is playing fast aggro (track life total and board presence) or control (track removal count and available counters). Pay attention to when they tap out, as that’s your window to go off.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Scapeshift into Forest's Caress, Tron, or a massive creature/planeswalker. Sometimes just going wide with basic lands and a finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 7+ lands in play/graveyard, a clear path to cast Scapeshift, and the opponent has no immediate answer. Also commit if you're behind on board and need to go off to win.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a hard lock (Leyline, Rest in Peace + counters), have no comeback in deck, or are down to 1 life against a fast deck with no stabilization.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats to force counters, cast Scapeshift in response to a counter, or use counterspells to protect your own spells. Sequence carefully.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them or disrupt their mana. Use counterspells on key ramp spells. Scapeshift faster.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with counterspells, spot removal, and Lumra's body. Mulligan for early interaction. Don't go off if you'll die before it resolves.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with threats, use counters to protect your combo, and go off when they've blown resources. Scapeshift is hard for control to answer if they lack specific hate.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a resource race. Win the card advantage war, protect your Scapeshift, and use counterspells to protect your own finishers. Track graveyard hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, pivot to a more interactive stance. Increase counterspells and spot removal against midrange/control. Add hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment, Disenchant) against combo/midrange. Against aggro, prioritize fast interaction and life gain. Adjust land count if needed (usually keep 24-25 lands, but can drop to 23-24 if bringing in more spells).
notes: Common mistakes: Going off too early without a finisher, overcommitting to creatures, forgetting that Scapeshift sacrifices lands (so you need enough in play/graveyard), miscounting landfall triggers. Unique interactions: Lumra's landfall can trigger multiple times if you fetch/Scapeshift. Spelunking can find basic lands for Scapeshift targets. Always track opponent's removal count.

philosophy: Rakdos Tree is a disruptive midrange deck that uses Thoughtseize to control the early game, then transitions into a sacrifice and graveyard engine to fuel an escalating, recursive threat. It excels at out-grinding midrange and control decks while using hand disruption to neutralize combo and tempo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Thoughtseize, and at least one of Tree of Perdition or Agatha's Soul Cauldron. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no disruption, or only high-cost threats without mana development. Prioritize hands that can play a land and cast Thoughtseize on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize to strip key disruption or early threats. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, deploy Agatha's Soul Cauldron or a sacrifice outlet, and begin sacrificing low-cost creatures or artifacts to fuel the engine. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit 2-drop and 3-drop mana bases without flooding, prioritizing dual lands or shock lands if available.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro and combo: Bring in additional discard (e.g., Duress, Inquisition), direct damage, and artifact hate. Against control and ramp: Bring in graveyard hate, extra disruption, and faster threats. Against the mirror: Bring in targeted removal, additional hand disruption, and protection spells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between developing your engine, casting disruption, and deploying threats. Prioritize keeping Thoughtseize for critical turns and use the graveyard as a resource rather than a liability. Sacrifice strategically to maximize Cauldron value without overextending into sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Tree of Perdition only when you can protect it or have removal ready for key threats. Hold back on overcommitting if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use discard to delay their game plan while you stabilize and establish board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation), counterspells, and graveyard recursion. Note if the opponent is playing token generators or ramp spells, as these dictate whether you need to race or grind. Track their hand size and discard patterns to anticipate their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Tree of Perdition as a game-winning threat, often fueled by Cauldron-generated value or graveyard recursion. Secondary finishers include efficient removal clearing the board and a resilient midrange threat that can close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established Cauldron value, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can safely cast Tree of Perdition with protection/disruption ready. Also commit when you can out-value them in trades and have the resources to follow up.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that outpaces your disruption, if you're dead-drawn against a control deck with no win conditions, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state with no removal in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to strip counterspells early. Cast threats in response to their spells, use cheap pingers or sacrifices to bypass counters, and rely on uncounterable effects or layered spells if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Thoughtseize to disrupt mana dorks. Use removal on key ramp spells. If they stabilize, transition to a grindier plan with Cauldron value and targeted removal to out-resource them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep Thoughtseize for early threats. Use efficient removal and discard to stall. Once stabilized, deploy Tree of Perdition to out-value their board and close out the game before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Trade resources efficiently. Use Thoughtseize to find their win conditions or removal. Apply incremental pressure with Cauldron value and threats. Outlast them with graveyard recursion and card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize Thoughtseize on their key pieces. Use Cauldron value to out-resource them. Protect Tree of Perdition with targeted removal and discard, and be willing to trade up in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your disruption to the metagame. Against control and combo, increase discard and artifact/graveyard hate. Against aggro, add early removal and life gain or damage prevention. In the mirror, prioritize targeted removal and additional hand disruption. Always ensure your mana base can handle sideboarded cards and adjust your game plan to prioritize the matchups that favor your win conditions.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the Cauldron engine without board presence, failing to use Thoughtseize proactively, and mismanaging the graveyard as a resource. Tree of Perdition likely scales with sacrifices or graveyard interactions, so sequence your plays to maximize its impact. Watch for format-specific hate cards like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void, and adjust your sacrifice strategy accordingly. Practice reading opponent hands to optimize Thoughtseize targets and avoid dead draws.

philosophy: Mono-Blue Rona Combo is a fast, resource-efficient strategy that aims to assemble its signature engine using mana acceleration and card draw to execute a game-winning interaction before opponents can stabilize. It dominates slower midrange and control fields but must navigate early aggression and hard disruption carefully.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and at least one of Mox Amber, Stormchaser's Talent, or Valley Floodcaller. Mulligan hands with two or more lands, or hands lacking acceleration/card draw unless they contain multiple combo pieces. Keep hands with early interaction if facing expected aggro.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Amber if possible, or hold for Stormchaser's Talent if it's a one-mana play. Turn 2: Cast the other piece, begin cycling or drawing into Valley Floodcaller. Prioritize untapped lands that can produce blue mana on demand and avoid tapping Mox Amber unless you can afford a one-turn delay.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Golgari Midrange/Selesnya Company: +1 Dispel, +1 Spell Pierce, -2 Mox Amber. vs Niv to Light/Izzet Prowess: +1 Counterspell, +1 Mana Leak, -2 Stormchaser's Talent. vs Orzhov Greasefang: +1 Force of Will, +1 Negate, -1 Valley Floodcaller. vs Mirror: +1 Force of Will, +1 Negate, -1 Valley Floodcaller.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve blue mana for protection spells until the combo is within two turns of winning. Use card draw efficiently to avoid flooding; discard excess lands or redundant acceleration only when necessary. Track opponent's mana and stack to time your pieces safely.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can protect them or when the board state is clear. Avoid trading into removal unless it enables your combo or stabilizes against aggro. Maintain hand size over board presence to out-value midrange and control.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells on stack (hold up protection), graveyard hate (Golgari), and board wipes. Note when opponents tap out for removal or counters, as this creates windows to cast combo pieces. Track when they use their main phase resources, leaving them vulnerable to disruption.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Valley Floodcaller combined with Stormchaser's Talent's effect creates an unstoppable finish. Secure it with counterspells or discard, then resolve the combo piece to win immediately.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have the full combo in hand, protection for at least one piece, and the opponent has no immediate board wipe or hard counter. Commit when you can resolve your win condition through the stack or on the opponent's turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're on the draw with no lands, facing a lethal aggro board, or if the opponent has established a lock (e.g., graveyard hate + counterspells) with no way to break it. Concede when your win condition is mathematically impossible to assemble before they win.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use layered threats, cast pieces at end step, or rely on uncounterable effects if available. Prioritize drawing through counterspells with card draw and force them to use resources on your early pieces.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to interrupt their mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures and hold up counterspells for their finishers. Disrupt their acceleration to delay their game plan.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Mulligan for early interaction. Use counterspells to protect your combo pieces and stabilize. Trade efficiently to buy time for your win condition, and prioritize survival over combo assembly in the first two turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with card advantage. Force them to use resources on your threats, then assemble the combo when they tap out. Use discard or counters to protect your key pieces and exploit their lack of early interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw and protection. Sideboard in more counterspells and discard to disrupt their assembly. Control the stack and force them to overcommit before you execute.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo consistency to interactive control. Bring in more counterspells and spot removal against midrange, and more protection against control. Adjust land counts if the format has high artifact/ramp density, and consider adding discard to disrupt mirror matches and control decks.
notes: Mox Amber's tap ability can be a double-edged sword; only tap it for mana when you can't afford to wait a turn. Stormchaser's Talent's timing is crucial—cast it to maximize value or protect your combo. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection, as blue's lack of removal makes you vulnerable to sweepers. Practice stack sequencing to ensure your combo resolves through typical disruption.

philosophy: Mono-Red Goblins is an aggressive tribal strategy that aims to flood the board with cheap, efficient creatures and use haste + pump synergies to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. It excels at racing midrange and control decks but struggles against decks with heavy board wipes or efficient spot removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, at least two 1-drops (e.g., Goblin Guide, Goblin Racketeer, or a cheap pump), and a finisher or pump effect. Drop hands with three or more lands, only a single 1-drop, or exclusively high-cost cards. Prioritize hands that can play a 1-drop on turn 1 and a land + threat/pump on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a 1-drop goblin (preferably one with haste or an immediate impact). Turn 2: Play a land, then either a 2-drop goblin or a pump effect like Legion Loyalist. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your pump spells on the same turn as your threats. If you have Goblin Warchief, play it early to buff your board, then follow up with Legion Loyalist or Rundvelt Hordemaster for haste + +1/+1 synergy.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Golgari Midrange, Niv to Light, and Orzhov Greasefang: Bring in 2-3 spot removal cards (e.g., Lightning Strike, Abrade) and 1-2 additional pump effects or finishers. Against Selesnya Company and Izzet Prowess: Bring in 2-3 discard spells (e.g., Thoughtseize, Duress) or fast pressure to disrupt their early game. Against mirror or token-heavy decks: Bring in 2-3 board wipes (if format allows) or hate bears, and prioritize value trades. Always keep your core goblins and pump effects.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your most critical resource; always keep open mana for pump spells or interaction, especially when the opponent taps out. Hand is your primary resource—avoid overcommitting to the board without backup. Graveyard is generally irrelevant unless you're running specific discard or sacrifice synergies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have pump support or when the opponent has no removal. Hold back if they have sweepers or spot removal ready. Use haste to pressure life total, and chump block with expendable goblins to preserve your key pumpers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), spot removal (e.g., Path to Exile, Lightning Strike), and life gain. Note when they tap out for a big spell or when they start drawing into answers. Pay attention to their mana base for potential sweepers or removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Rundvelt Hordemaster and Legion Loyalist with pump effects, or a fully pumped board of goblins. Win by attacking with haste threats or chump blocking to preserve key pumpers. Use pump spells to make threats too big to block.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have multiple pump effects, the opponent has no removal, or you're ahead on life total and can race. Also commit when you can play a pump and a threat on the same turn to swing for lethal or massive damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to one life, opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're facing a deck that outvalues you long-term with no way to close. Also concede if you're consistently drawing lands or dead hands against control.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that can't be countered (if any), or overcommit with multiple threats. Use pump spells to make threats too big to ignore. If possible, use instant-speed interaction to clear the way.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum pressure early. Don't let them stabilize. Use removal on their ramp sources if possible. If they have a big threat, trade your goblins efficiently to survive until you can close.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use spot removal efficiently. Don't overextend. Trade goblins to preserve your pumpers. Focus on stabilizing your life total and then close the game with your own threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure life total. Don't give them time to draw into answers. Use pump spells to break through. If they have a sweeper, wait for the right moment to swing or use chump blocks to preserve your board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange. Use removal efficiently. Win by having better late-game threats or more pump effects. Track their hand for key cards like Legion Loyalist or Rundvelt, and prioritize trading your expendable goblins for their key pieces.

sideboard_advice: After sideboarding, shift from pure aggression to a more calculated tempo game. Against control/midrange, prioritize preserving your pump effects and using spot removal efficiently to clear paths for your goblins. Against aggro, focus on stabilizing your life total with early pressure and efficient trades. Always keep your core goblins and pump effects, and adjust your mulligan strategy to favor hands with interaction or additional pressure depending on the matchup.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting to board without pump support, ignoring life total, failing to sequence lands properly. Unique interactions: Legion Loyalist and Rundvelt can be played on the same turn for massive haste pump. Watch for 'Goblin Piledriver' or similar pump effects if format allows. Always prioritize playing your pump effects before your threats to maximize damage.

philosophy: 5c Heroic leverages a dense suite of cheap, targeted spells to rapidly stack +1/+1 counters on a diverse roster of creatures, transitioning from early board presence to evasive or resilient threats that overwhelm opponents through combat damage and spell synergy. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that struggle to answer multiple threats, while struggling against fast aggro and hard control that can disrupt the stack or race the early game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a Heroic creature (especially Illuminator Virtuoso or a cheap pump target). Prioritize hands with 2-3 lands and at least one targeted spell like Practiced Offense or a combat trick. Mulligan hands with only one color of mana, three+ lands with no early threats, or hands lacking a way to protect your early creatures (like Sheltered by Ghosts or similar protection).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, ideally a fetch/shock or dual that enters untapped to set up five-color mana. Turn 2: Cast a cheap Heroic creature, then immediately target it with a pump spell or combat trick to establish a 2/2 or 3/2 threat. If holding Sheltered by Ghosts, consider holding it for protection against removal or to enable indirect targeting if the card's mechanics allow. Sequence mana carefully to ensure you can cast both the creature and its first pump spell on curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Golgari Midrange and Orzhov Greasefang: Bring in additional removal and counterspells, plus cards that disrupt graveyard recursion. Against Selesnya Company and Izzet Prowess: Add more combat tricks, evasion enablers, and direct damage to handle token swarms and aggressive prowess creatures. Against Niv to Light and control: Bring in card draw, additional pump spells, and ways to protect your key threats from sweepers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Heroic relies on spell density; never run out of targeted spells unless you're at lethal. Use mana efficiently by chaining pump spells onto the same creature to maximize value, but diversify threats to avoid being one-sweeper'd. Manage your graveyard carefully if recursion is a threat; use exile effects or discard if needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit creatures when you have protection or enough pump spells to answer removal. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard removal in hand. Prioritize trading your smaller Heroic creatures to clear blockers for your larger, evasive threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (like Wrath of God effects), hard removal (Path to Exile, Doom Blade), and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents are holding up mana for counterspells or protection spells. Track their life total and board state to decide between racing or stabilizing.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large, evasive Heroic creatures with multiple +1/+1 counters, often paired with unblockable effects or combat tricks. Use Practiced Offense or similar late-game pumps to push lethal through blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no sweepers, or you've established a board state they cannot clear with their remaining resources. Commit when you can trade up favorably or when your life total is low enough that you must attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a dominant control deck with multiple sweepers and no card draw, if your hand is empty of targeted spells or creatures, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state (like Greasefang or a massive Company token army) that you cannot answer with your current resources.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap spells in a row to force counter spells, then pump the remaining creatures. Utilize indirect targeting if available, or rely on unblockable effects. Save protection spells for your key threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their mana acceleration. Use removal or counterspells to disrupt their key ramp creatures. If they stabilize, switch to a control-oriented approach with targeted removal and sweepers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and protection spells. Use your Heroic creatures to block and trade efficiently, then stabilize with removal. Avoid overcommitting to large creatures that can be wiped by sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total early to force mistakes. Use card draw and additional pump spells to out-value their removal. Target their key control pieces with your spells, and be prepared to trade creatures for their sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with better card draw and more efficient pump spells. Prioritize protecting your key threats with Sheltered by Ghosts or similar effects. Trade up favorably and use your sideboard cards to disrupt their spell density.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your spell-to-creature ratio based on the metagame. Against control and combo, increase interaction and card advantage. Against aggro and midrange, add more early-game threats and protection. Always ensure your mana base can handle the expected format demands, and consider bringing in format-specific hate cards against the top archetypes.
notes: 5c Heroic is highly dependent on spell density and mana consistency. Avoid overcommitting to a single creature unless you have protection. Be mindful of indirect targeting rules if your format supports them. Practice stack interactions carefully, as Heroic triggers resolve on the stack and can be responded to. Common mistake: pumping a creature to lethal but forgetting to account for combat damage or blocking, leading to unexpected losses.

philosophy: Boros Heroic is a spell-slinging combo-aggro deck that uses the Heroic mechanic to rapidly grow a single threat or flood the board, aiming to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. It wins by out-scaling midrange and out-pacing control through explosive turns fueled by Leyline of Resonance and Honor of the Pure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably one of each color or duals), at least one early creature (Monastery Swiftspear, Knight of the Skyward Eye, or Champion of the Parish), and 2+ cheap spells (Lightning Helix, Path to Exile, God's Willing, etc.). Keep Leyline of Resonance if you have a solid mana base and early threats. Mulligan hands with >2 lands, only lands, or no creatures/spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap spell on your creature if possible, or hold for optimal sequencing. Turn 2: Drop your main threat (Swiftspear), immediately cast a spell on it to trigger heroic, then attack. If you have Leyline, it enters untapped if you open with it. Sequence spells to maximize +1/+1 counters before combat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Helix), and life gain. Against control/combo: Bring in more threats (Champion of the Parish, Serra Ascendant), card draw (Farewell, God's Willing), and hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment). Against midrange: Bring in more removal, protection, and potentially more Honor of the Pure.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is critical; always keep open mana for protection spells or additional heroic triggers. Don't overcommit creatures if you have no spells to protect them or trigger heroic again. Graveyard is generally not a focus, but watch for graveyard recursion from opponents.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a spell in hand to protect them or trigger heroic again. Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted removal. Use your spells defensively (e.g., Lightning Helix on opposing threats, Path on key removal).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Damnation), targeted removal (Path, Murder), and combo pieces. Note if opponents are holding up mana for protection or counters. Track how many +1/+1 counters your creatures have to calculate combat math.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Honor of the Pure is the primary finisher; cast it when you have a board of heroic creatures to flood them with counters. Swiftspear with multiple counters can also close games. Leyline of Resonance doubles triggers for explosive turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a lethal threat on board, enough spells to protect it or trigger heroic again, and your opponent has no sweepers in hand (based on previous turns). Also commit when you can cast Honor of the Pure and have enough creatures to make it unblockable or lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one creature with no protection, facing a guaranteed sweeper or targeted removal, and have no cards in hand to recover. Also concede if you're locked out of mana or facing an unbeatable combo finisher with no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter. Cast threats in response to their counterspells. Use Leyline of Resonance to make triggers go off even if you cast multiple spells. Consider sideboarded hate (Leyline of Punishment, Rest in Peace if applicable).
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their mana development. Use removal to kill their ramp sources. Don't overextend; wait for them to overcommit.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and board wipes. Use your creatures as blockers when necessary, but prioritize surviving to cast Honor of the Pure. Gain life if possible.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats. Don't let them clear your board without trading. Use spells to protect your key creatures. Outlast them by having more threats than they have removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race. Prioritize casting spells on your creatures faster. Use removal to kill their threats before they can grow. Track their heroic triggers to know when they're about to go wide or big.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against control/combo, increase your threat density and add card advantage. Against aggro, add more removal and sweepers. Always keep Leyline of Resonance if you're running it, as it's a game-winning enabler. Adjust your land count if you're bringing in more non-basic lands or hate cards.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting creatures without spells to protect them or trigger heroic again. Another: Forgetting to count +1/+1 counters when calculating combat. Unique interaction: Leyline of Resonance doubles heroic triggers, so casting two spells on one creature gives it four +1/+1 counters. Honor of the Pure copies the +1/+1 counters on your creatures, so timing is crucial. Always check for 'target' spells to maximize heroic.

philosophy: A grindy control deck that stabilizes early, leverages Yorion, Sky Nomad for card advantage and incremental board presence, and wins through attrition and out-valuing midrange/control opponents. It excels against resource-heavy archetypes like Golgari Midrange and Orzhov Greasefang by out-grinding them, while carefully navigating faster matchups like Izzet Prowess and Selesnya Company with precise interaction and early stabilization.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early interaction spell (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares, or similar), and a value generator (Caretaker's Talent, Enduring Innocence, or a cheap creature). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or 0-1 lands with no interaction. Prioritize hands that can stabilize by turn 2-3 and have a clear path to casting Yorion by turn 4-5.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Caretaker's Talent if possible to draw a card and generate a token, or hold interaction for key threats. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Enduring Innocence if you control a creature, or develop a cheap creature. Sequence lands to ensure untapped mana for Yorion by turn 4-5. Prioritize playing lands that enter untapped and avoid overextending into potential sweepers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Izzet Prowess): +1 more removal, +1 board wipe, +1 anti-aggro protection. Against Control/Midrange (Golgari, Orzhov, Niv to Light): +1 more value engines, +1 targeted removal for key permanents, +1 anti-removal or protection. Against Selesnya Company: +1 more targeted removal, +1 way to disrupt their commander/synergy. Against Mirror: +1 more card draw, +1 more removal. Always keep Yorion and your core token generators.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve interaction for high-impact threats like planeswalkers, key creatures, and combo pieces. Use Yorion's +1 to cycle through your deck and find answers, but avoid overusing it if you're already ahead on board. Don't overcommit to board wipes unless necessary; prefer targeted removal to preserve your own tokens. Manage your graveyard by being aware of recursion threats and timing your removal accordingly.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answered their removal or when you're ahead on board. Use Yorion's -1 to create tokens for blocking or pressure, but hold back if you suspect sweepers. Prioritize board presence over hand size when you're ahead, but prioritize hand size when you're behind. Always assess whether committing a creature will leave you vulnerable to their interaction.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Day of Judgment, Wrath of God, etc.), key removal, and combo pieces. Note when opponents use their interaction on your early threats versus holding it for Yorion. Track their graveyard if they play recursion, and monitor their mana development to predict when they can cast their win conditions. Pay attention to their sideboard choices post-board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Yorion, Sky Nomad (via -1 for a token army or +1 for card advantage), aerial threats, or combat tricks. Win by out-valuing through multiple turns of Yorion activations and token generation, forcing opponents to trade resources inefficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have answered their removal, have Yorion on board, and can generate enough tokens to overwhelm their blockers or pressure their life total. Also commit when you have a clear advantage in card advantage and they have no sweepers or interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards, they have a guaranteed win condition (e.g., Greasefang with protection, Niv-Mizzet with mana open, or a combo piece), or if you're facing a hard counter to your game plan with no sideboard answers. Don't bluff through known interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost threats, cast Yorion when they're tapped out, use instant-speed interaction to clear the way, and consider casting spells in response to their counters to force trades. Prioritize casting Yorion's -1 to generate tokens, which are harder to counter than single threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Answer their mana accelerants early, use board wipes to reset their advantage, and race with Yorion tokens if they go wide. If they have a late-game finisher, use Yorion's +1 to out-draw them and find removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction, stabilize with Yorion as soon as possible, use board wipes sparingly to preserve your own threats, and transition to aggressive Yorion activations once stabilized. Don't overcommit to the board before turn 4.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Yorion, use card draw to out-resource them, and pressure with tokens when they're low on interaction. Avoid overcommitting to the board until you have card advantage. Force them to use interaction on your early threats to set up Yorion.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war, use Yorion's +1 to out-draw them, and be more willing to trade removal for their value engines. Prioritize keeping interaction to answer their Yorion, and track their sideboard for anti-value cards.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction count based on the metagame. Against control/midrange, increase your value engines and targeted removal. Against aggro, increase your early interaction and sweepers. Always keep Yorion and your core token generators. Adjust your land count if needed (usually 24-25 lands). Be prepared to sideboard in anti-removal or protection if the meta shifts.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board too early, failing to use Yorion's +1 efficiently, miscounting life totals when activating -1, and not tracking opponent's interaction. Unique interactions: Enduring Innocence can be cast for free if you control a creature, making it a powerful early play. Caretaker's Talent draws a card, so sequence it before Yorion to maximize value. Yorion's -1 creates tokens that can be used for blocking, so don't just use them for offense. Always be mindful of the format's specific meta shifts and adjust your sideboard accordingly.

philosophy: Dimir Aggro seeks to dominate the early game through aggressive disruption and efficient, evasive threats, out-resourcing midrange and control decks by trading resources favorably and closing out faster matchups before they stabilize. It wins by leveraging card quality, graveyard synergy, and tempo to out-value opponents while minimizing the impact of their sweepers and answers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (Thoughtseize, Kaito, Mockingbird, or a 2-drop). Keep hands with 2 lands and a turn-2 play, or 3+ lands with a turn-1 Thoughtseize. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of the same low-impact spell, or hands lacking early interaction/pressure. Prioritize hands that can execute a turn-1 Thoughtseize into a turn-2 threat or Kaito.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible. Strip the opponent's most disruptive card (removal, hand disruption, or early threat). Turn 2: Play a land, cast Kaito or Mockingbird if mana allows, or follow up with another Thoughtseize/removal. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your 1-drop and 2-drops on curve. If you have multiple lands, prioritize fetching/finding the right ones for your early spells, and avoid overcommitting to a board without answers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, etc.): Bring in more spot removal, discard (Thoughtseize, Duress), and graveyard hate. Against control/midrange (Golgari, Orzhov): Bring in additional threats (Kaito, Mockingbird), card advantage, and graveyard hate. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, discard, and board wipes if available. Against mirror: Bring in extra hand disruption and removal to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction when needed, but don't overcommit to holding up removal if you can safely develop your board. Use Thoughtseize to strip key answers or combo pieces. Manage your graveyard carefully; Bane of Nightmares can be a liability if the opponent has recursion, so consider graveyard hate or playing around it. Track your life total, as Kaito's emblem and Bane's effect can swing games quickly.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough interaction to protect them or when the opponent is out of answers. Hold back if you suspect a sweepers or if you need to preserve mana for a crucial Thoughtseize. Prioritize trading efficiently; don't overextend into known sweepers or when the opponent is holding up multiple answers. Use Kaito's emblem to draw into threats or answers when you're low on resources.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for the opponent's hand size and graveyard state. Note if they're holding up mana for removal or counters. Track when they play lands that suggest a specific strategy (e.g., dual lands for control, fetches for midrange). Pay attention to when they start casting their win conditions and adjust your pressure accordingly. Watch for graveyard recursion threats that could punish Bane of Nightmares or Mockingbird.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kaito, the Sunken City and Mockingbird are primary finishers. Use them to apply pressure, draw cards, and close out the game. Bane of Nightmares can also finish games if the opponent is low on life and you can protect it or sacrifice it for damage. Kaito's -7 ability wins the game instantly if you can survive long enough to activate it.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, the opponent has no sweepers, and you have enough threats to pressure for lethal. Commit when you can safely cast your finishers and have answers to their potential removal. If the opponent is at low life and you have a clear path to damage, don't hold back for marginal card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a dominant control or combo deck with no disruption, if you're down to 1-2 cards with no way to interact or deal damage, or if the opponent has stabilized with a board wipe and you lack the resources to rebuild. Concede early if you're out of resources and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to strip counterspells. Cast threats in response to their spells or when they're tapped out. Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter. Consider sideboard hate against specific counters. Don't overcommit to a board if you can't close quickly; trade efficiently and wait for them to use their answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to beat them before they hit their big mana. Use Thoughtseize to take their ramp spells or key threats. Use spot removal to deal with their early threats. Don't overextend into their late-game finishers. Force them to use resources inefficiently and close out the game before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and discard. Don't overcommit to a board if you can't close quickly. Use Bane of Nightmares to punish their graveyard recursion or sacrifice it for damage. Prioritize surviving the early turns and out-value them in the midgame. Keep your life total high and don't trade into their removal unnecessarily.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly with efficient threats. Use Thoughtseize to strip answers and combo pieces. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying pressure and force them to use resources inefficiently. Use Bane of Nightmares to disrupt their graveyard or finish them. Play around sweepers by holding back threats until they've used their answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Thoughtseize to strip their key cards. Trade efficiently and don't overextend. Use Bane of Nightmares to punish their graveyard or as a finisher. Sideboard in more disruption and removal to win the hand war. Track their hand size and graveyard state to make optimal discard and threat decisions.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against control/midrange, increase your threat density and card advantage. Against aggro/combo, prioritize disruption, graveyard hate, and early interaction. Always keep a balance between removal, discard, and threats. Don't over-index on one type of hate; maintain flexibility and be ready to pivot your game plan based on what the opponent is doing.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without answers, misusing Thoughtseize on low-impact cards, and ignoring graveyard hate when facing recursion decks. Kaito's emblem is powerful but can be a liability if you're low on life; manage your life total carefully. Bane of Nightmares requires careful timing; don't cast it into sweepers or when the opponent has graveyard recursion. Practice sequencing your turns to maximize value from each spell, and always prioritize winning the resource war over short-term board presence.

philosophy: The deck aims to out-value opponents through relentless spell-slinging, using Quintorius to dig for key pieces while leveraging Yorion and Sea Gate Restoration to generate flying Spirit tokens that overwhelm the board. It excels against control and midrange decks that overcommit to single answers, but struggles against fast aggro and heavy disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including duals/fixing), Quintorius, and at least one cantrip or removal spell. Mulligan to hands with 3+ lands and no spells, or hands with multiple copies of the same low-impact card. Prioritize fixing and early interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, ideally a dual or shock land if possible, or a basic that fixes into your next turn. Turn 2: Cast a cantrip or removal to stabilize, or play Quintorius if you have mana for it. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your second spell on turn 2 to trigger Yorion if possible, or set up Quintorius for turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Greasefang): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and life gain. Against control/midrange (Golgari, Selesnya): Bring in more cantrips, counterspells, and graveyard hate. Against combo: Bring in specific hate and additional removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Quintorius to cycle through your deck, but avoid discarding key combo pieces or removal. Prioritize casting instants/sorceries to trigger Yorion, but balance with board presence. Manage graveyard carefully if facing graveyard strategies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction or a board wipe ready. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered removal or counterspells. Use Yorion tokens to trade efficiently and apply pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and targeted removal. Note when opponents save removal for your key spells. Track graveyard activity if facing Niv to Light or Greasefang.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sea Gate Restoration (creates two flying Spirits), Yorion (generates tokens from spell casting), and Quintorius (digging for the final piece). Win by overwhelming the board with flying Spirits or out-resourcing the opponent.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have multiple cantrips/removal in hand, a clear path to cast two spells, and the opponent has no sweepers. Commit when you can trigger Yorion reliably and have enough interaction to protect your board.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card against a combo deck with a guaranteed win, or if you're facing a midrange deck with multiple sweepers and no removal in hand. Concede if your mana base is broken and you can't cast your key spells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig past counters, cast spells in response to counterspells, or use cheap spells to trigger Yorion. Consider sideboarding in spell Pierce or similar if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cantrips and removal. Use sweepers to clear their large threats. Out-value them with card advantage from Quintorius.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize spot removal and life gain. Use Yorion tokens to block efficiently. Don't overextend; stabilize first, then combo.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early spells, use Quintorius to dig for combo pieces. Bait out counterspells with cheap spells, then cast your win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, use Quintorius to dig deeper, and time your Yorion triggers to out-value their token generation. Sideboard in additional removal and counters.
