sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your mana base if needed, but focus on swapping in matchup-specific hate. Against control, bring in more counterspells and graveyard hate. Against aggro, bring in sweepers and life gain. Always keep your core combo pieces and interaction.
notes: Quintorius's ability is a draw/discard/play loop, so manage your graveyard carefully. Yorion requires casting two spells per turn, so sequence your turns to maximize token generation. Sea Gate Restoration is a powerful finisher but vulnerable to removal; protect it or cast it when safe. Avoid overcommitting to the board without interaction. Watch for opponent's sideboard cards that target your strategy.

philosophy: This deck leverages heavy mana acceleration and fixing to overcome its five-color mana requirements, deploying massive scaling threats like Soulflayer and Zetalpa that demand multiple answers to remove. It wins by out-scaling midrange and control archetypes with exponential board states, resilient finishers, and flexible tutoring that turns any board state into a win condition.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetches, duals, or shock lands for fixing), at least one mana acceleration spell (e.g., Arcane Signet, Sol Ring, Llanowar Elves, Rampant Growth), and a turn-2 or turn-3 play. Mulligan any hand with only single-colored basic lands, no ramp, or exclusively high-cost spells without acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-drop or hold for turn 2 ramp. Turn 2: Cast acceleration or a 2-drop creature. Turn 3: Cast a second ramp or a 3-drop to reliably hit five mana by turn 4-5. Prioritize fixing and ramp over non-essential threats; your game plan hinges on casting five-color spells on curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Golgari Midrange): Bring in spot removal (e.g., Path to Exile, Doom Blade) and early interaction. Against control (Selesnya Company, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in additional threats, card advantage, and protection spells. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells, and discard to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary constraint; always maintain a 2-3 mana buffer for protection or interaction. Use your graveyard strategically if recursion is present, but prioritize keeping key creatures on board. Draw spells to maintain hand size, as five-color decks often run dry of answers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or can follow up with another threat. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use Zetalpa’s Golem token to block or trade into removal, and time Soulflayer’s entry to maximize its power-scaling ability.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Hero’s Downfall), targeted removal, and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for answers. Track graveyard hate if recursion is present, and monitor when control decks stabilize with card draw or lifegain.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Soulflayer with multiple +1/+1 counters or board presence, Ketramose tutoring for a game-ending threat, or Zetalpa generating a flying Golem that trades into key blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 5+ mana available, protection for your threat, and the opponent has no sweepers or has already used theirs. Go all-in when you can follow up with a second threat or have enough board presence to weather a wipe.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you’re down to one life source with no ramp, facing a locked board, or in a matchup where your win conditions are consistently answered (e.g., heavy graveyard hate or infinite combo).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split the cast (cast one, hold mana for protection), or use uncounterable effects if available. Bounce or sacrifice Zetalpa to bypass counters if needed.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on their ramp sources, and don’t let them stabilize. Use your own acceleration to stay ahead.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction, board wipes, or fast threats. Don’t overcommit to big creatures if you can’t stabilize. Use Zetalpa’s Golem to block.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats, use card advantage, and don’t let them stabilize. Use Ketramose to find value creatures or win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize removal, card draw, and protection. Out-value them with better sequencing and sideboard cards.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against control, bring in more threats and card advantage. Against aggro, bring in removal and early interaction. In mirrors, bring in counterspells and targeted removal. Always keep your core ramp and fixing.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to a single big threat without protection. Unique interaction: Zetalpa’s Golem can be sacrificed to trigger 'when a creature dies' effects or trade into removal. Ketramose can tutor for any 5-mana or less creature, making it incredibly flexible. Mana base is critical; consider fetching lands early.

philosophy: Rakdos Prowess aims to win by efficiently trading spells for damage, leveraging the Prowess mechanic to scale threats into unblockable or unkillable attackers. It excels at racing control and combo decks while applying relentless tempo pressure to midrange and aggro strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one turn-1 or turn-2 threat (Slickshot Show-Off, Monastery Swiftspear, Emberheart Challenger), and cheap spells/removal (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike, Go Blank). Prioritize hands that can cast a spell on turn 1 or 2. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only expensive threats, or no way to trigger Prowess early.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land, cast a cheap spell (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike, or Slickshot Show-Off if mana allows). Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another spell, and drop a mid-tier threat like Emberheart Challenger or Slickshot. Sequence lands to ensure access to double red/black for Swiftspear and Emberheart, but don't flood with red if you need black for removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Golgari Midrange, Selesnya Company): Bring in additional removal (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike), discard, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Control/Combo (Niv to Light): Bring in more threats (Swiftspear, Emberheart), discard, and board-wipe protection. vs Izzet Prowess/Mirror: Bring in discard, efficient removal, and cards that punish overextensions.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your hand as a resource to trigger Prowess; don't hoard spells. Use removal only when necessary to preserve threats. Keep an eye on your graveyard for Fatal Push targets and Bloodtithe Harvester value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal. Hold back Monastery Swiftspear until it can swing for lethal or trade favorably. Don't overextend into potential board wipes or hard removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Doom Blade), and counterspells. Note when the opponent taps out for a single spell—that’s your window to drop a threat. Track graveyard contents for Fatal Push and Harvester synergies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Monastery Swiftspear is your primary win condition. Once it reaches 4/4 or larger, swing aggressively. Emberheart Challenger can also close games if it survives combat or trades into a key blocker.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, no sweepers, and you have Prowess triggers ready to scale your attackers. Also commit when you can race their win condition or force a combat math advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re stuck on one land against aggro, have no answers to a board wipe, or face a hard lock (e.g., graveyard hate, artifact lock) with no way to break it. Also concede if you’re down to a dead hand and the opponent has stabilized with card advantage.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast multiple cheap spells to force counters, then drop a threat. Use instant-speed threats or sacrifice outlets to bypass counters. Sometimes accepting a counter is fine if you maintain pressure and keep triggering Prowess.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them aggressively. They’re slow early, so don’t let them stabilize. Use discard and removal to disrupt their mana development and force them to spend resources on interaction instead of acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use removal efficiently—save it for key threats. Trade Slickshot Show-Off into early attackers. Hold Swiftspear for late-game combat or to finish them off. Don't blow removal on 1/1s unless necessary.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Use Prowess to out-value their removal. Force them to use multiple spells to kill one threat. Don’t let them stabilize with card draw; keep the board state threatening enough to force mistakes.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through resource efficiency and sequencing. Use discard to disrupt their game plan. Avoid overextending; value trades and win by out-scaling or out-removing. Prioritize keeping your Swiftspear alive until it can swing for lethal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the matchup. Against slower decks, add more threats and discard to apply pressure. Against faster decks, add removal and early-game interaction. Always prioritize keeping hands that can interact early and threaten by turn 3. Trim excess lands if you're consistently flooding, and add more interaction if you're getting steamrolled early.
notes: Prowess rewards spell efficiency, so don’t fear casting seemingly 'bad' spells if they trigger Prowess. Watch your graveyard for Fatal Push and Harvester value. Don’t overcommit to the board without considering sweepers. Practice combat math with Swiftspear to avoid misplays. Remember that Prowess triggers on any instant, sorcery, or activated ability, so use creature abilities and discard effects to your advantage.

philosophy: Gruul Midrange leverages early mana acceleration and efficient threats to outpace control and grind down midrange decks through incremental board advantage. It wins by converting mana advantage into unblockable or resilient pressure, closing games before opponents can stabilize or find their win conditions.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2 lands and at least one of Elvish Mystic, Reckless Stormseeker, or a two-drop threat. Prioritize hands with 1-2 lands if they contain acceleration plus a turn-2 play. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or hands with only removal and no threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally tap Elvish Mystic if you have another land or can afford to delay. Turn 2: Cast Elvish Mystic or a two-drop threat, then play a second land. If opening with acceleration, play land, then Elvish Mystic, then cast a two-drop on turn 2. Always prioritize developing mana quickly to cast turn-3 threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): Bring in direct damage, spot removal, and early interaction. vs Control (Niv to Light): Bring in more threats, card advantage, and protection. vs Midrange (Golgari/Orzhov): Bring in sweepers, removal, and graveyard hate if needed. vs Selesnya Company: Bring in targeted removal and early pressure.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction when needed, but aggressively convert acceleration into board presence. Don't overextend into sweepers; keep a threat in hand if you suspect a board wipe. Manage hand size by trading removal for threats when ahead.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no removal or sweepers. Hold back a key threat if you suspect a board wipe or if you're behind on board. Prioritize trading your smallest threats into their removal to preserve your finishers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal, and ramp spells. Note if the opponent is tapping out for a single spell (likely a sweeper or big threat). Track graveyard usage for midrange decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Reckless Stormseeker and late-game Gruul threats. Win by overwhelming the board with unblockable/haste threats or trading up into their remaining resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear board advantage, the opponent has no interaction, or you're facing a combo/control deck that needs to stabilize. Also commit when you can close the game before their next turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card against a deck with a proven win condition, if your hand is completely dead against the current board state, or if you're in a heavily unfavorable matchup with no comeback tools.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that can be cast in response to counterspells. Employ uncounterable threats if available. Sequence plays to force counterspells on non-critical spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use spot removal to eliminate their mana dorks. Don't overcommit to the board; trade efficiently.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and spot interaction. Don't overextend; focus on surviving turn 3-4. Use your midrange threats to close the game once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early to force mistakes. Use card advantage to out-value them. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying pressure and trading resources favorably.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing. Prioritize keeping a threat in hand for interaction. Use removal efficiently to trade up. Win the resource war by converting acceleration into board presence faster.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against control, add more late-game threats and card draw. Against aggro, prioritize early interaction and clock. In mirror matches, bring in targeted removal and protection. Always ensure your sideboard cards synergize with your acceleration strategy.
notes: Common mistake: Over-tapping Elvish Mystic and failing to play a second land, stalling your curve. Another: Overextending into sweepers without checking for interaction. Unique interaction: Using acceleration to cast threats in response to removal, forcing unfavorable trades. Always track opponent's mana and hand size to predict their next move.

philosophy: A U/B graveyard recursion engine that trades efficiently to out-value opponents, leveraging discard to protect its game plan and using creatures like Bloodghast, Prized Amalgam, and Narcomoeba to generate escalating board presence and card advantage. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and control decks while out-grinding graveyard-dependent strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably one of each color or fetch/shock-compatible), at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), and a recursion enabler (Narcomoeba/Bloodghast). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no discard, or only high-cost spells without graveyard support. Prioritize hands that can establish the graveyard quickly or apply early pressure.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Inquisition to strip key threats or discard a cheap spell to build the graveyard. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a cheap threat or recursion enabler, or continue applying discard. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/finding duals or shock lands early to ensure consistent U/B mana, but don't overextend into removal if the board is vulnerable.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess): Bring in board wipes, early interaction, and life gain. Against control (Niv to Light): Bring in more discard, graveyard protection, and direct damage. Against graveyard decks (Golgari Midrange, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in Rest in Peace, Tormod's Crypt, or Relic of Progenitus. Against Selesnya Company: Bring in targeted removal and sweepers. Against mirror: Bring in additional recursion hate, discard, and card draw.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting threats, recursion, and interaction. Don't overcommit to the graveyard if you lack recursion payoffs. Use Narcomoeba to trade efficiently, Bloodghast to stabilize and generate mana, and Prized Amalgam to snowball when the graveyard is full. Keep hand size optimal by using discard proactively.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent lacks removal. Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted hate. Use recursion to maintain board presence without depleting your hand. Trade Narcomoeba into removal to preserve Bloodghast and Prized Amalgam for later.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Tormod's Crypt, Relic), board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), and targeted removal (Path to Exile, Murder). Note when opponents save removal for your key threats. Track their discard targets to anticipate what they're trying to stop and adjust your graveyard strategy accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Prized Amalgam with multiple instant/sorcery counters, Bloodghast armies, or Narcomoeba swarms. Win by overwhelming the board or trading up into unblockable threats. Use discard to strip win conditions and force unfavorable trades.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, no graveyard hate, or when you can safely recurse a Prized Amalgam into a lethal threat. Commit when you have card advantage and can protect your board. Push through damage when recursion can outpace their life total or removal cycle.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no interaction, if your graveyard is permanently exiled, or if you're down to a dead hand with no recursion enablers. Also concede against sweepers if you have no board presence to rebuild or if the opponent has stabilized with life gain and card draw.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to strip counterspells or win conditions. Cast threats in response to counterspells if possible. Use graveyard recursion to bypass counters since recursion triggers from the graveyard, not the stack. Force them to use removal on your graveyard threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and cheap threats. Don't let them stabilize. Use graveyard hate if they rely on it, otherwise out-value them with recursion. Disrupt their mana base with discard and pressure their early game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and discard to disrupt their curve. Use Bloodghast to stabilize and generate mana. Don't overextend into removal; trade efficiently with Narcomoeba. Sideboard in sweepers and early blockers to survive the initial assault.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with discard to strip answers. Build the graveyard steadily. Use Prized Amalgam to snowball when they run out of removal. Force them to use resources on your graveyard threats and punish their slow game plan with incremental value.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping recursion enablers. Use Prized Amalgam to out-value. Track their graveyard hate and adapt. Trade efficiently and don't overcommit to the graveyard without payoffs. Favor games where you can recur multiple threats before they can answer them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control-heavy formats, increase discard and graveyard protection. Against aggro, add early interaction and sweepers. Against graveyard strategies, prioritize hate cards. Always keep a balance of recursion enablers and interaction. Monitor opponent play patterns and adjust sideboard cards mid-tournament, prioritizing hate against the most common top archetypes.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting to the graveyard without recursion payoffs, ignoring discard timing, failing to track opponent's hate cards, and overextending into removal. Unique interactions: Prized Amalgam scales with every instant/sorcery in the graveyard, so even cheap spells like Thoughtseize or discard make it massive. Bloodghast's mana ability can be used to cast spells in response to removal. Narcomoeba can be used as a sacrifice target for other synergies. Always prioritize graveyard consistency over raw card count.

philosophy: Jeskai Ascendancy is a five-color control-aggro hybrid that aims to resolve Jeska's Ascension and rapidly stack +1/+1 counters on a key threat to win via combat or card advantage. It thrives in control- and midrange-heavy metagames by outlasting opponents with escalating board presence, efficient card selection, and resilient win conditions.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks), Jeska's Ascension, and a protection or casting enabler (e.g., Lightning Greaves, Karn, or cheap cantrips). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of Ascension without answers, or hands lacking early interaction/mana acceleration. Prioritize hands with Badgermole Cub or Stormchaser's Talent for early card draw and pump synergy.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction. Turn 2: Cast Stormchaser's Talent or Badgermole Cub, fetch a shock land, or play a cheap threat. Sequence mana carefully to ensure you can cast Ascension by turn 3-4 while maintaining interaction. Against aggro, prioritize early threats and spot removal; against control, prioritize card draw and protection.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and counterspells. Against control/midrange (Golgari Midrange, Orzhov Greasefang): Bring in additional protection, extra threats, and graveyard hate if needed. Against combo: Bring in counterspells, graveyard hate, and additional interaction. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells, and extra protection for Ascension.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is tight due to five-color requirements; use fetches and shock lands efficiently to avoid flooding. Keep card advantage flowing with Badgermole Cub and Stormchaser's Talent. Protect Ascension at all costs—it is the engine. Do not overcommit to the board if Ascension is still in hand or on the stack.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or removal for the opponent's key pieces. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy interaction. Use Ascension to stabilize against aggressive boards by turning a single threat into a game-ending threat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), counterspells, and graveyard strategies. Note when opponents tap out for interaction, as this is your window to cast Ascension. Track their mana base to predict if they can answer your threats or stabilize.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully pumped creature (often via Badgermole Cub/Stormchaser's Talent + Ascension) or a board of counters. Win via combat or card advantage if the opponent is out of resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Ascension on the battlefield, protection for it, and enough mana to cast a threat or pump spell. Also commit when the opponent has no sweepers or interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard lock (e.g., empty hand, no lands, no interaction), if the opponent has multiple sweepers and you have no way to protect Ascension, or if you are consistently drawing dead cards in a long game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use protection (Lightning Greaves, counterspells), split your spells, or cast Ascension when the opponent is tapped out. Use cantrips to dig for answers or protection.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Do not let them stabilize; win before they cast their finisher.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and spot removal. Use Badgermole Cub/Stormchaser's Talent to stabilize and draw into answers. Do not overextend; wait for Ascension to turn a single threat into a win condition.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and incremental advantage. Use Ascension to pressure their life total while they try to find answers. Do not commit all resources at once; apply pressure gradually.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize protection for Ascension, use targeted removal to eliminate their threats, and out-draw them with Badgermole Cub/Stormchaser's Talent. Control the board with sweepers if available.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite to match the metagame: prioritize sweeps and counters against aggro, and protection/targeted removal against control/midrange. In the mirror, focus on counterspells and targeted removal. Always keep Jeska's Ascension and your primary pump targets in the main deck, and avoid overloading on single-target answers that become dead draws against sweepers.
notes: Five-color mana can be inconsistent; practice land sequencing and fetch usage to avoid flooding or screwing. Badgermole Cub and Stormchaser's Talent are crucial for card draw and pump synergy—do not cast them lightly without a clear plan. Jeska's Ascension is fragile; always have a plan to protect it or recast it. Be aware of format-specific hate cards (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment, or graveyard hate) and adjust your sideboard accordingly. Avoid overcommitting to a single threat if the opponent has sweepers in hand.

philosophy: Mono-White Humans is a fast, aggressive tribal strategy that floods the board with efficient, synergistic Human creatures while applying tax effects like Thalia to disrupt opponents' resources. It aims to outpace midrange and control decks through relentless early pressure, combat-based card advantage, and late-game resilience with Adeline.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands and at least 3-4 one-mana Human creatures (e.g., Knight-Captain, Ambitious Assault, Thalia's Lieutenant, or early pump spells). Prioritize hands that can play a creature on turn 1 and a second on turn 2. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no early threats, 0-1 lands with no recovery plan, or hands missing both lands and one-mana threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Plains, cast a one-mana Human (Knight-Captain, Ambitious Assault, or Thalia's Lieutenant). Turn 2: Play a Plains, cast a second Human or a pump spell (Rally the Righteous, Steelshaper's Gift). If you have Thalia's Lieutenant, prioritize casting it to tax opponent non-Human spells. Land sequencing: Play basic Plains first; if using fetch/shock, ensure you can hit basics for consistency.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs. Golgari Midrange/Orzhov Greasefang: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells, and Adeline protection. vs. Selesnya Company: Bring in more removal, counters, and a board wipe if available. vs. Niv to Light/Izzet Prowess: Bring in early threats, counters, and lifelink enablers to beat them out. vs. Aggro: Bring in +1/+1 counters, removal, and lifelink. vs. Mirror: Bring in removal, counters, and card draw/pump to out-value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction and finishing blows. Avoid overcommitting to the board if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use combat to trigger lifelink/vigilance and maintain card advantage. Protect your graveyard from discard effects and keep key pieces (especially Adeline) safe from targeted removal.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection for Adeline or the opponent has no sweepers. Hold back if the opponent has potential board wipes or heavy removal. Use Thalia's tax to force unfavorable trades or make the opponent discard/sacrifice creatures. Trade efficiently to maintain board presence without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and counterspells. Note when the opponent taps out for a sweeper or heavy removal. Track graveyard hate if facing combo/midrange. Pay attention to when the opponent uses their last interaction, as this is your window to close out the game.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Adeline, Resplendent Cathar (with pump/vigilance), and fully buffed Humans. Win by trading efficiently, leveraging lifelink to stabilize, and using combat tricks to close out the game. Adeline's +1/+1 and vigilance make her a formidable finisher that can outlast control and midrange decks.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection for Adeline, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can trade up favorably. Commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns or when the opponent has exhausted their interaction. Use combat tricks to maximize damage and secure lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 lands with no threats, the opponent has a locked board with sweepers/removal, or you're facing a combo deck that has assembled its win condition. Don't bluff through known counters or sweepers. If you're consistently losing combat despite optimal play, concede to preserve resources for future games.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (1-mana Humans). Save interaction for key spells. Use pump spells or combat tricks to close out before counters can react. If possible, bring in more uncounterable threats or protection spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to beat them out. Use removal to kill their mana dorks or key ramp creatures. Thalia's tax makes their ramp spells expensive, slowing them down. Force them to spend resources on interaction rather than acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and lifelink. Don't overextend; trade efficiently. Use Thalia to tax their aggressive spells. Bring in more removal and lifelink enablers. Focus on surviving the early turns and out-grinding them in the midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Use combat tricks to bypass blockers. Force them to use removal on your key pieces. Adeline can outlast them if protected. Use card advantage to out-value their control elements. Don't let them stabilize; keep the pressure high.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-valuing. Use removal to kill their key pieces. Save counters for their Adeline or Thalia. Trade efficiently and use pump spells to win combat. Card advantage and protection are key. Look for opportunities to trade your less valuable creatures for their better ones.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Generally, bring in more removal and protection against control/midrange, and more early threats/lifelink against aggro. In mirrors, prioritize removal, counters, and card draw. Always keep Thalia's Lieutenant and Adeline if possible, as they are core to the game plan. Be flexible with your land count and interaction based on opponent strategies.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without protection for Adeline, ignoring opponent potential sweepers, and mismanaging mana for interaction. Thalia's Lieutenant's tax applies to opponent spells, not yours, so use it to disrupt their game plan. Adeline's +1/+1 and vigilance make her a formidable finisher, but she requires protection. Use combat tricks to maximize damage and watch for format-specific removal and sweepers. Practice tracking opponent interaction to know when to commit or hold back.

philosophy: Dimir Control dictates the game through efficient interaction, card advantage, and tempo control, out-resourcing opponents until it can deploy Ashiok, Nightmare Muse or leverage Fae of Wishes to access tailored hate. It excels against midrange and combo decks that overcommit to the board, while struggling against fast, uninteractive aggro or highly resilient value engines.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or shocklands) and at least one interaction or cantrip. Keep Fae of Wishes or Ashiok only if supported by 2+ lands and a land drop or cantrip. Mulligan to 4+ lands, hands with zero spells, or hands with multiple Drown in the Loch but no lands. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a meaningful spell by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic Island or fetch/dual land. If you have a cantrip or cheap interaction, cast it to maintain parity. Turn 2: Develop mana with a second land, then cast Drown in the Loch or a discard spell. If you have Fae of Wishes, prefer casting it on turn 3 with a cantrip to maximize value. Always prioritize maintaining card parity; never cast removal prematurely if you can answer with a land drop and hold interaction for a bigger threat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Golgari Midrange/Selesnya Company: Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace/Leyline of the Void), extra removal (Go for the Throat/Fatal Push), and artifact/enchantment hate. Against Orzhov Greasefang/Izzet Prowess: Bring in more direct damage, counterspells, and early interaction (additional Drown in the Loch, Thoughtseize, or a board wipe). Against Niv to Light/control: Bring in card advantage engines, additional removal, and planeswalker hate. Against mirror: Bring in extra counterspells, discard, and targeted removal for key threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Keep mana flexible; avoid overcommitting to a single color unless casting a finisher. Use cantrips to cycle through dead draws and maintain hand size. Protect your graveyard from exploitation, but don't fear it if you have Ashiok. Prioritize keeping interaction in hand over casting it prematurely; Dimir wins by answering the most threatening spell while preserving resources for the endgame.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to resolving them or when the board state demands it. Ashiok should be played when you can protect it or when you have enough removal to handle immediate threats. Hold back on overextending; Dimir's win condition is out-resourcing, not out-timing. If the opponent is going wide, prioritize board wipes or targeted removal over single-target answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for the opponent's mana development and hand size. Key tells include holding up mana on your turn (likely holding a counter or removal), casting spells at instant speed after you've committed to a play, and graveyard activity (signaling midrange or combo). Track when they use their removal on your threats versus holding it for your finishers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ashiok, Nightmare Muse is the primary win condition, generating card advantage and board control through its triggered abilities. Fae of Wishes can also close games by fetching targeted hate or additional threats. Secondary finishers include planeswalkers or late-game removal that clears the board and leaves you with card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, the opponent has no clear answer to your finisher, or you're ahead in card advantage. Commit when you can resolve Ashiok safely or when you have enough interaction to handle the opponent's response.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast, uninteractive deck with no way to stabilize, if you're down to one card in hand against a multi-card combo, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state with no removal or counterspells in your deck to address it. Also concede if you're consistently drawing lands and no interaction in the late game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig through counters, cast spells at unexpected times (end step, after they've used their counter), or rely on uncounterable threats like Ashiok. If possible, bring in more uncounterable spells or discard to remove their counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to disrupt their mana development. Use discard to remove key ramp spells, and board wipes to handle their big threats. If they get ahead, use Ashiok to stabilize and out-value them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early interaction in hand, prioritize cheap removal and counterspells, and use Fae of Wishes to fetch early hate. Don't overextend; stabilize and win the late game with card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with superior card draw and efficient removal. Use Ashiok to generate advantage, and pressure them with threats they can't answer. If they're slow, apply pressure with Fae of Wishes and late-game threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better card advantage, more efficient interaction, and superior sideboard choices. Prioritize keeping discard and counterspells, and use Ashiok to out-resource them. Track their hand and play around their key answers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the metagame. Against slower decks, bring in more late-game interaction and card advantage. Against faster decks, prioritize early removal and hate. Always ensure your sideboard cards synergize with Fae of Wishes and Ashiok. Keep an eye on opponent trends and adjust your main deck if certain cards are consistently underperforming.
notes: Fae of Wishes is a versatile tool but requires careful timing; don't use it to fetch a card you could just draw. Ashiok's triggers are powerful but can be disrupted by graveyard hate, so protect your graveyard or have a backup plan. Drown in the Loch is excellent but can be countered; have a backup plan if it's met with a counter. Avoid overcommitting to the board; Dimir wins by out-resourcing, not out-timing. Practice recognizing when to hold up mana versus when to commit to a play.

philosophy: Bant Spirits is a fast, evasive tempo-aggro deck that leverages cheap spells and instant-speed interaction to grow +1/+1 counters on its spirit threats, overwhelming opponents before they can stabilize. It excels against slower midrange and combo decks that struggle to answer multiple flying, growing threats efficiently.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetch/shock-compatible), at least one 1-drop threat (Skyclave Apparition, Phantom Warrior), and a mix of cheap spells or removal. Keep hands with Thoughtseize/Inquisition if you have a solid early curve. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only 2-drops or higher, or hands with multiple removal but no threats or mana acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-drop threat or cast a cheap spell (Spell Pierce, Remand, or Thoughtseize) to grow your board and disrupt. Turn 2: Play a shock land/fetch, cast a second threat or spot removal, and continue casting spells to grow your creatures. Prioritize establishing a board of evasive threats while holding up interaction for key opponent plays.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Golgari Midrange, Selesnya Company): Bring in Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Meek, Leyline of Punishment, Lightning Bolt, Chalice of the Void. vs Control/Combo (Niv to Light, Izzet Prowess): Bring in Leyline of Sanctity, Leyline of Abundance, additional spot removal, and graveyard hate. vs Mirror: Bring in Leyline of the Meek, Leyline of Punishment, and additional removal to win the interaction war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Optimize mana for shock lands and instant-speed interaction. Avoid overextending into sweepers. Use the graveyard strategically by not giving opponents free targets or recursion material. Manage your hand by holding removal for key threats rather than burning it on small creatures.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to answer their removal or when they're low on life. Hold back if they have sweepers or heavy removal in hand. Use removal efficiently on their most impactful threats, saving spot removal for creatures that can swing the game state.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline), and counterspells. Note when they tap out for a big spell or when they're holding up mana for interaction. Track their life total to know when you can safely commit to lethal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Supreme Phantom and Mausoleum Wanderer are primary finishers. Once they're large enough (3+ counters), they can close out the game in 1-2 turns. Use evasion and spot removal to clear the path and force trades.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a board of 2+ growing threats, they have no sweepers, and you're within lethal range. Also commit when you've established graveyard advantage or when they're tapped out for a non-interactive spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no draw engines, if you're down to 1 threat against a board of sweepers, or if you're facing a hate-heavy sideboard (Rest in Peace + Leyline) with no way to break through or rebuild.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats that can be cast in response to counterspells, or hold up interaction to protect your key spells. Use Thoughtseize to remove their counterspells. Consider Chalice of the Void on 1 or 2 to disrupt their mana or key spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum pressure early before they can cast their big spells. Use spot removal to kill their ramp creatures. Sideboard in additional removal and hate cards to slow their acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and counterspells. Don't overcommit to the board. Use Leyline of the Meek or Rest in Peace to disrupt their graveyard/strategy. Transition to a control-oriented midgame if needed.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Use Thoughtseize to remove their counterspells and sweepers. Cast threats in response to their removal. Use evasion to bypass board wipes. Sideboard in additional pressure and hate cards to break their lock.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use Thoughtseize/Inquisition to strip their key spells. Prioritize graveyard advantage. Use Leyline of the Meek/Punishment to disrupt their strategy. Win the interaction war by holding up removal for their key threats and out-resourcing them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggro to a more control-oriented tempo game plan. Prioritize hate cards against the metagame (Rest in Peace, Leylines, additional removal). Adjust land count if needed (usually keep 23-24 lands). Focus on disrupting opponent's game plan while maintaining enough pressure to close out games.
notes: Common mistake: Overextending into sweepers or holding up too much mana for interaction. Always track opponent's mana and hand size. Use fetch/shock cycle efficiently to avoid flooding. Supreme Phantom's late-game power is immense, so don't be afraid to cast it even if it seems slow early. Remember that casting any instant/sorcery grows your board, so use removal and counterspells aggressively to maximize your threat size.

philosophy: Orzhov Humans is a resilient, value-driven aggro deck that leverages +1/+1 counters, lifelink, and card advantage to outlast and overwhelm opponents. It aims to close games quickly with evasive threats while using Dark Confidant and Thalia's Lieutenant to maintain resource parity against control and midrange strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably shocklands/duals or fetches), a turn-1 play (Adeline, Resplendent Cathar, or Thalia's Lieutenant), and Dark Confidant if possible. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only 2-drops+, or hands missing early threats and mana fixing. Prioritize hands that can play a threat on turn 1 or 2 while maintaining a 2-land curve.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Dark Confidant or a 1-drop (Adeline/Cathar/Lieutenant). Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-drop or attack with existing threats. Sequence lands to ensure consistent early drops; fetch if you have a shockland in hand. If ahead, commit threats; if behind, hold back and stabilize with lifegain or removal. Never overextend into potential sweepers without protection.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control/combo: Bring in Disenchant/Path to Exile, Surgical Extraction, Rest in Peace. Against aggro/ramp (Niv to Light, Izzet Prowess): Bring in Heroic Intervention, Lightning Helix, additional removal. Against midrange (Golgari, Selesnya Company): Bring in targeted removal, graveyard hate, and additional lifegain. Against mirror/Orzhov Greasefang: Bring in Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and extra removal to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Dark Confidant as a win condition and engine; protect it with lifegain or removal if possible, but don't over-invest if you're already ahead. Manage hand size to avoid discarding key threats to discard spells. Use lifegain from Cathar and Lieutenant to stabilize against burn and enable Confidant's draw engine.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or pump ready to protect them, or when the opponent has tapped out for answers. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment) or heavy targeted removal. Don't overextend into combat if you can't trade efficiently or stabilize with lifelink.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal (Path, Murder), and discard (Thoughtseize, Inquisition). Note when opponents tap out for counterspells or answers. Track graveyard usage against Greasefang and midrange decks. Pay attention to when the opponent holds up mana for multiple turns, indicating a potential board wipe or finisher.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Adeline, Resplendent Cathar, and Thalia's Lieutenant with pump spells or +1/+1 counters. Dark Confidant can also close games if left unchecked. Use lifegain to stabilize while chipping away at life totals.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers, or you're at lethal range. Commit when you can protect your board with lifegain, removal, or pump spells. Don't hold back if the opponent is on a clock and you have the tools to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, the opponent has a guaranteed sweeper/removal combo, or you're facing a lock piece (like Rest in Peace + graveyard hate) with no way to break it. Concede if you're out-valued in a control mirror or facing a combo that can't be disrupted.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap 1-drops that are hard to counter effectively, or sequence around counterspells by playing around them on turns they could have mana. Use pump spells or discard to disrupt their hand. Don't overcommit to a single threat if they have multiple counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to beat their ramp out. Use removal for their mana dorks/acceleration. Don't overcommit if they have a massive finisher; stabilize with lifegain and trade efficiently. Force them to answer your threats instead of developing their board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use lifegain from Cathar and Lieutenant to stabilize. Hold removal for their key threats. Don't overextend into combat; trade efficiently and use discard to disrupt their game plan. Prioritize survival over aggression in the first few turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure consistently. Use Dark Confidant to out-value them. Don't give them free turns; attack every opportunity. Use discard to remove key answers and force them to use resources inefficiently. Push through damage even if it means taking a few hits.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize keeping Dark Confidant alive. Use discard to remove their threats. Trade efficiently and value out their Lieutenant/Cathar. Control the board with targeted removal and graveyard hate. Don't overcommit; value out their plays and close the game when you have an advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to a more controlled, value-oriented approach. Bring in targeted removal and graveyard hate against control/combo. Adjust land counts if needed, and prioritize hands with early threats and interaction. Play more conservatively against control, focusing on out-valuing them with Confidant and Lieutenant. Against aggro, prioritize stabilization and efficient trades.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without considering sweepers or holding back too long against control. Unique interaction: Thalia's Lieutenant's lifelink and +1/+1 counter synergy with Cathar's lifegain creates a resilient board that can outlast midrange and control. Dark Confidant is a double-edged sword; manage its death trigger carefully and don't let it die to removal if you're already ahead. Always track the opponent's graveyard against Greasefang and midrange decks.

philosophy: Azorius Spirits is a fast, interactive aggro deck that leverages instant-speed Spirit synergy to generate board presence, disrupt opponents, and close games before they can stabilize. It wins by out-racing midrange, out-tempoing control, and overwhelming tribal or aggressive strategies with evasion and targeted removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetches), at least one 1-drop Spirit (e.g., Cryptbreaker or Spirited Phantom), and early interaction (No More Lies, spot removal, or bounce). Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no early threats, multiple expensive cards with no mana base, or zero interaction against aggro/control.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-drop Spirit. Turn 2: Develop board with another Spirit, use Cryptbreaker’s ability to bounce a blocker or disrupt a spell, or cast No More Lies to protect a threat or counter an opponent’s play. Prioritize casting Spirits in response to opponent spells to maximize Rattlechains and Supreme Phantom triggers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Prowess, Greasefang): Bring in more spot removal, counters, and early interaction. Against control (Niv to Light): Bring in more threats, card advantage, and recursion. Against midrange (Golgari): Bring in more removal, counters, and graveyard hate. Against Company/mirror: Bring in more spot removal, counters, and artifact/enchantment hate if relevant.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Keep 1-2 mana open for instant-speed interaction and Spirit triggers. Don’t overcommit to the board if the opponent has sweepers. Use the graveyard strategically with No More Lies to recycle threats or disrupt the opponent.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to handle blockers or sweepers. Hold back if the opponent has potential board wipes or can out-value you. Use flying tokens to pressure planeswalkers or close games.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for big spells. Track if they’re playing around No More Lies by holding blue mana. Watch for ramp/acceleration in midrange decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Supreme Phantom token generation, Cryptbreaker for targeted removal, and aerial assaults with flying Spirits. Use No More Lies to cast a key Spirit from the graveyard to close the game or disrupt a combo.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board presence, interaction to handle sweepers, and the opponent is at low life or has no clear answer. Commit when you can pressure through potential removal or when the opponent is tapped out.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face a hard lock, have no interaction, the opponent has multiple sweepers, or you’re dead to the board (no Spirits in hand/graveyard, opponent has graveyard hate + sweepers). Also concede if you’re consistently losing the race to aggro without answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use No More Lies to counter their counters or protect your spells. Cast threats in response to their counterspells to trigger Rattlechains/Supreme Phantom. Use Cryptbreaker to bounce their counterspell if possible. Don’t overcommit to one threat; trade up.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them before they cast big spells. Use spot removal and counters to handle their acceleration. If they stabilize, use flying tokens and card advantage to out-value them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early interaction, then transition to a control/aggro hybrid. Use No More Lies to protect your board or disrupt their combo. Once stabilized, use Supreme Phantom to generate tokens and close the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early, then use card advantage and recursion (No More Lies) to out-value them. Force them to use multiple answers. Use flying tokens to bypass board wipes.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource race. Prioritize keeping interaction, track their graveyard, and use No More Lies to steal their key Spirits. Win the trigger war by casting Spirits in response to their triggers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Generally, bring in more interaction against control/midrange, more threats against aggro, and hate cards against specific strategies. Keep the deck's tempo intact and prioritize cards that synergize with Spirits or provide flexible interaction.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without interaction, mismanaging mana for instant-speed triggers, forgetting to use Cryptbreaker’s ability, and not tracking the opponent’s graveyard for No More Lies. Unique interactions: Rattlechains can shut down key blockers mid-combat. Supreme Phantom’s tokens are flying, making them hard to block. No More Lies can be used offensively to cast a Spirit from the graveyard for a surprise trigger.

philosophy: Boros Burn is a red-white aggressive archetype that aims to deal 20+ damage to the opponent as quickly as possible using a combination of cheap creatures, direct damage spells, and combat tricks. It wins by outpacing control and midrange decks before they can stabilize, while using precise damage sequencing to bypass life gain and removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a one-mana spell (Shock, Lightning Helix, Boros Charm, or a 1/1 like Raging Goblin/Firebrand Archer). Keep two lands plus one mana spell, or two lands plus two one-mana spells. Mulligan hands with three or more lands, no one-mana spells, or only expensive threats (Kari Zev, Grim Lavamander, Screaming Nemesis) without early damage. Prioritize hands that can play a turn-one or turn-two threat or spell.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Shock/Lightning Helix at the opponent or a 1/1 creature. If holding a 1/1, play land, cast 1/1, end. Turn 2: Play second land, cast another 1/1 or cheap burn, attack with everything. If drawing Screaming Nemesis, cast it on turn 2 or 3 to capitalize on instant/sorcery spells, then use Boros Charm to pump it into a lethal 3/1 or remove a blocker. Mana base: Prioritize dual lands (Steam Vents, Bloodstained Mire, Stomping Ground) early; fetches are crucial for consistency and fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs. Control/Combo (Niv to Light, Izzet Prowess): +2 Lightning Axe, +2 Dragon's Rage Channeler, +2 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Firebrand Archer, -1 Raging Goblin. vs. Midrange/Grindy (Golgari Midrange, Orzhov Greasefang): +2 Lightning Axe, +2 Dragon's Rage Channeler, +2 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Firebrand Archer, -1 Raging Goblin. vs. Aggro/Company (Selesnya Company): +2 Lightning Axe, +2 Dragon's Rage Channeler, +2 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Firebrand Archer, -1 Raging Goblin.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Burn is a race; conserve lands but never miss land drops. Use direct damage to close out games or remove key blockers. Track opponent's life total carefully. Don't overcommit to the board if you can win with spells. Prioritize keeping mana open for Boros Charm and Shock to maximize efficiency.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can safely attack or when you have removal/tricks to clear blockers. Boros Charm is your primary combat trick; use it to turn a 1/1 into a lethal 3/1 or to remove a problematic creature. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, life gain engines, or established board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for life gain (Orzhov), sweepers (Selesnya/Golgari), counterspells (Izzet/Niv), and graveyard recursion (Golgari). Key tells include the opponent tapping for multiple colors, holding up mana past turn 3, or playing enchantments/planeswalkers. Adjust your damage sequencing to bypass life gain or remove key threats before attacking.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kari Zev, Swiftblade Scavenger, Kuldotha Cackler, Grim Lavamander, and Screaming Nemesis are your primary win conditions. Chain direct damage spells with combat to reach 20. Boros Charm can pump a threat into lethal or remove a blocker to enable a kill.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have lethal damage in hand/creature pool, or when the opponent has no removal/sweepers. Use Boros Charm to pump a threat into lethal or remove a blocker. If the opponent is at 3 or less life, attack with everything and burn the rest.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1-2 cards with no lands, no damage spells, and the opponent has 10+ life with a board presence. Also concede against established control with a locked board or infinite life gain, or if you are consistently losing the race due to bad draws.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Boros Charm and Shock on turn 1 to pressure before counters are up. Save Lightning Axe/Dragon's Rage Channeler for later when you can value out counters. Attack with creatures to force counter decisions and protect your spells with cheap threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Outpace them. Play lands, cast burn, attack. Don't trade creatures; burn their ramp sources or the opponent directly. Use Boros Charm to pump your threats into lethal before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Boros Charm to pump your 1/1s into lethal. Use Shock/Lightning Helix to remove key threats. Don't overextend; race them. Track their life total and your own to ensure you win the race.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early, then pivot to value engines (Dragon's Rage Channeler, Lightning Axe) if they stabilize. Use Red Elemental Blast to counter their key counterspells. Attack with creatures to force them to spend removal on threats rather than your spells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It is a race. Prioritize lethal damage over board presence. Use Boros Charm to pump your threat into lethal or remove theirs. Track life totals carefully; often the first to deal 20 wins. Save removal for their threats and burn for the opponent.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, you are typically playing fewer raw burn spells and more value/tempo tools. Adjust your mulligan to keep hands with Lightning Axe/Dragon's Rage Channeler and dual lands. Remember that control matchups are slower, so you have more time to set up; do not rush into unfavorable combat. Prioritize keeping lands and one-mana spells to maintain early pressure.
notes: Boltwave appears to be a typo or custom card; assuming it refers to a high-damage burn spell like Lightning Strike or Shock. Always calculate exact damage to the opponent versus blockers. Boros Charm's 'deal 2 to target creature' can remove key creatures, but prioritize life gain removal or pump in aggressive matchups. Screaming Nemesis synergizes heavily with your instant/sorcery suite; cast it early to maximize its +1/+1 trigger. Mana base is critical; never play a land that does not produce the color you need for your next spell.

philosophy: A five-color combo-control deck that aims to win the game immediately by resolving Song of Creation, using Beseech the Mirror to recur it if answered. It leverages fast mana and card advantage to outpace midrange and control archetypes while stabilizing against aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with Mox Amber or Beseech the Mirror plus two lands. Keep Song of Creation only if paired with fast mana (Mox Amber, fetches, or shock lands) and a land. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only Song of Creation and no mana, or hands with Beseech but no way to get Song of Creation to the graveyard.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a fetch land, tap for a basic, fetch a dual/shock land, and play Mox Amber. Turn 2: Cast Song of Creation. If it resolves, you win. If it is countered or removed, use Beseech the Mirror to sacrifice a permanent and recur Song of Creation from the graveyard. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Song of Creation on time while retaining mana for protection or recursion.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Izzet Prowess, Selesnya Company): Bring in countermagic, spot removal, and card draw. Against control/midrange (Golgari Midrange, Orzhov Greasefang, Niv to Light): Bring in additional recursion, protection spells, and graveyard hate if needed. Against mirror: Bring in extra recursion and card advantage to out-grind the opponent.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Song of Creation and Beseech the Mirror in hand or graveyard. Use Mox Amber for tempo and mana acceleration, timing your spells to trigger its untap ability. Don't overextend into board wipes; keep recursion tools ready and manage your graveyard carefully if facing hate cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or recursion backup. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use spot removal to clear key threats (e.g., Greasefang, Company leaders) before casting Song of Creation to ensure a clean win condition.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (Path, Swords to Plowshares), and graveyard hate (Leyline, Rest in Peace). Note if opponents are holding up removal for Song of Creation versus early threats, and adjust your recursion timing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Song of Creation is the primary finisher. Beseech the Mirror enables recursion wins if you have multiple copies or ways to sacrifice permanents. Use Mox Amber to fuel the recursion loop and ensure you can cast Song of Creation even after it's been answered.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to resolving Song of Creation, have answered or bypassed the opponent's removal, or when you're ahead on board and can protect the win condition. Commit fully if the opponent has no interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Niv to Light with no interaction), have no way to protect Song of Creation, or are out-grinded by midrange with no recursion tools. Concede if your graveyard is empty and you lack the mana to cast Beseech the Mirror.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split second if available, or cast Song of Creation when the opponent has no mana up. Use Beseech the Mirror to recur it after it's countered. Employ cheap threats to force interaction, then win with Song of Creation on the following turn.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Mox Amber and cheap spells. Use spot removal to stop key ramp pieces. Don't let them get too far ahead; recur Song of Creation quickly and pressure their life total to close the game before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and countermagic. Use Mox Amber to fuel spells and maintain tempo. Once stabilized, cast Song of Creation to close the game before they can recover or find board wipes.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with card draw and recursion. Use Beseech the Mirror to bypass their removal and pressure their life total. Force them to use resources on early threats, then win with Song of Creation when they run out of answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the recursion war. Prioritize card advantage and protection. Use Beseech the Mirror to recur Song of Creation faster. Sideboard in extra recursion and hate for their recursion tools. Trade efficiently and force them to overcommit to removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo to a more resilient control-combo hybrid. Against aggressive decks, prioritize interaction and early threats. Against control/midrange, bring in additional recursion, protection, and card draw. Always keep at least one Beseech the Mirror and one Song of Creation in your main deck or sideboard to ensure the win condition remains accessible.
notes: Common mistake: Casting Song of Creation without a plan for recursion if it's answered. Always keep Beseech the Mirror in hand or ensure you have a way to get it to the graveyard. Watch out for cantrips that let opponents find removal. Mox Amber's untap trigger can be a massive tempo swing, so time your spells to maximize it. The deck is fragile to mass removal, so don't overcommit to the board before casting Song of Creation.

philosophy: Boros Humans is a fast, efficient aggro deck that leverages human synergy, combat tricks, and Thalia’s tax to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. It wins by out-racing aggro, out-valuing control with Warden’s card advantage, and exploiting non-human decks with Thalia’s tax effect.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably shock/duals), 1-2 one-drops (e.g., Kitesail Freebooter, Legion Warboss, or similar), and a Thalia or Warden. Mulligan if you have >3 lands, no one-drops, or a hand full of removal/tricks with no early threats. You need early pressure to set up Thalia’s tax and Warden’s scry engine.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then drop a one-drop. If you have a double-swap land, sequence it to ensure colored mana for tricks. Turn 2: Play a second land, drop Thalia or a second one-drop. Attack immediately with haste creatures to force unfavorable blocks or removal. Prioritize playing lands that enter untapped if possible to maintain mana flexibility for combat tricks.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Control: +1 Leyline of Punishment, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Lightning Strike/Go for the Throat, -1 Warden, -1 land. vs Midrange/Combo (Golgari/Orzhov): +1 Thalia (if not main), +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Lightning Strike, -1 Warden, -1 land. vs Izzet Prowess: +1 Leyline of Punishment, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Lightning Strike, -1 Warden, -1 land. vs Mirror: +1 Leyline of Punishment, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Lightning Strike, -1 Warden, -1 land.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for combat tricks and removal rather than overcommitting to attacks. Use Warden’s scry to dig for lands when mana-screwed or for specific threats when you’re behind. Track your graveyard if facing discard or graveyard hate, as Thalia and Warden are vulnerable to targeted removal.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no removal or sweepers, or when you can trade up using tricks. Hold back if they have spot removal or a sweeper in play; use Thalia to tax their blockers and force unfavorable combat math. Don’t overextend into known sweepers—preserve Warden for card advantage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), spot removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Strike), and combo win conditions. Note if the opponent is playing non-human decks (Thalia’s tax applies) or if they’re ramping out of control. Track their graveyard if they play discard or sacrifice engines.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Warden of the Inner Sky (card advantage + evasion), Legion Warboss (double strike + +1/+1 counter engine), and Thalia (tax + tax on blockers). Win by trading up with tricks, attacking with haste/vigilance creatures, and using Warden to dig through their layers until you reach lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, has already used a sweeper, or is at lethal range. Commit with tricks to clear blockers and attack with all haste creatures. If you have Warden and a clear path to value, commit to out-drawing them.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has a hard lock (e.g., graveyard hate + discard), you’re down to one card, and they have a sweeper or combo finisher you can’t race. Also concede in mirrors if they have Leyline of Punishment + Rest in Peace while you lack the answers to break it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Warden to dig for tricks or lands. Attack with multiple cheap threats to force counter decisions. Prioritize one-drops and haste creatures that are hard to counter effectively. Save removal for their counterspells if they’re playing a control shell.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thalia is your primary answer; tax their non-human ramp creatures. Use removal/tricks to kill their early threats before they stabilize. Attack early to race before they can cast their finishers. Don’t overcommit to trades—preserve Warden for value.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and combat tricks, then use Warden to out-value them. Don’t overextend; trade efficiently and use Thalia to tax their blockers. If they’re playing humans, focus on combat math and sideboard hate.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early to force mistakes and force them to use removal on low-value threats. Use Warden to dig through their layers and find answers. Save tricks for their removal and attack with haste to bypass sweepers. Don’t let them stabilize—keep the pressure on.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Combat math is king. Use tricks to clear blockers and trade up. Track opponent’s tricks and removal. Sideboard Leyline of Punishment and Rest in Peace are crucial; if you can cast Leyline, it often wins the game. Prioritize Warden for card advantage and Warboss for double strike.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggro to a more resilient midrange/aggro hybrid. Bring in hate cards (Leyline of Punishment, Rest in Peace, Lightning Strike) to handle specific matchups. Adjust land count based on matchup speed (reduce lands vs aggro, increase vs control). Prioritize keeping Thalia and Warden unless facing extreme graveyard hate or sweepers. In mirrors, Leyline is a priority inclusion.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to combat without accounting for tricks/removal, forgetting Thalia’s tax applies to opponent’s non-human spells, and not tracking the opponent’s graveyard. Warden’s scry is a tool, not a free card; use it strategically to dig for lands or threats. In mirrors, Leyline of Punishment can be a game-ender if you can cast it. Always track opponent’s removal and sweeper counts before committing to attacks.

philosophy: A fast, disruptive midrange-aggro hybrid that leverages cheap, synergistic humans and Reflector Mage to control the early game, then uses Collected Company to cheat in a high-impact two-creature swing that overwhelms cooperative or slow strategies. Wins by out-speeding aggro, out-resourcing midrange, and out-swinging control.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one turn-1 land, and a turn-1 play (Thoughtseize, Reflector Mage, or a 1-drop Human). Keep hands with a fetch/shock + land + disruption. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no plays, 1 land with 5+ spells, or hands missing a turn-1 land entirely.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Thoughtseize (discard removal/land) or Reflector Mage (if opponent plays a spell) or a 1-drop Human. Turn 2: Play second land, cast a 2-drop Human or Reflector Mage. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast disruption/creatures on curve. Use fetches/shocks to fix for WUBR or WUBRG. If you have Knight of the Reliquary, fetch a basic land for shock lands or utility.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Selesnya Company, Izzet Prowess): Bring in Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and additional Reflector Mages. Against control/Greasefang/Niv to Light: Bring in more disruption (Thoughtseize, Spell Pierce), Reflector Mages, and late-game threats. Against midrange (Golgari): Bring in graveyard hate, additional Reflector Mages, and a sweeper. Mirror: Bring in more Reflector Mages, Thoughtseize, and 3-drops.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Reflector Mages for key spells (counterspells, removal, or combo pieces). Don't tap out for a 1/1 if you can hold up mana for a Reflector Mage counter. Use Knight of the Reliquary to fetch basics for shock lands or utility lands. Manage hand size: Collected Company can refill your hand, so don't overcommit early. Thalia's Lieutenant draws a card when you cast a spell if you control a Human, so chain spells to generate card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have Reflector Mage protection or when the opponent has no removal. Hold back if they have sweepers or targeted removal. Use Thalia's Lieutenant to stabilize and draw cards. Don't overextend into known sweepers; wait for Collected Company to swing.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Murder), and combo pieces. Note when they tap out for a big spell or creature. Track graveyard hate vs. no graveyard hate. Watch for mana bases that indicate ramp or control.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Collected Company into two high-impact humans (e.g., Reflector Mage + Knight of the Reliquary, or Thalia's Lieutenant + a 3-drop). Once you have a board of 2-3+ humans, you can often swing for lethal or establish an unblockable/untouchable threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have a Reflector Mage on the board, or when you've established card advantage via Thalia's Lieutenant. Go all-in when the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal, or when you can swing past their blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1-2 lands with no creatures, no disruption, and the opponent has a board of 3+ creatures or a combo piece in play. If you're facing a hard lock (e.g., graveyard hate + no fetches, or a control deck with multiple counterspells and no way to break through).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Reflector Mage to counter their counterspells. Cast threats in response to their removal. Use Thoughtseize to discard their counterspells. Cast Collected Company in response to their removal or when they're tapped out.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use Reflector Mage to counter their mana rocks or ramp spells. Thoughtseize to discard their ramp. Collected Company to swing for lethal before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Reflector Mage to counter their key spells. Thoughtseize to discard their threats. Thalia's Lieutenant to stabilize and draw cards. Collected Company to swing for lethal or establish a board.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply early pressure to force them to use removal. Use Reflector Mage to counter their removal or sweepers. Thoughtseize to discard their answers. Collected Company to swing for lethal or establish a board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the disruption war. Thoughtseize to discard their Collected Company or key humans. Reflector Mage to counter their Reflector Mage or Collected Company. Thalia's Lieutenant to out-draw them. Collected Company into a better two-creature combo.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to a more resilient midrange/control hybrid. Increase disruption (Reflector Mage, Thoughtseize) against control and combo. Add graveyard hate against midrange and control. Adjust land count if needed (usually 24-25 lands). Keep Collected Company as the win condition but support it with more protection and card draw.
notes: Common mistakes include tapping out for Reflector Mage without a land to cast it, overcommitting to the board without protection, and misusing Knight of the Reliquary (fetch basics for shock lands or utility lands, not just any land). Remember that Thalia's Lieutenant draws a card when you cast a spell if you control a Human, so chain spells to generate card advantage. Collected Company looks at the top five cards, so don't rely on graveyard synergy for it. Reflector Mage is a 1/1 that dies to combat, so don't use it to block unless necessary.

philosophy: This deck is a midrange artifact engine that uses Simulacrum Synthesizer to fuel and protect Metalwork Colossus, aiming to out-value opponents with escalating threats and card advantage. It wins by stabilizing early against aggro, out-grinding midrange and control, and leveraging artifact synergies to close out games with unblockable, counter-stuffed Colossi.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Simulacrum Synthesizer, and at least one early artifact or cantrip. Drop hands with three or more lands and no early plays, or hands with only Metalwork Colossus and no way to cast it. Keep hands with Leyline Axe if paired with early mana and a target artifact.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap artifact or cantrip. Turn 2: Cast Simulacrum Synthesizer; use its enter-the-battlefield trigger to put a +1/+1 counter on Leyline Axe or another artifact, or sacrifice it later to fuel Colossus. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit your two-drop and six-drop without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Golgari Midrange and Selesnya Company: Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, early removal, and sweepers. Against Orzhov Greasefang and Niv to Light: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells, and card advantage. Against Izzet Prowess: Bring in early interaction, artifact hate, and board wipes. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and counterspells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting Colossus, holding up interaction, and fueling Simulacrum Synthesizer. Use Leyline Axe's sacrifice ability for card draw when you have board control, and use its pump ability only when it secures combat or trades favorably.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Metalwork Colossus when you have removal for their threats or a board wipe ready. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted hate in play. Use Simulacrum Synthesizer to trade efficiently or protect key artifacts.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Supreme Verdict), targeted removal (e.g., Path to Exile, Doom Blade), and combo finishers. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Metalwork Colossus with enough +1/+1 counters to be unblockable or too large to remove, or Leyline Axe pumped to lethal. Simulacrum Synthesizer can also close games by repeatedly fueling Colossus or trading into key threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and they have no sweepers in hand. Commit when you can trade your early artifacts for their board wipes or removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock, have no answers to their sweepers, or are down to a dead hand with no recovery. Concede if you're consistently losing combat to their optimized threats without a clear path to stabilize.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple small threats, sacrifice triggers, and artifact synergies to out-value counterspells. Cast Colossus in response to their counterspells if possible, or use Leyline Axe's draw ability to dig for answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use artifact hate, and don't let them stabilize. Trade efficiently and use sweepers to reset the board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early artifacts and removal, then transition to Colossus. Use Leyline Axe's pump to block or trade favorably.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use card advantage to out-grind, and protect Colossus from targeted removal. Use Leyline Axe's draw to dig past their answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing, using better sequencing, and targeting their key artifacts with removal. Use Simulacrum Synthesizer to fuel Colossus faster or trade efficiently.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize hate cards against the top metagame decks (Golgari, Selesnya, Orzhov, Niv, Izzet). Adjust land counts if needed, and ensure you have enough interaction for the specific format. Focus on artifact/enchantment hate, early removal, and sweepers.
notes: Watch out for artifact hate (Shatter, Disenchant). Simulacrum Synthesizer's ability is a key resource engine. Don't overcommit to Colossus without board presence or answers. Leyline Axe's pump is a tempo tool, not just a finisher.
